- Nested struct fields obey the same unsigned domain rules as top-level params.
- Optional or pointer unsigned fields obey unsigned constraints.
- Regression includes a top-level `uint64` param and a nested struct field of type `uint64`.

---

title: Go scan omits HTTP execution profile
description:
Type: bug
Priority: 1
Labels: go,frontend,http,scan

## Symptom
Normal scans discover net/http handler methods, but execution fails with `execution adapter not supported by Go frontend: go/http-handler`.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
The following targets fail:

```text
internal/provider/anthropic/handler.go::(*Handler).ServeHTTP
internal/provider/gemini/handler.go::(*Handler).ServeHTTP
internal/provider/openai/handler.go::(*Handler).ServeHTTP
```

Each reports:

```text
unsupported: execution adapter not supported by Go frontend: go/http-handler
```

Current code facts:
- `shatter-go/protocol/nethttp_recognizer.go` recognizes `func(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request)` and emits invocation model `{kind:"adapter", adapter_id:"go/http-handler"}` with synthetic params.
- `shatter-go/protocol/handler.go` registers `createHTTPHandlerFactory()` in `newHandler`.
- `shatter-go/protocol/handler.go` resolves runtime hooks from `req.ExecutionProfile`; if the execution profile does not include `go/http-handler`, `ChooseInvocationStrategy` returns unsupported even though the adapter is registered.

## Diagnosis
The adapter exists and is registered. The failing path is likely the Rust scan-to-execute bridge: scan analysis records the adapter invocation model, but the execute request sent to the Go frontend does not include an execution profile activating `go/http-handler`.

## In Scope
Ensure normal `shatter scan` carries the selected execution adapter profile from Go analysis into Go execute requests for adapter-owned targets.

## Out of Scope
Making complex handler receivers constructible. If receiver setup remains impossible, the failure should be a clean setup unsupported reason, not adapter-not-supported.

## Acceptance Criteria
- An eligible net/http handler target discovered during `shatter scan` receives an execute request with `execution_profile.adapters[0].id == "go/http-handler"`.
- The Go frontend resolves the registered HTTP hook and enters the adapter path instead of returning adapter-not-supported.
- Regression covers the scan path, not only direct Go protocol unit tests.
- If Zolem handler receivers remain unconstructable, the report shows a setup unsupported reason after adapter selection, not `execution adapter not supported`.

---

title: Go parameterized receiver constructors
description:
Type: feature
Priority: 1
Labels: go,frontend,executability,planner

## Symptom
Receiver methods that require construction remain unsupported when the only available constructor takes parameters, even when those parameters are simple enough for Shatter to generate or source from runtime values.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
- `internal/fixture/loader.go` defines `type Loader struct` and `func NewLoader(dir string) *Loader`.
- The scan reports `(*Loader).WithNamespace` and `(*Loader).WithRunner` as malformed unsupported outcomes whose underlying reason is that `Loader` requires constructor initialization and has no parameterless constructor or hint available.
- Current code fact: `shatter-go/planner/receiver.go` skips constructor candidates with parameters in `PlanReceivers`, with a comment saying the wrapper cannot synthesize constructor arguments and would otherwise emit an unknown receiver kind.

## Diagnosis
The receiver planner can use same-package constructors only when they are parameterless. That excludes common Go constructors like `NewLoader(dir string) *Loader`. A bounded implementation should support parameterized constructors whose arguments are already satisfiable by existing value plans, such as primitives and registered runtime values.

## In Scope
Support receiver construction through same-package constructors whose parameters can be satisfied by existing Shatter value planning.

## Out of Scope
Arbitrary dependency injection, constructors requiring application startup, network resources, or unsatisfied interface params.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A Go method receiver with `NewThing(name string) *Thing` can be planned and executed using a generated string argument.
- Constructor argument plans are represented in the invocation plan and consumed by wrapper generation.
- Constructors with unsatisfied params remain clean unsupported.
- Regression includes a receiver method that panics or fails as a zero value but succeeds when constructed by a parameterized constructor.

---

title: Go unsupported outcomes miss performance
description:
Type: bug
Priority: 1
Labels: go,frontend,protocol

## Symptom
In the updated Zolem scan, Go targets that should be reported as `unsupported` or `skipped_by_policy` are counted as hard failures because the frontend response omits required protocol timing data.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
- Scan totals: discovered 143, attempted 141, completed 95, failed 46, skipped 2.
- 43 of 46 failures have `failed to deserialize frontend response ... missing field performance`.
- Examples from `shatter-report/updated-scan.md`:
  - `(*localControlPlane).ListProfiles`
  - `(*jsonlRecorder).Record`
  - `(*Loader).Load`
  - `(*Matcher).Match`
- The malformed response hint begins with `{"protocol_version":"0.1.0","status":"execute","functions":null,"outcome":{"status":"unsupported"...}}`.
- At least two malformed outcomes are `skipped_by_policy`, e.g. `(*Loader).Load` and `GenerateContract`, so this is not limited to `unsupported`.

## Diagnosis
Go execute responses for non-completed outcomes do not consistently include `performance`. The core requires that field, so it rejects the response before it can classify the outcome.

## In Scope
Make every Go execute response variant satisfy the protocol schema expected by the core.

## Out of Scope
Making the unsupported targets executable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Go `unsupported` execute outcomes deserialize successfully.
- Go `skipped_by_policy` execute outcomes deserialize successfully.
- These outcomes are counted as unsupported/skipped, not failed.
- Regression coverage includes both unsupported and skipped Go execute responses.

---

comments.author: Ketan Gangatirkar
comments.text:
Reproduced again after the 2026-05-25 Shatter update. This issue should stay open.

Focused repro from `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  --parallelism 1 --parallelism-min 1 --parallelism-max 1 \
  --scheduler-policy serial \
  --isolation function \
  --core-sample 10 \
  -o shatter-report/scan-2026-05-25-isolated-sample.md \
  -o shatter-report/scan-2026-05-25-isolated-sample.json .
```

Result:

```text
Scan complete: 5 completed, 5 failed, 2 unsupported, 0 skipped (1 worker)
```

All 5 failures were unsupported execute outcomes that still omitted `performance`, for example:

```text
(*httpLocalServer).Addr: missing field `performance`
(*recordingResponseWriter).Write: missing field `performance`
(*Loader).WithNamespace: missing field `performance`
(*SSEWriter).WriteEvent: missing field `performance`
(*ContractLoader).Refresh: missing field `performance`
```

This is distinct from the new invocation-plan enum mismatch tracked separately in `str-ptjk`: this issue is still specifically about Go `execute` responses whose `outcome.status` is `unsupported` and whose response is rejected because required performance timing data is absent.

comments.author: Ketan Gangatirkar
comments.text:
Reproduced again on 2026-05-26 with the updated shatter binary. Focused command from ~/project/zolem:

shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds --parallelism 1 --parallelism-min 1 --parallelism-max 1 --scheduler-policy serial --isolation serial --core-sample 10 -o shatter-report/scan-2026-05-26-serial-isolation-sample.md -o shatter-report/scan-2026-05-26-serial-isolation-sample.json .

Result: exit 0 but only because fail-on-failures is not set; scan summary was 5 completed, 5 failed, 2 unsupported, 0 skipped. The five failures were all unsupported execute outcomes rejected by the core because the response was missing required `performance`.

Affected sampled functions:
- (*httpLocalServer).Addr
- (*jsonlRecorder).Record
- (*recordingResponseWriter).Flush
- (*LegacySelector).HasCEL
- (*SSEWriter).WriteEvent

Representative response hint: {"protocol_version":"0.1.0","id":6,"status":"execute","functions":null,"outcome":{"status":"unsupported","short_reason":"receiver type httpLocalServer requires constructor initialization..."}}

This closure was premature; the acceptance criteria 'Go unsupported execute outcomes deserialize successfully' and 'These outcomes are counted as unsupported/skipped, not failed' are still failing.

title: Rust timeout mismatches response IDs
description:
Title: Rust timeout mismatches response IDs
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: rust,frontend,timeout,protocol

## Problem

Rust exploration can exceed the configured time limit and then fail with a frontend protocol correlation error:

```text
response id 4 does not match request id 5
```

The user-visible failure should be a deterministic timeout or cancellation result. A protocol request/response ID mismatch after timeout suggests Shatter is draining or cancelling in-flight Rust frontend work incorrectly.

## Evidence

Observed with a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `949f5fe5f2d1` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

Repro command:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore api/src/suggestions.rs:line_status_for_quantities \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 90 \
  --request-timeout 60 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --color never
```

Observed output:

```text
[progress] failed 1/1: line_status_for_quantities (95.6s)
[batch 1/1] line_status_for_quantities: 0 iters, 0 paths, 0/3 branches, 155.7s (err)
[info] Stop flag reached: time limit (90.0s); draining in-flight batches
[info] Wrote explore artifact for line_status_for_quantities -> api/shatter-artifacts/explore-results/api_src_suggestions.rs/00226_line_status_for_quantities.json
[error] Exploration error for line_status_for_quantities: observe stage failed: frontend error: response id 4 does not match request id 5
```

Two symptoms are visible:

- The configured `--time-limit 90` was reached, but the command continued until the batch reported `155.7s`.
- The final error was a request/response ID mismatch rather than a clean timeout.

## Diagnosis

The Rust frontend or orchestrator appears to continue draining in-flight observe/execute requests after the stop flag is set. During that drain, a response is matched against the wrong request ID. This could happen if request IDs are advanced after a timed-out request, if stale responses remain in the frontend stream, or if cancellation does not flush the response queue before the next request is issued.

## Desired Behavior

When the time limit is reached, Shatter should:

- stop scheduling new Rust frontend requests,
- either await or cancel in-flight requests without corrupting request/response pairing,
- report the target as timed out or cancelled, and
- exit within a bounded grace period.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add a Rust frontend fixture where observe or execute exceeds a small configured time limit.
- Run `shatter explore` with that small time limit.
- Assert the final failure category is timeout/cancelled, not `response id X does not match request id Y`.
- Assert request IDs remain paired correctly during timeout drain.
- Assert wall-clock process duration stays within a bounded grace window after the stop flag is reached.

## In Scope

- Rust frontend request lifecycle and timeout/cancellation handling.
- Orchestrator draining behavior for timed-out Rust frontend requests.
- Regression tests for request ID pairing around timeouts.

## Out of Scope

- Making `line_status_for_quantities` complete successfully.
- Changing normal non-timeout request ID behavior for other frontends except where shared protocol code requires it.

title: Rust instrumentation rewrites source modules
description:
Title: Rust instrumentation rewrites source modules
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: rust,frontend,safety

## Problem

Running Shatter Rust exploration or mixed Rust+TypeScript scan can rewrite normal tracked Rust source modules in the target repository. This is unsafe for audit usage because a failed or interrupted run can leave user code changed even when the user only requested exploration.

Ignore manifest bridge edits and generated files for this issue. The bug here is that ordinary source modules such as `api/src/suggestions.rs` and `api/src/catalog.rs` are modified.

## Evidence

Observed with a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `949f5fe5f2d1` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

Direct Rust explore repro:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore api/src/suggestions.rs:line_status_for_quantities \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 90 \
  --request-timeout 60 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --color never
```

After the run, `git status --short` in `pickpackit` showed:

```text
 M api/src/suggestions.rs
```

The saved diff `/tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-rust-explore-source-mutation.diff` shows Shatter inserted a wrapper block directly into the target module:

```text
diff --git a/api/src/suggestions.rs b/api/src/suggestions.rs
...
+// __SHATTER_WRAPPER_BEGIN__
...
+// __SHATTER_WRAPPER_END__
```

The wrapper spans roughly diff lines 91 through 879.

Mixed scan repro:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed 20260521 \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --request-timeout 20 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --progress \
  --format json \
  -o /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-mixed-scan.json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

After the mixed scan, `git status --short` showed:

```text
 M api/src/catalog.rs
```

The saved diff `/tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-source-mutation.diff` shows `api/src/catalog.rs` was substantially rewritten: the file changed from 1340 original lines to 1046 lines, and a generated wrapper block was inserted directly into the source module.

## Desired Behavior

Rust instrumentation may stage generated code in an isolated harness workspace, but it must not edit ordinary tracked target source modules. A read-only exploration or scan should leave project source files byte-for-byte unchanged unless the user explicitly invokes a write-mode command.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add or reuse a self-contained Rust fixture crate in this repository with a normal source module and a target function.
- Run `shatter explore` against the fixture target.
- Assert the fixture source module remains byte-for-byte unchanged after the run.
- Assert generated wrapper code is staged only in an isolated harness location, not inserted into the target module.
- Add coverage for the scan path if scan and explore use different Rust instrumentation staging paths.

## In Scope

- Fix Rust frontend instrumentation staging so wrapper code is written to an isolated harness copy.
- Add regression coverage for source immutability.

## Out of Scope

- Whether manifest bridge edits are allowed.
- Whether generated harness files are allowed.
- Improving Rust type synthesis or runtime coverage.
close_reason: Implemented staging-copy approach: wrapper code now goes to isolated harness dir, original source modules never modified

title: Mixed scan breaks TS execution
description:
Title: Mixed scan breaks TS execution
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: scan,rust,typescript,frontend

## Problem

A mixed Rust + TypeScript scan can fail TypeScript functions with parser/instrumentation errors even though a TypeScript-only scan of the same sampled functions succeeds.

## Evidence

Observed while running a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `ed999dfd6638` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

Mixed scan command shape:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed 20260521 \
  --max-iterations 5 \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --request-timeout 20 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --progress \
  --format json \
  -o /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-scan.json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

Result:

- `attempted_functions`: 24
- `completed_functions`: 0
- `failed_functions`: 24
- 21 TypeScript/TSX functions failed with:

```text
error: exploration error: unexpected response from frontend: instrument error (ParseError): failed to parse file: cannot parse string into token stream
```

- 3 Rust functions timed out after 20 seconds.

Representative TypeScript failures from the mixed run:

- `web/src/api/client.ts::suppliesQueryKey`
- `web/src/components/Shell.tsx::TopBar`
- `web/src/domain/items.ts::filterItemsByOwnership`
- `web/src/features/keyboardShortcuts.ts::shouldOpenShortcutsHelp`
- `web/src/lib/auth/supabaseAdapter.ts::toAuthSession`

Those same sampled TypeScript functions completed under the TypeScript-only control scan below.

Control command shape:

```bash
/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --language typescript \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed 20260521 \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --timeout-total 120 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --request-timeout 20 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --format json \
  -o /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-ts-scan.json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

Result:

- `attempted_functions`: 24
- `completed_functions`: 24
- `failed_functions`: 0

## Desired Behavior

Mixed-language scans should isolate language frontends so Rust instrumentation/build failures cannot cause TypeScript files to fail with unrelated parser errors. TypeScript results should be at least as reliable in a mixed scan as they are in a TypeScript-only scan over the same file/function sample.

The likely implementation areas are scan orchestration, frontend process/cache isolation, and per-language instrumentation workspace ownership. The issue is not asking for a sampling change; the fixed test should pin the file set or seed so the mixed and TypeScript-only comparisons are deterministic.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add a self-contained mixed Rust + TypeScript fixture in the Shatter repo with at least one Rust target that fails or is skipped and at least one TypeScript target that should complete.
- Re-running the mixed fixture scan no longer produces TypeScript `cannot parse string into token stream` failures when the same TypeScript target succeeds under `--language typescript`.
- Failure reporting separates Rust frontend/harness failures from TypeScript parser or instrumentation failures.

## In Scope

- Frontend isolation and scan orchestration for mixed-language runs.
- Correct attribution of per-language failures.
- Deterministic fixture/sample setup for the regression.

## Out of Scope

- Fixing every Rust timeout in `pickpackit`.
- Increasing TypeScript coverage quality for React components.
- Changing core sample selection semantics.

title: Rust harness nested crate path
description:
Title: Rust harness nested crate path
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: rust,frontend,harness

## Problem

Rust exploration fails for a nested workspace member crate because the generated harness looks for the crate manifest at a duplicated nested path.

## Evidence

Observed while running a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `ed999dfd6638` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`, where the Rust API crate lives under `api/`.

Command shape:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore api/src/suggestions.rs:line_status_for_quantities \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 90 \
  --request-timeout 60 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --color never
```

The command failed with:

```text
execute error (CompilationError): error: failed to load manifest for dependency `pickpackit-api`

Caused by:
  failed to read `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit/api/.shatter/cache/harness/rust/crate-bridge/7700e68be18679fd/api/Cargo.toml`

Caused by:
  No such file or directory (os error 2)
```

The harness was already rooted under `pickpackit/api/.shatter/...`, but it still looked for `api/Cargo.toml` underneath that harness, producing a duplicated `api/.../api/Cargo.toml` expectation.

## Desired Behavior

Rust harness generation should resolve workspace root, package root, and relative dependency paths correctly for nested crates. For this project, the harness should be able to build `pickpackit-api` from the real or copied `api/Cargo.toml` without adding an extra `api/` path segment.

## Acceptance Checks

- The repro command no longer fails with a missing `api/Cargo.toml` inside `api/.shatter/cache/.../api/Cargo.toml`.
- Add or update a fixture test covering a workspace with a member crate under a subdirectory such as `api/`.
- The fix remains compatible with root-level Rust crates and nested workspace members.

## In Scope

- Correct crate root and manifest path resolution for Rust crate bridge harnesses.
- Add regression coverage for nested workspace member crates.

## Out of Scope

- Making opaque domain types synthesizable.
- Changing Rust function discovery policy.

title: Rust instrumentation mutates source
description:
Title: Rust instrumentation mutates source
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: rust,frontend,safety

## Problem

Running Shatter against a Rust crate can modify tracked user source files in the target repository during instrumentation. This makes Shatter unsafe to run as an audit tool because a failed or interrupted run can leave user code rewritten.

## Evidence

Observed while running a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `ed999dfd6638` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

This external project is evidence only. The implementation should create or reuse a self-contained Rust fixture in the Shatter repository so the regression test does not depend on `pickpackit` or on any `/tmp` artifact.

Command shape:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore api/src/suggestions.rs:line_status_for_quantities \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 90 \
  --request-timeout 60 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --color never
```

After the run, `git status --short` in `pickpackit` showed tracked modifications:

```text
 M api/Cargo.toml
 M api/src/catalog.rs
 M api/src/lib.rs
 M api/src/server.rs
?? api/.shatter/
?? api/src/__shatter.rs
```

The diff included:

- `api/Cargo.toml`: added optional `shatter-rust-runtime` dependency and `shatter-crate-bridge` feature.
- `api/src/lib.rs`: added `pub mod __shatter` behind `#[cfg(feature = "shatter-crate-bridge")]`.
- `api/src/server.rs`: inserted generated wrapper code and changed formatting of normal source.
- `api/src/catalog.rs`: large rewrite/reformat from instrumentation.
- `api/src/__shatter.rs`: generated bridge entry file.

Relevant observed diff excerpts:

```diff
api/Cargo.toml
+shatter-rust-runtime = { path = "/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust-runtime", optional = true }
+
+[features]
+shatter-crate-bridge = ["dep:shatter-rust-runtime"]

api/src/lib.rs
+#[cfg(feature = "shatter-crate-bridge")]
+pub mod __shatter;

api/src/server.rs
+// __SHATTER_WRAPPER_BEGIN__
+#[cfg(feature = "shatter-crate-bridge")]
+mod __shatter_inner { ... }
```

`api/src/catalog.rs` was also heavily rewritten/reformatted by instrumentation. `api/src/__shatter.rs` was created with generated crate bridge entry code.

## Desired Behavior

Rust instrumentation must operate in an isolated temp or harness copy. A Shatter run should not modify tracked files in the user's target repository unless the user explicitly requested a write-mode command.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add or reuse a self-contained Rust fixture crate in this repository that can reproduce Rust instrumentation without depending on `pickpackit`.
- Running `shatter explore` against that fixture leaves tracked fixture source files byte-for-byte unchanged.
- Running `shatter explore` against that fixture creates no unexpected untracked files inside fixture source directories, such as `src/__shatter.rs`.
- Rust crate bridge dependency/feature edits, wrapper modules, and generated bridge files are written only inside an isolated harness workspace.
- Add a regression test for the observed `explore` flow. If `scan` uses a different Rust instrumentation path, add a separate assertion or file a follow-up issue.

## In Scope

- Fix the Rust frontend instrumentation and crate bridge staging path used by `shatter explore` so it does not write into the target worktree.
- Clean up any generated Rust harness paths used by this flow.
- Add a regression test for source immutability.

## Out of Scope

- Improving Rust type synthesis coverage.
- Fixing runtime behavior of the explored function after instrumentation isolation is fixed.
- Broad scan orchestration changes unless the same instrumentation staging layer is shared by scan.

title: Explore external audit writes target repo
description:
Title: Explore external audit writes target repo
Type: bug
Priority: P1

## Problem

`shatter explore` can initialize and write Shatter state inside an audited target repository even when the user supplies external output and disables cache/seeds. This differs from `scan`, which already has an external-audit guard in `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` for `-o <external> --no-cache --no-seeds`.

## Evidence

Observed during the Kapow audit recorded in `audits/2026-05-21.md`.

Reproduction command from a Shatter checkout:

```bash
./target/release/shatter explore /home/ketan/project/kapow/api/internal/search/access.go:IsFieldSearchable \
  --project-dir /home/ketan/project/kapow/api \
  --max-iterations 1 --timeout-explore 30 --exec-timeout 10 --build-timeout 60 \
  --workers 1 --parallelism-min 1 --parallelism-max 1 \
  --no-cache --no-seeds \
  --output /tmp/is-field-searchable-explore-api.md
```

Observed side effects in the audited Kapow nested module:

```text
/home/ketan/project/kapow/api/.shatter/config.yaml
/home/ketan/project/kapow/api/shatter-artifacts/explore-results/...
```

Those generated files had to be manually removed after the audit run.

## Desired Behavior

When `explore` is run in an external-audit shape with external output plus `--no-cache --no-seeds`, it should not create `.shatter/`, `.shatter-cache/`, or `shatter-artifacts/` inside the target repository unless the user explicitly requests project-local artifacts.

## Acceptance Checks

- A regression test covers `explore` against a nested Go module with external `--output`, `--no-cache`, and `--no-seeds`.
- The test verifies the target module does not receive `.shatter/`, `.shatter-cache/`, or `shatter-artifacts/`.
- Existing normal `explore` artifact behavior remains available for users who do not opt into this external-audit shape.

## Scope

In scope: `shatter explore` implicit init and artifact path behavior.

Out of scope: Changing `scan` behavior, except for sharing an existing helper if useful.

title: Rust harness panic_impl duplicate
description:
Title: Rust harness panic_impl duplicate
Type: bug
Priority: P1

## Problem

Running Shatter against a Rust fixture in Kapow fails during harness compilation because the generated harness emits a panic handler while compiling a `std` binary. The compiler reports a duplicate `panic_impl` lang item.

## Evidence

Observed during the Kapow audit recorded in `audits/2026-05-21.md`.

Reproduction command from a Shatter checkout with a freshly built CLI and Rust frontend:

```bash
cargo build -p shatter-cli --release
cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml --release
PATH="$PWD/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH" ./target/release/shatter scan /home/ketan/project/kapow \
  --include 'docs/demo/zolem-fixtures/openai-top-50-technology-california/match.rs' \
  --require-rust --all --max-iterations 3 --timeout-per-fn 8 \
  --timeout-total 120 --parallelism 2 --parallelism-min 1 --parallelism-max 2 \
  --no-cache --no-seeds --format json \
  --output /tmp/rust-doc-scan.json --progress
```

Observed error for `match.rs::contains`:

```text
error[E0152]: found duplicate lang item `panic_impl`
note: the lang item is first defined in crate `std`
note: second definition in the local crate (`shatter_exec_temp`)
```

The same scan also reports `zolem_match` as incompatible with the current bin-only harness because it has raw-pointer / extern-style parameters.

## Desired Behavior

Rust harness generation should not emit `#[panic_handler]` for harnesses that link `std`. Raw-pointer or extern-style targets should be classified as unsupported before compilation unless a crate-bridge execution mode is implemented and selected.

## Acceptance Checks

- Running the reproduction scan no longer fails `contains` with duplicate `panic_impl`.
- A regression test covers a std Rust harness and verifies no panic handler is emitted.
- Raw-pointer / extern-style targets either execute through a documented crate-bridge path or are skipped as unsupported before harness compilation.

## Scope

In scope: Rust harness generation and Rust frontend unsupported-target classification.

Out of scope: Implementing broad Rust crate-bridge support unless it is already the intended minimal fix.

title: Go context scan timeout
description:
Title: Go context scan timeout
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: go,frontend,scan

## Problem

`shatter scan` timed out on a tiny Go function that reads from `context.Context`, but `shatter observe` on the same function completed quickly. Scan-mode and observe-mode should not disagree this sharply on basic execution.

The affected function:

```go
func ListenerRuntimeFromContext(ctx context.Context) (ListenerRuntime, bool) {
    v := ctx.Value(listenerRuntimeKey{})
    if v == nil {
        return ListenerRuntime{}, false
    }
    rt, ok := v.(ListenerRuntime)
    return rt, ok
}
```

## Reproduction

From a checkout of `~/project/shatter`, first update the local binary:

```bash
cargo install --path shatter-cli --force
```

Then run the scan from `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
cd ~/project/zolem
shatter scan internal/runtime \
  --language go \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 15 \
  --max-iterations 8 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --scheduler-policy serial \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --capture-side-effects \
  --fail-on-failures=0 \
  -o /tmp/zolem-runtime-scan.json \
  --format text \
  --stdout \
  --color never \
  --render plain \
  --progress
```

Observed scan result:

```text
Scan complete: 25 completed, 1 failed, 0 unsupported, 0 skipped

Errors (1):
  /home/ketan/project/zolem/internal/runtime/context.go::ListenerRuntimeFromContext: timed out after 15s
```

Then run observe on the same function:

```bash
shatter observe internal/runtime/context.go:ListenerRuntimeFromContext \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --timeout 45 \
  --exec-timeout 5 \
  --request-timeout 10 \
  --build-timeout 30 \
  -o /tmp/zolem-listener-runtime-observe.json \
  --color never \
  --render plain
```

Observed observe result: command completed successfully and wrote `/tmp/zolem-listener-runtime-observe.json`.

## Expected Behavior

Scan and observe should use compatible execution semantics for the same Go function. If scan cannot execute the function because of context construction or scan scheduling, it should report that reason deterministically instead of timing out.

## Acceptance Checks

- A regression test covers a Go function taking `context.Context` and calling `ctx.Value`.
- Scan mode no longer times out on `ListenerRuntimeFromContext` in the reproduction above.
- If the function is unsupported in scan mode, the report uses an unsupported/input-construction reason rather than `timed out after 15s`.
- Any intended scan-vs-observe semantic difference is documented in CLI help or an internal comment near the execution path.

## Scope

In scope: Go scan execution, context input construction or stubbing, scan/observe execution parity, timeout classification.

Out of scope: broad context-aware symbolic modeling beyond this basic `ctx.Value` case.
notes:
Investigation 2026-05-22 (agent-v5qe): root cause is NOT context.Context specific. The Go frontend already handles context.Context via shatter-go/runtimeval/registry.go — applyRuntimeValueBindings (shatter-go/wrapper/wrapper.go:506) bakes context.Background() at the param-init site. Reran the exact repro against ~/project/zolem/internal/runtime: ListenerRuntimeFromContext completed in ~9.2s with 8 iterations and 1 unique path.

Actual root cause: per-fn timeout (--timeout-per-fn) includes Go launcher build/prepare wall time. tokio::time::timeout at shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs:2381 wraps the entire explore_single_function including Prepare/build. --build-timeout (30s default, SHATTER_BUILD_TIMEOUT in the frontend) only governs the in-frontend go-build phase; it does not bracket build time out of the per-fn budget. On a cold Go build cache the first scanned function pays the launcher build cost (observed ~9s for a tiny fn in zolem, ~1.5s with warm cache via observe) and can blow past --timeout-per-fn 15s, surfacing as the misleading 'timed out after 15s'.

Why ListenerRuntimeFromContext appeared in the original report: it was the first function in topological order so it carried the cold build cost. Same shape as str-6sie's tiny methods (Error/Set/String) — also build-bound, not method-construction-bound.

Closely related to str-ubp1 (scan timeout phase reporting). Deferring to wave 2 per team-lead. No code changes made; worktree torn down.

title: Go tiny methods timeout
description:
Title: Go tiny methods timeout
Type: bug
Priority: P1
Labels: go,frontend,scan

## Problem

Running Shatter's Go scan on a real Go command package timed out on three tiny exported methods that should complete quickly:

- `(*apiError).Error`
- `(*repeatedStrings).Set`
- `(*repeatedStrings).String`

These methods have no network or filesystem work and only call simple string formatting/join/append behavior. A timeout here makes Go scan results unreliable because users cannot distinguish real project complexity from harness or frontend failure.

## Reproduction

From a checkout of `~/project/shatter`, first update the local binary:

```bash
cargo install --path shatter-cli --force
```

Then run against `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
cd ~/project/zolem
shatter scan cmd/zolemc \
  --language go \
  --timeout-total 120 \
  --timeout-per-fn 10 \
  --max-iterations 5 \
  --parallelism 2 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --fail-on-failures=0 \
  -o /tmp/zolem-zolemc-scan.json \
  --format text \
  --stdout \
  --color never \
  --render plain \
  --progress
```

Observed result:

```text
Scan complete: 0 completed, 3 failed, 0 unsupported, 0 skipped

Errors (3):
  /home/ketan/project/zolem/cmd/zolemc/main.go::(*apiError).Error: timed out after 10s
  /home/ketan/project/zolem/cmd/zolemc/main.go::(*repeatedStrings).Set: timed out after 10s
  /home/ketan/project/zolem/cmd/zolemc/main.go::(*repeatedStrings).String: timed out after 10s
```

Relevant zolem source shape:

```go
func (e *apiError) Error() string {
    body := strings.TrimSpace(e.Body)
    if body == "" {
        return fmt.Sprintf("%s %s: %s", e.Method, e.URL, e.Status)
    }
    return fmt.Sprintf("%s %s: %s: %s", e.Method, e.URL, e.Status, body)
}

type repeatedStrings []string

func (v *repeatedStrings) String() string {
    return strings.Join(*v, ", ")
}

func (v *repeatedStrings) Set(s string) error {
    *v = append(*v, s)
    return nil
}
```

## Expected Behavior

Shatter should execute these tiny methods successfully or report a precise unsupported-receiver/input-construction reason. They should not time out under a 10 second per-function timeout.

## Acceptance Checks

- A regression fixture or test covers pointer receiver methods on simple structs and slice aliases in the Go frontend.
- The reproduction above completes at least one behavior for each of the three methods, or reports a deterministic unsupported reason without waiting for the timeout.
- The scan report preserves actual timeout failures separately from any low-coverage warnings.

## Scope

In scope: Go frontend/harness execution, receiver construction, timeout classification, and a focused regression test.

Out of scope: improving coverage strategy for these methods beyond avoiding the timeout.

title: Run invalid zero-complete exits success
description:
## Description

`shatter run` can exit successfully even when the report is explicitly invalid and zero attempted functions completed.

Observed in a temp copy of `pickpackit`:

```bash
shatter --color never --render md run \
  --output-dir /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/report \
  --timing summary --timing-format both \
  --timing-output /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/timing.json \
  --timeout 300 --request-timeout 120 \
  /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-work
```

The command exited `0` after timing out at 300 seconds, but the report said:

```text
Report Validity: stale-source-set
represented_source_percent=0.0 below low threshold 25.0
completed=0 of attempted=183; rep%=0.0
7 source path(s) changed content during run
```

`run.json` summary:

```json
{
  "completed_functions": 0,
  "failed_functions": 183,
  "represented_source_percent": 0.0,
  "report_validity": "stale-source-set"
}
```

This is dangerous in automation: a CI or agent workflow can treat a wholly invalid broad-run result as success unless the caller already knows to pass stricter flags.

## Acceptance

- `shatter run` exits nonzero when report validity is `stale-source-set` or when attempted functions are nonzero and completed functions are zero.
- If backwards compatibility requires opt-in failure behavior, the command prints a prominent warning naming the required flag and docs clarify the default.
- Add a CLI regression test for a synthetic zero-complete invalid run.

## In Scope

- CLI exit-code policy for invalid broad-run results.
- Documentation/help text if behavior remains opt-in.

## Out of Scope

- Fixing the frontend failures that caused this specific invalid report.
close_reason: str-jeen.84: Exit nonzero on invalid/zero-complete runs — landed on main

title: Rust crate bridge loses module context
description:
## Description

Rust crate-bridge wrapper generation can emit wrappers in a context that cannot see the target function's imports, private helpers, or argument types. In the `pickpackit` run, after generating `api/src/__shatter.rs`, compile errors included:

```text
error[E0425]: cannot find type `Config` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find type `PgPool` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find type `Request` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find type `Body` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find function `bearer_token` in module `super`
error[E0425]: cannot find function `unauthorized` in module `super`
```

The affected wrappers included functions from:

- `api/src/app.rs`: `build_router`, `cors_layer`
- `api/src/auth/middleware.rs`: `bearer_token`, `unauthorized`

Some functions are private to a nested module and should either be wrapped inside their defining module context or classified as non-executable/unsupported during discovery. Public functions that depend on module-local imports need wrapper code that resolves fully qualified paths or emits the required imports.

## Acceptance

- Crate-bridge wrappers either compile with the target function's module context or mark the target unsupported before attempting execution.
- Private functions in nested modules are not emitted into a root-level wrapper that cannot access them.
- Add a fixture crate with a nested module, module-local imports, one public function, and one private helper; assert the public wrapper compiles and the private helper is either executable through a valid in-module wrapper or classified unsupported.
- The `pickpackit` examples above no longer fail with missing type/function wrapper errors.

## In Scope

- Rust crate-bridge wrapper placement/import qualification.
- Discovery/execution agreement for private Rust targets.

## Out of Scope

- Axum-specific extractor support.
- Broader Rust input generation quality.
close_reason: str-31j.3: in-module wrapper (mod __shatter_inner + use super::*) fixes E0425 module-context errors — landed on main

title: Rust if-let instrumentation breaks compile
description:
## Description

The Rust frontend can generate invalid Rust when instrumenting `if let` expressions. In a temp copy of `pickpackit`, a longer Shatter run produced compile failures like:

```text
error: expected expression, found `let` statement
 --> /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-work/api/src/assistant.rs:1:11059
  |
1 | ... if { let __shatter_cond = { let __shatter_cond = let Some (message) = body . message ; ...
  |                                                                     ^^^
  = note: only supported directly in conditions of `if` and `while` expressions

error[E0425]: cannot find value `message` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find value `text` in this scope
error[E0425]: cannot find value `account` in this scope
```

Concrete source patterns involved:

- `api/src/assistant.rs`: `if let Some(message) = body.message { ... }`
- `api/src/assistant.rs`: `if let Some(text) = value.get("output_text").and_then(Value::as_str) { ... }`
- `api/src/auth/service.rs`: `if let Some(account) = ... { ... }`
- `api/src/auth/validator.rs`: `if let Some(key) = ... { ... }`

The instrumentor appears to wrap condition syntax in a block that is valid for boolean expressions but invalid for Rust `let` pattern conditions, and it also loses bindings introduced by the pattern.

## Acceptance

- Instrumented Rust preserves valid semantics for `if let` and similar let-condition patterns.
- Pattern-bound variables remain in scope inside the branch body after instrumentation.
- Add Rust frontend tests with `if let Some(x) = ...`, `if let Ok(x) = ...`, and an async function using `if let Some(x) = future.await?`.
- The `pickpackit` functions listed above no longer fail with `expected expression, found let statement`.

## In Scope

- Rust AST instrumentation for let-condition expressions.
- Regression tests in `shatter-rust`.

## Out of Scope

- General Rust pattern-matching completeness beyond preserving valid `if let`/let-condition compilation.
close_reason: str-31j.2: detect Expr::Let before wrapping; insert branch_hit in branch bodies where bindings are live — landed on main

title: Rust run leaves target source dirty
description:
## Description

Running Shatter against another repository can leave Rust instrumentation edits in the target checkout when the run fails or is interrupted.

Observed on 2026-05-16 while running Shatter from `/home/ketan/project/shatter` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`:

```bash
env PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/debug:$PATH \
  SHATTER_CACHE_DIR=/tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/cache \
  SHATTER_SEEDS_DIR=/tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/seeds \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter --color never --render md \
  run --output-dir /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/report \
  --timing summary --timing-format both \
  --timing-output /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-run/timing.json \
  /home/ketan/project/pickpackit
```

The run aborted on:

```text
Error: analyze failed for /home/ketan/project/pickpackit/api/src/assistant.rs::build_assistant_provider: request timed out after 30s
[shatter-rust] Fatal: Broken pipe (os error 32)
```

Afterward, the target repo had unexpected source changes:

```text
 M api/Cargo.toml
 M api/src/app.rs
 M api/src/lib.rs
?? api/src/__shatter.rs
```

The generated changes included adding `shatter-rust-runtime`, adding a `shatter-crate-bridge` feature, adding `pub mod __shatter`, creating `api/src/__shatter.rs`, and replacing most of `api/src/app.rs` with instrumented output.

## Acceptance

- A failed or timed-out Rust scan/run leaves the target checkout exactly as it was before the command.
- Rust frontend crate-bridge/instrumentation writes to a temporary workspace or always restores touched files on every error path.
- Add a regression test that runs a Rust frontend failure path against a fixture crate and asserts `git status --short` or a file-content snapshot is unchanged.

## In Scope

- Rust frontend and/or core orchestration cleanup for generated crate-bridge files.
- Failure paths including timeout, frontend crash, compile error, and broken pipe.

## Out of Scope

- Improving Rust exploration coverage itself.
- Changing TypeScript or Go frontend artifact behavior unless the same source-mutation bug is found there.

title: Coverage lines overcount
description:
## Problem
The scan report can show `lines_covered > total_lines`, which makes function coverage percentages and PASS/WARN/FAIL labels untrustworthy.

Evidence from the Zolem `shatter-report/full-scan.json` after a scan:

```text
NewFakerGenerator       2/1 lines
Generate                20/12 lines
IncrementProfileRequest 13/10 lines
UpsertProfile           36/11 lines
ValidateLoopbackAddr    9/3 lines
LoadProviderSchema      22/17 lines
```

The report clamps coverage to 100%, but the underlying counts are impossible. This is a report correctness bug independent of the target project.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? yes
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: scan/explore report JSON fields `lines_covered`, `total_lines`, and derived `coverage_pct`
- Is this change backward-compatible? yes, field names stay the same but values become corrected

## Parity Impact
- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) - verify line accounting in default reports
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) - verify concolic reports use the same accounting or are separately tested
- [x] CLI wiring in `main.rs` - verify scan and explore output paths use corrected metrics

## Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

## Required Test Updates
- [x] Add or update report metric tests that assert `lines_covered <= total_lines` for every function
- [x] Add a fixture covering multi-line statements or instrumentation expansion if that caused the overcount
- [x] Verify JSON and markdown reports agree after correction
- [x] Conformance tests verified (`task conformance`) if report JSON is contract-covered

## Acceptance Checks
- No function report can emit `lines_covered > total_lines`.
- Coverage percentages are derived from corrected counts and do not hide impossible data by clamping alone.
- The regression test fails on the old Zolem-style overcount.

## In Scope
- Line accounting, deduplication, and report metric calculation.
- Tests for JSON and markdown report consistency.

## Out of Scope
- Changing the public names of report JSON fields.
close_reason: str-uabz: reconcile_line_coverage ensures lines_covered <= total_lines in all report paths — landed on main

title: Go receiver setup missing
description:
## Problem
Shatter invokes Go methods on zero-value receivers even when the receiver type is only valid after construction. This creates nil-pointer panic clusters and low coverage that do not reflect target behavior.

Evidence from the Zolem scan:

- `cmd/zolem/local_admin.go::(*localControlPlane).ListProfiles` reported only a nil-pointer panic, but real instances are created by `newLocalControlPlane`, which initializes `store`, `counters`, and `listeners`.
- `cmd/zolem/local_admin.go::(*localControlPlane).UpsertListener` reported `2/76` lines and only nil-pointer behavior for the zero-value receiver.
- `cmd/zolem/local_admin.go::(*httpLocalServer).Addr` and `Close` similarly ran against nil internals.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

## Parity Impact
- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) - receiver construction or unsupported classification must apply in default exploration
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) - verify method receiver setup is consistent if concolic Go execution uses the same frontend path
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` - N/A

## Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

## Required Test Updates
- [x] Add a Go fixture with a method whose zero-value receiver panics but a constructor-created receiver is valid
- [x] Verify Shatter can use configured receiver setup or classifies the target unsupported instead of reporting zero-value panic as behavior
- [x] Verify report text makes the receiver setup decision visible

## Acceptance Checks
- Methods requiring initialized receiver state are not silently explored on invalid zero-value receivers.
- A regression fixture demonstrates either configured receiver construction or an unsupported reason for unconstructable receivers.
- Zolem-like `localControlPlane` methods would no longer be reported as meaningful nil-pointer-only behavior without setup.

## In Scope
- Go method receiver construction policy, including config/generator support or conservative unsupported classification.
- Tests and report wording for receiver setup failures.

## Out of Scope
- Full object-state search across arbitrary constructor argument combinations.
close_reason: str-g7h7: ReceiverRequiresConstruction heuristic classifies methods with unexported reference-typed fields as unsupported instead of zero-value — landed on main

title: Go byte slices invalid
description:
## Problem
Shatter generates invalid JSON values for Go `[]byte` and `[]uint8` parameters, causing many discovered inputs to fail before target code runs.

Evidence from the Zolem scan includes repeated errors:

```text
param wasmBytes: json: cannot unmarshal number -1 into Go value of type uint8
param data: json: cannot unmarshal number 9223372036854775807 into Go value of type uint8
param body: json: cannot unmarshal number -899 into Go value of type uint8
```

Affected examples included `internal/wasmgen/generator.go::Compile`, `internal/specs/openapi.go::NormalizeOpenAPI`, and `internal/specs/validator.go::Validate`. These are input-generation failures, not observed target behavior.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

## Parity Impact
- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) - byte slice candidate generation must produce representable Go JSON values
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) - verify concolic candidate generation, if any, uses the same representable domain
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` - N/A

## Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

## Required Test Updates
- [x] Add or update Go frontend tests for `[]byte` parameters
- [x] Include boundary values `0`, `1`, and `255`
- [x] Ensure generated JSON never contains negative values or values above 255 for byte elements

## Acceptance Checks
- Go `[]byte` / `[]uint8` inputs generated by Shatter unmarshal successfully before target invocation.
- A regression fixture with a `[]byte` parameter receives valid byte arrays and reaches function code.
- Existing invalid-input errors from out-of-range byte values are not counted as behavior clusters.

## In Scope
- Candidate generation and/or marshaling for Go byte slices.
- Targeted tests for valid byte element ranges.

## Out of Scope
- Semantic generation of valid file formats such as complete WASM or OpenAPI documents.
close_reason: str-ieuc: GoByte wire format is now raw JSON integer in [0,255]; Go analyzer maps byte/uint8 to GoByte — landed on main

title: Go variadic wrappers fail
description:
## Problem
A Zolem repository scan failed every attempted function in `internal/ollama` because Shatter generated uncompilable Go wrapper code for a variadic function.

Observed evidence from `shatter scan` in `/home/ketan/project/zolem`:

```text
/home/ketan/.config/shatter/go-workspace/generated/.../wrapper/shatter_wrapper_00e95aacea964a33.go:245:45: cannot use args (variable of type []string) as string value in argument to runCommand
```

The source function is `func runCommand(ctx context.Context, binaryPath string, args ...string) ([]byte, error)`. The generated wrapper declares `var args []string` but calls `runCommand(ctx, binaryPath, args)` instead of `runCommand(ctx, binaryPath, args...)`. Because the package wrapper does not compile, unrelated targets in the same package such as `Detect`, `Generate`, `SelectedModel`, `HTTPChatCompletion`, and `HTTPChatCompletionStream` all fail before exploration.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

## Parity Impact
- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) - N/A
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) - verify generated Go wrappers compile in the default scan/explore path
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) - verify no separate concolic wrapper path needs the same fix
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` - N/A

## Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

## Required Test Updates
- [x] Add or update a Go frontend fixture with a variadic function that accepts `...string`
- [x] Verify exploring that fixture compiles and executes
- [x] Verify a package with a variadic helper does not poison unrelated targets in the same package

## Acceptance Checks
- `shatter explore` or the relevant Go frontend test can analyze a Go variadic function and emits `args...` at the call site.
- A package containing `func runCommand(context.Context, string, ...string)` no longer fails wrapper compilation.
- The fix is covered by a regression test that would fail with `cannot use args ... as string value` on the old code.

## In Scope
- Fix Go wrapper invocation generation for variadic parameters.
- Add targeted regression coverage.

## Out of Scope
- Broader support for function-typed parameters or HTTP handler adapters.
close_reason: str-adff: variadic fix already in main (str-jeen.48); added regression test for package-poisoning invariant — landed on main

title: Mixed repo install docs
description: Document the mixed-project distribution rule: install Shatter once through the existing package manager or workflow that already owns repo-level tooling, and have all language-specific jobs call that one binary. Do not add Shatter to every language manifest in polyglot repos unless subprojects are intentionally versioned independently.
acceptance_criteria: Docs include owner-selection rules for root package.json, Go-only repos, GitHub Actions-only adoption, and Rust-only repos; examples cover Shatter/Kapow-style multi-language repos; docs state Cargo-native distribution is deferred and Rust-only users should use the setup action in CI or pinned shell installer locally for now.

title: Setup action uses binaries
description: Replace the current Docker-first composite action behavior with a setup action that downloads the requested GitHub prerelease payload, verifies it, and adds shatter to PATH. This gives CI-only and language-agnostic projects a dependency surface that existing GitHub Actions update workflows can manage.
acceptance_criteria: A workflow can use shatterproof-ai/shatter at a continuous tag and then run shatter --version; action inputs support exact build selection; the action no longer resolves images against the consuming repository; docs present the action as the fallback for projects without a package-manager owner.

title: Go tool wrapper
description: Add a Go 1.24+ tool wrapper so Go-only projects can track Shatter through go.mod without using a public package registry and without building Shatter from source. The wrapper should download and verify the matching GitHub prerelease binary payload before execing shatter.
acceptance_criteria: A temp Go module can run go get -tool for the wrapper at a continuous tag and then go tool shatter --version; the wrapper downloads the matching prerelease manifest, verifies checksums, caches the payload, and forwards arguments to the real shatter binary; docs recommend this path for Go repos without root Node tooling.

title: npm tarball wrapper
description: Publish GitHub Release npm tarball artifacts for Shatter without using the npm registry. The wrapper package should expose a shatter bin, select the current platform payload, and install from GitHub prerelease assets so TypeScript and mixed Node-tooling repos can use normal package.json and lockfile flows.
acceptance_criteria: A temp npm project can depend on the GitHub-hosted @shatterproof/shatter tarball URL, install it with npm, and run npm exec shatter -- --version; platform package selection works for supported Linux and macOS targets; docs recommend this as the default owner when a root package.json already exists.

title: Package-manager distribution
description: Make Shatter consumable through package managers and update workflows projects already use, without publishing to public registries for now. GitHub prereleases remain the shared binary payload source. Mixed-language repos should install Shatter once through one existing repo-level tool owner, not once per language.
acceptance_criteria: Projects can consume Shatter through GitHub-hosted npm tarball dependencies, a Go tool wrapper, and a setup GitHub Action; docs explain owner selection for mixed-language repos; Cargo-native distribution is explicitly deferred.

title: TS expression arrows uninstrumented
description:
Observed in the Pickpackit make shatter scan artifact /home/ketan/project/pickpackit/web/shatter-artifacts/scan-results/d1af50c637243db1b489346634e9be72a5faa5d7f4328928da057d0b3c301e5a while analyzing results on 2026-05-14.

Four exported expression-bodied arrow functions in web/src/api/client.ts, tagsQueryKey, tagCategoriesQueryKey, suppliesQueryKey, and tripsQueryKey, execute successfully and produce behavioral return clusters, but the markdown summary marks them FAIL with 0.0%, 0/0 branches, and 0/0 lines.

Direct TS frontend inspection shows analyzeFile reports real source spans for these functions, for example tagsQueryKey start_line 32 and end_line 32, while instrumentFunction(source, "tagsQueryKey", file, []) returns instrumentableLineCount: 0 and branchCount: 0.

Root cause: analyzeArrowFunction accepts expression-bodied arrows, but createInstrumentationTransformer and extractFunctionBody only handle arrow functions whose body is a block. The expression body executes without __shatter_record probes. Core then trusts the frontend's explicit instrumentable_line_count = 0 instead of falling back to the analyzed span, computes coverage_pct = 0.0, and the markdown table classifies the successful function as FAIL.
acceptance_criteria: A TypeScript fixture like export const key = (workspaceId: string) => ["tags", workspaceId] as const explores with total_lines >= 1 and lines_covered >= 1. Expression-bodied arrow functions receive line instrumentation or an equivalent truthful instrumentable-line count. Successful zero-branch expression arrows no longer appear as false FAIL rows solely because the frontend reported 0/0 lines. Regression coverage exists in shatter-ts and the report/status path.

title: Go variadic forwarding
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. 8 residual Go failed files under api/internal/fit/dimensions fail wrapper builds with cannot use choices (variable of type []string) as string value in argument to chipsHint. The source helper is variadic: chipsHint(choices ...string), and generated wrapper code forwards a []string without spread. Examples include cost_fit.go, climate_temperature_match.go, climate_precipitation_match.go, geographic_proximity.go, helpers.go, major_offered.go, outcomes_strength.go, and size_match.go.
acceptance_criteria: Generated Go calls forward slices to variadic parameters with ... where appropriate. Regression coverage includes a target that calls a variadic helper through an intermediate []string value and asserts wrapper compilation succeeds.

title: Go nested module replace
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. 10 residual Go failed files under tools/nleval/eval fail with reading github.com/ketang/kapow/api/go.mod at revision api/v0.0.0: unknown revision api/v0.0.0. The nested module tools/nleval/go.mod has require github.com/ketang/kapow/api v0.0.0 and replace github.com/ketang/kapow/api => ../../api, but the generated harness does not preserve that local replace.
acceptance_criteria: Go frontend honors local replace directives from the target module when preparing generated harnesses, including nested modules. Add a regression fixture with a nested module requiring and replacing a sibling local module; exploration succeeds without network revision lookup.

title: Go helper name collisions
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. 13 residual Go failed files in api/internal/search fail wrapper builds with boolPtr redeclared in this block. Examples include api/internal/search/access.go, blend.go, contract.go, field.go, normalize.go, registry.go, source.go, and sql.go. The generated harness helper collides with an existing package helper.
acceptance_criteria: Generated Go helper names are collision-checked against package scope or are namespaced to avoid user identifiers. Add a regression fixture with a package-level boolPtr helper and a target requiring generated helper support; wrapper compilation must succeed.

title: Go wrapper missing imports
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. 20 residual Go failed files build generated wrappers that reference strings without importing it. Examples include api/internal/nl/anthropic.go, api/internal/nl/client.go, api/internal/nl/contract_handler.go, api/internal/nl/convert.go, api/internal/nl/gemini.go, and tools/kapow/internal/fetch/fetch.go. Related prior work: str-jeen.33 and str-b7zh, but this current run still has concrete undefined: strings wrapper failures.
acceptance_criteria: Go wrapper import synthesis includes every stdlib package referenced by generated wrapper code. Regression coverage explores a Go target whose generated wrapper needs strings and asserts the wrapper compiles. A Kapow-style rerun no longer reports undefined: strings in generated shatter_wrapper files.

title: TS private target regression
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. 19 residual failed TypeScript targets report Function X not found in instrumented module exports even though Shatter discovered the target. Examples include CitationRow in web/src/components/search/CitationsFlyout.tsx, FooterLinks in web/src/components/layout/Footer.tsx, FilterDrawerSection in web/src/components/search/filterBar/FilterDrawer.tsx, and helpers in web/e2e specs. This is a current recurrence of the failure class closed under str-jeen.9.
acceptance_criteria: Discovery and execution agree on TypeScript target identity. Discovered private top-level helpers are executable through instrumentation, or are classified as unsupported/non-executable during discovery. Kapow-style Function X not found in instrumented module exports failures are eliminated or reduced to documented unsupported cases with regression coverage.

title: TS type syntax regression
description: Observed in /home/ketan/project/kapow make shatter-explore artifacts from 2026-05-13. After removing already-filed alias/timeout/TMPDIR/no-target issues, 32 failed TypeScript/TSX files still fail at execute time with unstripped type or TSX syntax, including Unexpected identifier ButtonProps, ReactNode, BadgeProps, FitSubset, StudentProfile; Unexpected token <; Unexpected token export; and Unexpected token {. Examples: shatter-artifacts/files/web__src__components__auth__DevSignInMenu.tsx.log, web__src__components__search__BookmarksDrawer.tsx.log, web__src__pages__About.tsx.log, web__src__App.tsx.log. This appears to be a current regression or uncovered path relative to closed str-jeen.11.
acceptance_criteria: TS/TSX exploration no longer executes modules containing unstripped TypeScript or JSX syntax. Regression fixtures cover React props types, ReactNode, exported TSX components, type-only syntax imported through dependencies, and JSX tokens. Failures that cannot be executed are reported as structured transform/unsupported errors rather than InternalError runtime failures.

title: Prerelease binary channel
description: Use GitHub prereleases for Shatter continuous binary builds so the project does not imply formal semver releases. Update installer resolution to find the newest continuous prerelease instead of relying on GitHub's formal latest-release endpoint. Follow-up context: the public GitHub project currently shows no release/package entry until CI actually publishes the first build.
acceptance_criteria: Release workflow marks continuous builds as GitHub prereleases; installer resolves latest/continuous/nightly by selecting the newest continuous-* prerelease; docs explain that binaries are prerelease/nightly-style and that a first CI publication is required before the install path works publicly.

title: TS path aliases unresolved
description:
Observed while running make shatter-explore in /home/ketan/project/kapow on 2026-05-13.

The TypeScript frontend says the path-alias adapter is active, but execution still fails to resolve Kapow path aliases. Example log:

shatter-artifacts/files/web__src__components__search__HideButton.tsx.log

Key lines:

- [info]   HideButton adapter [active]: ts/module-resolution/tsconfig-paths (config)
- [error] Exploration error for HideButton: observe stage failed: unexpected response from frontend: execute error (InternalError): Execute failed: Cannot find package @/lib imported from /home/ketan/project/kapow/web/src/stores/authStore.ts

Across the failed target logs, 25 failed files contain Cannot find package @/... . These failures affect normal Vite/React source files and make many component explores fail before exercising user code.

This is distinct from ordinary relative-import stubbing already tracked elsewhere: the adapter claims tsconfig path support is active, but aliased dependencies still fail at execute time.
acceptance_criteria: TypeScript frontend execution resolves project path aliases configured by tsconfig/Vite, including @/*, during execute as well as discovery. Add a regression fixture where one file imports an aliased module and an explored function reaches that import through a dependency. Kapow-style web component explores no longer fail with Cannot find package @/lib for imports rooted at web/src.

title: Explore timeout leaks
description:
Observed while running `make shatter-explore` in `/home/ketan/project/kapow` on 2026-05-13 with `SHATTER_TIMEOUT=30` and Shatter invoked as `shatter explore <path> --spec --timeout-explore 30`.

Several individual files/functions ran far beyond the configured 30 second timeout, with the Shatter parent process hot while the frontend child was idle. Representative examples captured from live process snapshots and per-file logs:

- `api/graph/resolver/search_fields_helpers.go`: log reports `enumOptionsToModel` as `[progress] failed 7/8 ... (321.9s)`, then `[batch 7/7] ... (ok)` and writes a spec bundle. This is both over-timeout and status-inconsistent.
- `tools/kapow/internal/activity/store.go`: 157 lines / 4 functions; Shatter parent was at about 100% CPU after ~2 minutes, frontend idle, and the file completed around ~3 minutes.
- `tools/kapow/internal/cacheartifact/cacheartifact.go`: 144 lines / 8 functions; same parent-hot/frontend-idle profile past ~2 minutes.
- `tools/samplesql/extractor.go`: 228 lines / 5 functions; parent hot near ~3 minutes while frontend idle.
- `web/src/components/search/compareTable.ts`: 259 lines / 7 detected function/const/class entries; TypeScript frontend mostly idle while Shatter parent was hot after ~2 minutes.

The run summary reported `Timed out targets: 0`, so these overruns are not surfaced as timeout failures. The observed behavior makes `--timeout-explore 30` unreliable for broad runs and makes progress output hard to trust.
acceptance_criteria: The explore timeout is enforced consistently for each function and for any parent-side solver/orchestrator phase covered by the function budget. Progress output and batch status use consistent terminology: a timed-out function is not later reported as ok without an explicit incomplete/timed-out status in the spec bundle. Add regression coverage for a small Go target and a small TypeScript target whose frontend returns quickly but whose exploration loop would otherwise continue past the configured timeout.

title: TMPDIR ignored for temp dirs
description:
Observed while running `make shatter-explore` in `/home/ketan/project/kapow` on 2026-05-13 with `/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/debug/shatter`.

The Kapow wrapper sets a per-file `TMPDIR` under `shatter-artifacts/tmp/run-*/explore-*` before invoking Shatter. Despite that, the broad run repeatedly reported host `/tmp` mutations and named Shatter-owned temp directories:

- `/tmp/kapow-shatter-explore-20260513.log` lines 403-413: `shatter-sandbox-*` entries after `tools/kapow/admissions.go`.
- Same log lines 661-667: `shatter-go-e2e-*` and `shatter-instrument-*` entries after `tools/samplesql/extractor.go`.

Earlier diagnostics also flagged unrelated ambient `/tmp` files, but the Shatter-owned names make the core issue clear: Shatter is creating or leaving temp directories in host `/tmp` instead of respecting the configured `TMPDIR`. This breaks sandbox/host-mutation checks and pollutes shared temp space during broad runs.
acceptance_criteria: Shatter creates sandbox, instrumentation, and frontend temporary directories under the effective TMPDIR or another configured run-local temp root. A regression test runs shatter explore with TMPDIR set to a sentinel directory and asserts no shatter-* entries are created in host /tmp. Kapow make shatter-explore no longer emits host /tmp sandbox-change diagnostics caused by Shatter-owned temp directories.

title: Markdown source counts wrong
description:
Observed while running `make shatter-scan` in `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit` on 2026-05-13 with the current local Shatter binary.

The machine-readable artifact and the rendered Markdown report disagree about the selected source set:

- `web/shatter-artifacts/scan-results/d1af50c637243db1b489346634e9be72a5faa5d7f4328928da057d0b3c301e5a/run-status.json` reports `source_snapshot.selected_source_files = 53`, `source_snapshot.selected_source_lines = 7733`, and `rollups.source_buckets[0] = production_ish 53 files / 7733 lines`.
- The default stdout Markdown report in `/tmp/pickpackit-make-shatter-scan-redirected-2026-05-13.log` says `production_ish | 52 | 665` and `Production-ish source lines: 665 (whole-source denominator; see Coverage section below)`.

This is a user-facing reporting integrity problem: the Markdown calls 665 a whole-source denominator even though the run-status artifact has the correct whole-source count of 7733. The mismatch makes broad-run coverage look much smaller and less clearly tied to the actual source snapshot.
acceptance_criteria:
Markdown scan reports derive Source Set Summary counts from the same source-snapshot or rollup data used by run-status.json.
The stdout Markdown report and run-status.json agree on selected source file and line counts for a pickpackit-style TypeScript scan.
Add a regression test that renders a scan report and asserts Markdown source bucket counts match the structured run-status/source snapshot counts, including when some functions fail.
The report no longer labels function-span or attempted-span lines as a whole-source denominator.

title: CLI stdout EAGAIN panic
description:
Observed while running `make shatter-scan` in `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit` on 2026-05-13 with the current local Shatter binary at `/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/debug/shatter` added to `PATH`.

Two runs isolate the failure:

- `PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/debug:$PATH make shatter-scan 2>&1 | tee /tmp/pickpackit-make-shatter-scan-localbin-2026-05-13.log` under the agent PTY exited 101.
- The same target with stdout/stderr redirected to `/tmp/pickpackit-make-shatter-scan-redirected-2026-05-13.log` exited 0.

The first run generated about 22k lines of output, reached Markdown report rendering, then panicked:

`thread main panicked at library/std/src/io/stdio.rs:1165:9: failed printing to stdout: Resource temporarily unavailable (os error 11)`

The scan artifacts show the scan itself had completed enough to write `summary.json` and `run-status.json`; the failure is in user-facing report output. A large report or slow/nonblocking stdout sink should not produce a Rust panic or crash the CLI with an internal backtrace path.
acceptance_criteria:
A large scan report written to a PTY, pipe, or simulated nonblocking writer does not panic the Shatter process.
Stdout write failures are handled as controlled CLI I/O errors, or output rendering retries/uses blocking writes where appropriate.
Add a regression test around report rendering with a writer that returns `WouldBlock` or `BrokenPipe`.
A pickpackit-style scan piped through `tee` either exits successfully or fails with a concise non-panic diagnostic.
close_reason: Fix merged: bca68eb7 Handle stdout EAGAIN/BrokenPipe without panicking

title: Source set drops failures
description:
Observed while running the direct Shatter scan equivalent in /home/ketan/project/refute on 2026-05-13. The report said: 108 functions discovered, 107 attempted, 2 completed, 105 failed, and 1 skipped.

Despite most attempted functions coming from /home/ketan/project/refute/cmd and /home/ketan/project/refute/internal, the Source Set Summary reported production_ish = 0 files / 0 lines, fixture_sample = 2 files / 6 lines, and Production-ish source lines = 0. The scan log separately reported 42 discovered source files. A local line count over cmd/refute/main.go, internal/**/*.go, and testdata/fixtures/go/**/*.go is 7,189 Go lines.

The report appears to build source_set from completed function report rows only. Because only two fixture functions completed, failed production files disappear from the source-set denominator. This makes a broad run with widespread production failures look like no production source was selected.
acceptance_criteria:
Source Set Summary is computed from the selected/discovered source set, not only completed function rows.
Failed, skipped, and unsupported production-ish files/functions still contribute to source_set production_ish file and line counts.
Markdown and JSON reports agree on source_set counts and make failed production span impact visible.
Add a regression scan fixture where production functions are discovered and fail before completion; the report must not show production_ish = 0 when those files were selected.

title: Go launcher go version
description:
Observed while running the direct Shatter scan equivalent in /home/ketan/project/refute on 2026-05-13, after the requested make shatter target was absent. Command: /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/debug/shatter --color never scan --progress .

Refute go.mod declares go 1.26.3. In the Refute checkout, go version reports go1.26.3 and go env GOVERSION reports go1.26.3. Shatter discovered 108 functions and attempted 107, but 105 failed with the same launcher build error:

go: module /home/ketan/project/refute requires go >= 1.26.3 (running go 1.26.1)

Root cause from artifacts: generated instrumented modules copy Refute's go 1.26.3, but generated launcher modules still declare go 1.23.0 while requiring/replacing github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute. Re-running go build -mod=mod -buildvcs=false . inside a generated launcher dir reproduces the same error. The Go command does not auto-upgrade the toolchain from the replaced dependency module's go directive in this launcher-module shape.

Expected behavior: generated launcher modules should use a go/toolchain directive compatible with the target module, or Shatter should preflight the mismatch once and classify the selected Go source as blocked by environment/toolchain instead of emitting one InstrumentationFailed row per function.
acceptance_criteria:
Generated Go launcher go.mod uses the target module's required Go version or another compatible version/toolchain directive.
A regression fixture with a target module requiring a newer Go patch/minor than the launcher default explores at least one function successfully under the normal Go frontend path.
If the required Go toolchain is unavailable, Shatter reports one clear preflight/toolchain status with installed and required versions instead of per-function InstrumentationFailed build failures.
A Refute scan no longer fails 105/107 attempted functions with running go 1.26.1 when the shell Go toolchain resolves go1.26.3.

title: Shatter eval harness unreliable
description:
## Current behavior

A Shatter evaluation pass from the Kapow repo is currently unreliable enough that it cannot produce a trustworthy completeness/performance/correctness report.

Observed on 2026-05-12/2026-05-13 from `/home/ketan/project/kapow` using `/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/debug/shatter` (`shatter 0.1.0`):

1. `make shatter` fails immediately because the Makefile has no `shatter` target.
2. The nearest target, `make shatter-explore`, spends about 2 minutes before the first eligible file starts, then exits with code 2 on `api/cmd/fixtureutil/main.go`.
