title: Swarm: Epic: Semantic harness exec
description:
Swarm molecule orchestrating epic str-pf51.

Epic: str-pf51
Coordinator:

title: Go semantic harness
description:
Replace the legacy Go temp-harness flow with a package-aware semantic harness backed by lifecycle-specific storage.

Scope:
- use one stable dispatch harness preserving package-private access
- keep reusable generated/build state in tool-owned cache outside the project tree
- replace delete-on-exit temp reuse as the normal execution path
acceptance_criteria:
- Go module-backed execution no longer depends on the legacy one-shot temp harness flow.
- Package-private helpers in sibling files remain callable through the semantic harness.
- Reusable harness/build state lives in tool-owned cache instead of temp or preserved-artifact locations.

title: Rust crate-bridge harness
description:
Add the opt-in Rust `crate_bridge` mode for functions that cannot be called from the default `bin_only` harness.

Scope:
- add a feature-gated normal module declaration in the library crate
- generate one stable library-side wrapper module
- route the stable harness bin through that wrapper when `crate_bridge` is enabled
acceptance_criteria:
- `crate_bridge` mode is explicit opt-in and adds only the documented stable integration points.
- Wrapper code is compiled inside the library crate through a feature-gated normal module declaration.
- Functions requiring crate-private callable surfaces can execute in `crate_bridge` mode.

title: Rust bin-only harness
description:
Implement the default Rust semantic harness mode using a stable generated bin target inside the user crate.

Scope:
- generate one stable harness bin entrypoint
- execute requests over stdin with dispatch by function identifier
- replace the legacy temp-project path for crate-backed Rust files in this mode
acceptance_criteria:
- Rust crate-backed execution uses the stable `bin_only` harness path instead of the legacy temp project.
- Different inputs for the same target no longer force source regeneration or recompilation.
- Functions with publicly nameable callable surfaces execute successfully in `bin_only` mode.

title: Harness storage API
description:
Add shared storage helpers that split semantic-harness state by lifecycle.

Scope:
- deterministic tool-owned cache/build location per project
- per-request scratch location under temp
- preserved output location under `shatter-artifacts/`
- remove assumptions that temp directories are reusable cache
acceptance_criteria:
- Storage APIs expose separate cache, scratch, and preserved-artifact roots.
- Reusable harness/build state no longer lives in temp-scoped directories.
- Preserved outputs resolve to `shatter-artifacts/` for project-backed runs.

title: Epic: Semantic harness exec
description:
Track the remaining work to replace legacy Rust and Go harness execution with the semantic harness architecture.

Scope:
- lifecycle-specific storage instead of temp-dir reuse
- Rust `bin_only` default mode and `crate_bridge` opt-in mode
- Go package-aware semantic harness execution
- standalone-file fallback using tool-owned cache
- cleanup, recovery, and docs alignment

This epic excludes persistent subprocess work for now.

title: Stdin-based input passing for Rust and Go harnesses
description:
Change harness generation in both Rust and Go frontends to read inputs from stdin (JSON) instead of baking them into generated source code. This is the highest-leverage single change: it eliminates per-iteration recompilation entirely, since the binary is the same across executions with different inputs.

Rust: change generate_harness() in shatter-rust/src/executor.rs to emit a main() that reads serde_json::from_reader(stdin()). Change execute_function_with_timing() to pipe inputs JSON to the subprocess stdin.

Go: change generateHarness() in shatter-go/instrument/executor.go to emit a main() that reads json.NewDecoder(os.Stdin). Change ExecuteFunctionWithTiming() to pipe inputs via stdin.

This is a prerequisite for all other harness caching work — without it, every input change forces a recompile.
acceptance_criteria:
Rust harness reads inputs from stdin, not baked into source.
Go harness reads inputs from stdin, not baked into source.
Second execute of the same function with different inputs does NOT trigger recompilation.
All existing executor tests pass (updated for stdin protocol).
E2E concolic tests pass.

title: Parity validator: adapt to canonical matrix schema
description: The parity validator (scripts/validate-parity.py from str-7jgm.2) expects a frontends-centric schema (with 'frontends' top-level key) but the canonical parity-matrix.yaml (from str-7jgm.1) uses a command-centric schema (with 'commands' top-level key). The validator fails immediately: 'missing required frontends key'. Rewrite the validator to consume the command-centric schema that parity-matrix.yaml actually uses.
acceptance_criteria: python3 scripts/validate-parity.py exits 0 against the canonical protocol/parity-matrix.yaml. npx task parity passes.

title: Side effect normalization
description: Normalize side-effect categories and payload shapes across TS, Go, and Rust so equivalent behaviors are reported consistently at the protocol level.
acceptance_criteria: Equivalent side-effect categories use the same protocol names and payload conventions across frontends.\nDivergences are explicitly documented where parity is not yet possible.\nParity tests validate normalized outputs.

title: Rust global state capture
description: Add notable mutable global/static state change capture to the Rust frontend so side-effect reporting moves closer to TypeScript parity.
acceptance_criteria: Rust reports notable mutable global/static state changes in structured side_effects output.\nBehavior is documented and deterministic.\nCross-frontend parity tests or fixtures cover the new category.

title: Go global state capture
description: Add notable in-memory global/package state change capture to the Go frontend so side-effect reporting moves closer to TypeScript parity.
acceptance_criteria: Go reports notable global/package state changes in structured side_effects output.\nBehavior is documented and deterministic.\nCross-frontend parity tests or fixtures cover the new category.

title: Capture flag semantics
description: Define and implement consistent side-effect capture flag semantics across TS, Go, and Rust frontends so capture-on/off behavior means the same thing everywhere.
acceptance_criteria: Capture-on/off semantics are consistent across frontends.\nProtocol outputs under capture-disabled mode are documented and aligned.\nCross-frontend tests cover flag behavior.

title: Epic: Side-effect parity
description: Bring side-effect capture and reporting behavior closer to parity across TypeScript, Go, and Rust frontends at the protocol/output level. Keep this separate from governance checks and from capture-performance/defaults work.

title: TS defer setup/generate
description: Defer setup-loader and wasm-generator imports until setup/generate requests actually need them.
acceptance_criteria: Handshake and analyze/execute flows do not eagerly import setup-loader or wasm-generator.\nSetup and generate still initialize correctly on first use.\nPerf evidence shows lower fixed startup cost.

title: TS lazy-load executor
description: Defer loading executor and its transitive dependencies until the first execute request, instead of importing it during frontend startup.
acceptance_criteria: Handshake/analyze-only startup does not eagerly import executor.\nExecute requests still work after lazy initialization.\nPerf evidence shows lower fixed startup cost.

title: TS light handshake path
description: Split handshake/protocol parsing from eager heavy handler imports so the TS frontend can become request-ready before loading analyze/execute/setup machinery.
acceptance_criteria: Handshake path does not eagerly load the full handlers dependency graph.\nFirst handshake response time is reduced.\nSubsequent command handling remains correct.

title: Repeated execute reuses prepared target
description: When the same function is executed multiple times in one session, the second and later runs reuse one already-prepared execution target instead of rebuilding behind generic execute calls. Scope this to the repeated-execute behavior end-to-end: same target identity, different inputs, warm reuse visible in one session. Do not broaden this issue into general lifecycle plumbing, cross-frontend parity work, or broader explore integration.
acceptance_criteria:
Two executes of the same function in one session reuse one prepared target.
Repeated execute does not rebuild or restart the harness when build-affecting inputs are unchanged.
If the prepared target is stale or invalid, execute follows a defined rebuild or recovery path and still returns a result.

title: Shrink budget policy
description: Replace one flat shrink budget with a policy that depends on witness and path complexity.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink budget policy is documented and deterministic.\nTrivial witnesses use less shrink work than complex witnesses.\nPerf evidence shows reduced shrink cost without obvious witness regressions.

title: Selective shrink targeting
description: Shrink only the highest-value path witnesses instead of shrinking every discovered path equally.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink selection policy is deterministic and documented.\nLow-value or already-simple paths can be skipped.\nPerf evidence shows reduced shrink tail with acceptable output quality.

title: Grouped shrink fallback
description: Add grouped parameter shrinking between bulk shrink and one-parameter-at-a-time fallback for multi-parameter witnesses.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink uses a deterministic grouped fallback strategy when bulk shrink fails.\nPath identity is preserved for accepted shrinks.\nPerf evidence shows benefit on multi-parameter witnesses.

title: Bulk shrink first
description: Attempt to shrink all parameters together before falling back to one-at-a-time shrinking, so broad simplifications are found with fewer execute round-trips.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink first attempts a deterministic all-parameter candidate.\nIf bulk shrink changes the path, the algorithm falls back to finer-grained shrinking.\nPerf evidence shows fewer shrink execute round-trips.

title: Shrink witness selection
description: Choose the best already-observed witness per unique path before shrinking instead of always shrinking the first witness seen.
acceptance_criteria: Shrink selects one representative witness per path using a deterministic ranking policy.\nOutputs remain valid for the same path identities.\nPerf evidence shows fewer shrink executes or smaller final witnesses.

title: Analyze-only light startup
description: Avoid execute/setup/generator-heavy frontend initialization for analyze-only command paths.
acceptance_criteria: Analyze-only flows use a lighter startup path than full explore/execute.\nAnalyze results remain unchanged.\nPerf evidence shows lower fixed cost for analyze-only commands.
notes: Startup profiling: direct module-load timings show handlers eagerly imports executor (~206ms), analyzer (~184ms), instrumentor (~185ms), setup-loader (~186ms), wasm-generator (~8ms), and logger/pino (~19ms) during handshake/analyze-only startup. Prioritize this issue before broader startup staging; analyze-only can likely avoid executor/setup-loader/wasm-generator entirely.

title: TS startup stages
description: Split TS frontend bootstrap into staged readiness so early requests are not blocked on execute-heavy initialization.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend reaches handshake-ready state before all execute-heavy initialization completes.\nRequests that need only earlier stages can begin sooner.\nPerf evidence shows reduced fixed startup cost or earlier first useful work.
notes: Startup profiling: handlers module load is ~233-242ms in-process. After preloading executor, residual handlers load drops to ~16ms; after preloading analyzer/instrumentor/setup-loader too, residual drops to ~1.4ms. The main opportunity is to split handshake/protocol readiness from eager heavy imports, then stage or lazy-load executor, setup-loader, wasm-generator, and possibly analyzer/instrumentor.

title: TS no-capture fast path
description: Add a cheaper TypeScript execute path for capture-disabled runs that skips capture-specific wrappers and bookkeeping.
acceptance_criteria: TS execute has a dedicated no-capture path.\nNon-capture outputs remain correct.\nPerf evidence shows lower per-execute cost with capture disabled.

title: Side effects default-off
description: Make rich side-effect capture opt-in instead of default so the fast path is optimized for exploration throughput.
acceptance_criteria: CLI exposes side-effect capture control.\nDefault behavior disables rich side-effect capture.\nDocs describe capture-on vs capture-off behavior.

title: Epic: Explore cost reduction
description: Reduce fixed per-function cost, repeated execute rebuild cost, shrink overhead, and frontend bootstrap waste without changing exploration completeness rules. Excludes early-stop heuristics.

title: Explore same-lang reuse
description: Reuse one frontend session across multiple same-language explore targets in a single command so per-target handshake cost is not paid repeatedly.
acceptance_criteria: A single explore command with multiple same-language targets does not spawn one frontend per target.\nReported results remain correct and target-scoped.\nPerf evidence shows reduced fixed overhead for multi-target explore.
close_reason: Already implemented and merged to main via d68d653 (Merge: 69a77db). One frontend per language reused across same-language targets. All acceptance criteria met.

title: Scan idle worker reaping
description: Reap idle frontend workers during scan when queued work drops, so large pools do not stay warm longer than needed.
acceptance_criteria: Idle workers can be shut down after they are no longer justified by queued work.\nWorker replacement on timeout/crash still works correctly.\nPerf evidence shows no regression on large scans and reduced wasted startup on shrinking workloads.

title: Scan adaptive pool growth
description: Grow the frontend worker pool on demand during scan up to the --parallelism ceiling, based on runnable stale work rather than fixed eager startup.
acceptance_criteria: Parallel scan starts with fewer than the maximum workers when appropriate.\nThe pool can grow toward --parallelism when runnable work justifies it.\nPerf evidence shows no regression on large no-cache scans and improved behavior on mixed workloads.

title: Scan lazy worker spawn
description: Delay TS/Go/Rust worker creation in parallel scan until there is confirmed exploration work to run, instead of eagerly spawning the full pool before cache-hit filtering.
acceptance_criteria: Warm cache-hit scans do not eagerly spawn unused workers.\nParallel scan still explores stale functions correctly.\nPerf evidence shows reduced startup overhead on cache-hit workloads.

title: Epic: Frontend worker pool
description: Improve frontend worker lifecycle so startup cost is paid only when justified by real exploration work. Cover lazy spawn, adaptive growth, reaping, and frontend reuse where it materially improves scan/explore performance.

title: Frontend issue template
description: Add or update issue templates and checklists so frontend and protocol work must declare parity impact, default behavior, and required test updates.
acceptance_criteria:
Issue template asks whether protocol-visible behavior changed
Template records parity impact and default on/off state
Template requires test and contract updates when applicable

title: Parity task target
description: Add a single repo task or command that runs the parity validator and parity test suite together for CI and local verification.
acceptance_criteria:
One documented task runs all parity checks
The task is wired into CI or documented for gating
Failure output identifies the offending capability or fixture

title: Parity contract spec
description: Add a checked-in frontend parity contract in docs plus a machine-readable matrix that classifies protocol-visible capabilities as required, optional, or frontend-specific.
acceptance_criteria:
Checked-in docs and machine-readable parity matrix exist
Each capability has required/optional/frontend-specific status
Allowed divergences are explicitly documented

title: Parity validator check
description: Add a deterministic parity validator that fails when required capabilities are missing, undocumented, or default states differ from the checked-in parity contract.
acceptance_criteria:
Validator fails on missing required capabilities
Validator fails on undocumented protocol-visible capabilities
Validator is runnable in CI and locally

title: Parity golden tests
description: Add cross-frontend golden tests over shared fixtures that compare normalized protocol outputs for required capabilities.
acceptance_criteria:
Shared fixtures cover representative side effects and protocol outputs
Normalized outputs are compared across TS, Go, and Rust
Tests fail on unintended required-capability divergence

title: CLI parity checks
description: Add deterministic checks for cross-frontend CLI flags and defaults for frontend-visible controls, including future side-effect capture controls.
acceptance_criteria:
Relevant commands expose the same governed flags where required
Default values are checked against the parity contract
CI fails on mismatched governed CLI defaults

title: Parity workflow rules
description: Update project instructions and completion workflow so any protocol-visible frontend change must update the parity contract and add or adjust parity tests.
acceptance_criteria:
AGENTS or project instructions require parity updates for protocol-visible changes
Completion guidance references parity validation explicitly
The rule distinguishes output parity from implementation details

title: Isolation perf corpus
description: Add benchmark scenarios that compare serial, function, and none isolation modes and track the now-identified hot phases: handshake, execute transpile/module load, and shrink/refine.
acceptance_criteria:
Corpus includes simple, medium, and branchier functions
Corpus includes scan and explore cases
Results are reportable by isolation mode and by hot timing phases

title: Isolation perf guard
description: Extend perf reporting so isolation-mode changes are compared against baseline and the main serial cost centers remain visible over time.
acceptance_criteria:
Reports compare wall time by isolation mode
Reports surface handshake, execute transpile/module load, and shrink/refine costs
Docs explain when to use each isolation mode and how to interpret regressions

title: Scheduler policy layer
description: Refactor explore/scan scheduling behind an isolation-policy abstraction so overlap rules are centralized and can evolve with profiling evidence.
acceptance_criteria:
Current conservative behavior is preserved under serial
Policy selection is centralized rather than ad hoc
Tests cover mode selection and scheduling eligibility rules

title: Function-level parallel mode
description: Implement --isolation function so different functions may run concurrently, while one function is owned by only one worker at a time. This is the lower-risk overlap mode before same-function multi-worker execution.
acceptance_criteria:
Different functions may run concurrently
Same-function executions do not overlap across workers
Representative scan/explore cases match current behavior and improve throughput where multiple functions are pending

title: Same-function parallel mode
description: Implement --isolation none scheduling that allows the same function to run on multiple workers, assuming side-effect-safe/stateless execution by default and merging discovery state across workers.
acceptance_criteria:
Same-function executions can run on different workers
Per-function discovery state merges correctly
Duplicate-work controls exist so multi-worker execution does not explode redundant effort

title: Intra-function pipelining
description: Overlap candidate generation and bookkeeping with in-flight executions inside the exploration loop. This targets the serial execute pipeline directly rather than only coarse worker parallelism.
acceptance_criteria:
Next candidates can be prepared while current executions run
External semantics remain unchanged
Profiling shows reduced serial idle time inside one function exploration

title: Reuse execute artifacts
description: Eliminate repeated execute-side transpile and module-load work where safe by reusing instrumented execution artifacts across requests. Profiling shows execute.transpile and execute.module_load dominate serial runtime more than analyze or instrument.
acceptance_criteria:
Instrumented execute path avoids recompiling/reloading on every request where semantics allow
Timing artifacts show a material drop in execute.transpile and/or execute.module_load
Correctness is preserved for representative functions under the supported isolation modes

title: Shrink and refine budgeting
description: Make shrink/refinement cost explicit and controllable in the default exploration flow. Profiling shows shrink.execute_round_trip is often the single largest cost bucket, even for simple functions.
acceptance_criteria:
Reports distinguish discovery-time execution from shrink/refine execution
Representative runs can reduce or disable shrink/refine work through explicit controls
Profiling shows lower total runtime when shrink/refine is reduced on simple functions

title: Isolation flag semantics
description: Add --isolation {none,function,serial} to explore and scan, with semantics shaped by the profiling findings: default to minimal isolation and maximum overlap, while retaining stricter fallback modes for debugging and comparison.
acceptance_criteria:
CLI accepts --isolation for explore and scan
Help text defines none, function, and serial clearly
Default is none and docs explain that it assumes side-effect-safe/stateless executions by default

title: MC/DC E2E tests and walkthrough
description: Phase 9. Add known-answer functions with compound decisions (examples/typescript/src/13-mcdc-compound.ts). E2E test in e2e_concolic.rs verifies --mcdc achieves 100% MC/DC. Add --mcdc to one explore invocation in demo/walkthrough.sh.
acceptance_criteria:
- 13-mcdc-compound.ts with compoundAnd and compoundOr functions
- Expected branches and triggering inputs documented
- E2E test passes with 100% MC/DC on known-answer functions
- Walkthrough includes --mcdc invocation
- Walkthrough passes without errors

title: MC/DC coverage reporting
description: Phase 6. Add MC/DC section to format_exploration_report when mcdc metrics present. Show: total decisions, total conditions, opaque/always-masked counts, independence percentage, per-decision breakdown with condition status. Add McdcMetrics computation in pipeline::analyze. Add DiscoveryMethod::McdcTarget handling in CoverageMetrics::from_exploration.
acceptance_criteria:
- MC/DC section in exploration report with tree-style formatting
- Per-decision breakdown showing which conditions satisfied/missing
- McdcMetrics computed correctly from McdcTable
- DiscoveryMethod::McdcTarget counted in metrics
- Infeasible pairs reported separately from missing
- Coverage bar for MC/DC percentage

title: Targeted MC/DC solver: solve_for_mcdc_independence
description: Phase 5. Add solve_for_mcdc_independence to solver.rs. Takes prefix constraints, per-condition SymExprs, observed values, and target index. Holds non-target conditions at observed values, flips target. Skip Unknown conditions. Orchestrator must re-execute with solved inputs and verify decision outcome actually flips before recording independence pair. Add worklist integration with InputSource::McdcTarget.
acceptance_criteria:
- solve_for_mcdc_independence function in solver.rs
- Correct constraint assembly: prefix + hold others + flip target
- Unknown conditions skipped gracefully
- Re-execution verification before recording independence pair
- Worklist entries created with McdcTarget source
- Unit tests: known-solvable and UNSAT cases
- proptest: valid inputs never panic

title: Core MC/DC tracking in orchestrator
description: Phase 4. After each new-path observation in the concolic loop, when config.mcdc is true: update McdcTable from BranchDecision.conditions, identify conditions lacking independence pairs, feed gaps to solver (phase 5). Add McdcTarget to InputSource (between Drilled=3 and Z3Solved, renumbering Z3Solved to 5 and UserProvided to 6). Plateau counter resets on new MC/DC pair satisfaction. Add McdcComplete termination reason.
acceptance_criteria:
- McdcTable updated after each new-path observation
- Independence pair detection works for masking MC/DC
- InputSource::McdcTarget added with correct priority ordering
- Plateau counter resets on MC/DC progress
- TerminationReason::McdcComplete added
- proptest: independence detection is monotonic (adding observations never decreases count)

title: TS frontend: condition decomposition for MC/DC
description: Phase 3. Modify instrumentor.ts to decompose pure && and || chains into per-condition thunks when SHATTER_MCDC=1. Add __shatter_mcdc_record runtime function that evaluates thunks left-to-right respecting short-circuit semantics. Masked conditions get value:null, masked:true. Add __shatter_branch_mcdc that records decision + conditions. Mixed &&/|| trees emit diagnostic warning and fall back to branch-only. Add flattenConditions() parallel to buildSymExpr. Simple non-compound decisions use existing __shatter_branch unchanged.
acceptance_criteria:
- flattenConditions extracts leaf conditions from pure &&/|| chains
- __shatter_mcdc_record uses thunks, never evaluates masked conditions
- __shatter_branch_mcdc records decision + per-condition ConditionOutcome
- Mixed operators emit warning, treated as single decision
- Existing non-compound branches unchanged
- fast-check property: flattenConditions output logically equivalent to input
- fast-check property: masking consistent with short-circuit semantics
- Cap at 16 conditions per decision

title: MC/DC CLI flag and budget multipliers
description: Phase 2. Add --mcdc flag to explore command in args.rs. When set: max_iterations 5x (500), plateau_threshold 3x (60), solver_timeout default 10s, wall-clock timeout 5x (300s). User-explicit values override. Add mcdc: bool to ExploreConfig. Set SHATTER_MCDC=1 env var before spawning frontends. Must work with both --concolic and random explorer paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- --mcdc flag in CLI args
- Budget multipliers applied when no explicit override
- ExploreConfig.mcdc field added
- SHATTER_MCDC=1 env var set for frontends
- Flag works with both --concolic and random paths
- Unit test: budget defaults with/without --mcdc

title: MC/DC data model: ConditionOutcome, McdcTable, McdcMetrics, protocol schema
description: Phase 1. Add ConditionOutcome type to execution_record.rs, McdcTable to new mcdc.rs, McdcMetrics to coverage_metrics.rs. Add condition-outcome.schema.json. Add optional conditions field to BranchDecision (all 4 locations: core + 3 frontends). Add valid/invalid fixtures per protocol governance. Path hash must not be affected by conditions field.
acceptance_criteria:
- ConditionOutcome type in execution_record.rs with value: Option<bool>, masked: bool, constraint: SymConstraint
- BranchDecision has optional conditions field in core + TS + Go + Rust frontend types
- condition-outcome.schema.json with value type [boolean, null]
- McdcTable and DecisionMcdc in new mcdc.rs with independence pair detection
- McdcMetrics in coverage_metrics.rs
- Protocol roundtrip tests pass for ConditionOutcome in all frontends
- Protocol fixtures added

title: [epic] MC/DC coverage support
description: Add Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) to Shatter via --mcdc CLI flag. Full hybrid approach: frontend condition decomposition + core-side MC/DC tracking and targeted Z3 solving. See docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-17-mcdc-coverage-design.md for the full spec.

title: Perf stat capture
description: Extend the external profiling harness so selected scenarios run under perf stat and emit key counters in raw and parsed form. This is the routine counter collection path for Linux performance tracking.
acceptance_criteria: Per-scenario perf stat can be enabled; raw perf stat output is persisted; parsed counters are stored in machine-readable form; summary includes cycles, instructions, task-clock, branches, branch-misses, and cache-misses
notes: Merged locally into main at 51d2935. Not pushed yet; leaving issue open/in_progress. Pre-completion is still blocked by the local E2E environment error from z3-sys missing z3-4.16.0.

title: Perf harness runner
description: Add wrapper scripts and tasks to run a named profiling scenario or the stable external benchmark corpus repeatedly and store raw timing artifacts. This should be the supported entrypoint for repeatable profiling runs.
acceptance_criteria: One command runs a single named scenario; one command runs the stable corpus; raw per-run results and aggregate output are stored under a stable artifact layout; harness isolates temp and cache directories per run
notes: Merged locally into main at 51d2935. Not pushed yet; leaving issue open/in_progress. Pre-completion is still blocked by the local E2E environment error from z3-sys missing z3-4.16.0.

title: Perf scenario corpus
description: Define the fixed external profiling scenario set for walkthrough, explore, and scan. Cover cold-cache and warm-cache variants and include representative TS, Go, and Rust command paths. The result is a checked-in manifest that drives the external benchmarking harness.
acceptance_criteria: Checked-in scenario manifest exists; each scenario specifies command, env, timeout, warmups, repeats, and cache policy; corpus covers walkthrough plus targeted explore and scan scenarios; corpus includes at least one Go-focused scenario suitable for pprof follow-up
notes: Merged locally into main at 51d2935. Not pushed yet; leaving issue open/in_progress. Pre-completion is still blocked by the local E2E environment error from z3-sys missing z3-4.16.0.

title: Perf regression compare
description: Add a comparator that reads two benchmark result bundles and reports regressions and improvements by scenario and phase.
acceptance_criteria: Consumes benchmark JSON artifacts without needing raw logs.

title: Benchmark run harness
description: Add a repeatable benchmark runner that executes the canonical scenario set with fixed parameters, repeats runs, and emits structured JSON timing bundles.
acceptance_criteria: Persists output only when the user passes an output destination, with untracked output as the default expectation.

title: Sample manifest for perf
description: Define a canonical sample manifest for timing and benchmark scenarios using the same examples already exercised by tests and walkthrough.
acceptance_criteria: Sample set uses existing sample code rather than a separate perf-only corpus.

title: Walkthrough timing summary
description: Update the walkthrough to request and present timing summaries with a hard cap of three human-readable timing lines per step, and optionally persist structured artifacts when a timing destination is provided.
acceptance_criteria: Walkthrough timing uses structured timing data and only persists artifacts when a destination is supplied.

title: TS frontend timing
description: Add TypeScript frontend timing collection and emit protocol timing payloads for the major analysis and execution phases.
acceptance_criteria: Timing collection is quiet unless requested by the core and merges cleanly into the unified run dataset.

title: Explore flow timing spans
description: Instrument the production explore path with named timing phases so the timing system answers where time goes in the real walkthrough path.
acceptance_criteria: Detailed mode can add iteration-level detail without changing default output.

title: Timing schema and flags
description: Define the canonical timing schema and CLI surface. Add Rust timing types for run metadata, scenario metadata, phase summaries, and optional detailed span trees. Add new timing flags and specify --perf compatibility behavior.
acceptance_criteria: If no timing output destination is supplied, no timing artifact is persisted; performance remains execution metrics while broader breakdowns live under timing.

title: Rust timing collector
description: Implement Rust timing collection using tracing in CLI/core, backed by a Shatter-owned collector that aggregates spans into the shared timing schema.
acceptance_criteria: Collector exposes programmatic access for walkthrough and benchmark code.

title: Protocol timing payloads
description: Extend protocol responses with optional timing payloads that use the shared schema, so frontend timings can join the same dataset as core timings.
acceptance_criteria: Existing performance metrics remain intact and backward compatibility is preserved when a frontend does not yet send timing.

title: Go frontend timing
description: Add Go frontend timing collection and emit protocol timing payloads for analysis, rewrite/build, harness execution, and serialization phases.
acceptance_criteria: No persisted timing is written unless the CLI requested output.

title: Perf timing + regress
description: Introduce explicit timing across Shatter CLI, Rust core, and frontends; replace --perf with a structured timing interface; keep timing persistence opt-in via command-line output destination; add repeatable benchmark runs and regression comparison; and keep walkthrough timing to at most 3 human-readable lines per step.

title: Examples: richer mirrored fixtures
description: Add more sophisticated standalone example functions with parity across TypeScript, Go, and Rust. Candidate families: Accept-Language negotiation, robots.txt rule matching, and .env parsing from permissively licensed GitHub code. Also assess walkthrough coverage and update the demo to feature the strongest examples.

title: Walkthrough avoids cargo lock stalls
description: demo/walkthrough.sh appears hung at step 1 when another cargo command holds the workspace artifact lock because every step runs through cargo run --quiet. Reproduced by running the walkthrough while a separate cargo test is active. The walkthrough should avoid the shared target lock or at least stop failing silently.
acceptance_criteria: Running demo/walkthrough.sh while another cargo command is active does not silently stall on Cargo's shared artifact lock. The walkthrough either uses an isolated target directory or otherwise avoids the shared lock. The script remains self-cleaning and documented.
close_reason: Merged. Walkthrough now uses isolated CARGO_TARGET_DIR to avoid lock contention.

Swarm molecule orchestrating epic str-fpgb.

title: Rust version check parity
description: The Rust frontend requires exact protocol-version equality, while the core and TypeScript frontend use a major/minor compatibility rule. This creates frontend-specific handshake behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Add a failing automated test that captures the intended compatibility rule for the Rust handshake; verify it fails on current exact-match behavior; update the Rust frontend so version handling matches the canonical protocol rule
close_reason: Merged to main. Added is_version_compatible() with major.minor parsing to Rust frontend handshake.

title: Rust handshake capability parity
description: The Rust frontend handles setup and teardown commands but does not advertise those capabilities during handshake, making the negotiated contract inaccurate.
acceptance_criteria: Add a failing automated test that checks the Rust handshake capability list against implemented lifecycle commands; verify it fails on current behavior; update the handshake so advertised capabilities match implemented commands

title: Rust invalid JSON response
description: The Rust frontend currently logs malformed JSON requests and drops them without emitting an invalid_request protocol error, unlike the TypeScript frontend.
acceptance_criteria: Add a failing automated test that sends malformed JSON and expects invalid_request; verify it fails on current Rust behavior; update the handler so malformed JSON yields an error response instead of being silently skipped
close_reason: Rust invalid JSON response fix merged to main

title: Rust teardown_ack parity
description: The Rust frontend returns status "teardown" instead of the protocol's "teardown_ack" and its tests currently encode that non-standard behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Add a failing automated test that asserts teardown returns teardown_ack; verify it fails for the current behavior; update the Rust frontend and tests so teardown returns teardown_ack consistently

title: Rust frontend quality gate
description: Include shatter-rust and shatter-rust-runtime in repo quality scripts and future CI so protocol drift in the standalone Rust frontend is exercised by default.
acceptance_criteria: Repo quality entrypoints run shatter-rust and shatter-rust-runtime tests explicitly; CI docs show how to invoke them; protocol checks include the Rust frontend path
close_reason: Rust frontend included in quality gate scripts

title: Frontend conformance harness
description: Add a black-box protocol conformance harness that runs the same NDJSON request/response cases against each frontend subprocess and reports behavioral drift.
acceptance_criteria: Shared conformance cases run against TS, Go, Rust, and the noop frontend; protocol field mismatches fail the harness; expected dynamic fields are normalized explicitly
close_reason: Completed: black-box protocol conformance harness with 6 test cases across all 4 frontends

title: Schema fixture validator
description: Add a repo-owned schema validation runner so protocol fixtures are validated against the shared JSON Schemas in local quality checks and future CI.
acceptance_criteria: A quality entrypoint validates all protocol fixtures against the shared schemas; failures report which fixture and schema mismatched; the check is documented for CI use
close_reason: Merged. Comprehensive schema validator covering 43 fixtures, quality entrypoint at scripts/quality/check-schemas.sh

title: Protocol fixture corpus
description: Add shared valid and invalid protocol fixtures for requests and responses so schema checks and frontend conformance tests use the same corpus.
acceptance_criteria: Fixtures exist for every command, lifecycle message, malformed request class, and canonical error response; fixtures are organized for reuse by schema and conformance checks

title: Lifecycle schema coverage
description: The shared request/response schemas do not fully model setup/teardown lifecycle messages even though the protocol and implementations support them. This leaves schema validation unable to represent valid traffic.
acceptance_criteria: Schemas include valid setup and teardown request/response variants; fixtures cover those messages; schema validation accepts lifecycle traffic that current frontends emit

title: Protocol registry scaffold
description: Add a canonical machine-readable protocol registry under protocol/ that becomes the single source of truth for commands, statuses, capabilities, error codes, and compatibility policy.
acceptance_criteria: A repo-owned protocol registry exists; generated or checked artifacts derive command/status/capability/error-code lists from it; docs describe it as the authoritative contract source

title: Error code contract parity
description: Error-code lists have drifted across schemas, docs, TS types, Go constants, and Rust behavior. There is no single authoritative error contract today.
acceptance_criteria: One canonical error-code set is defined; schema/docs/frontends use the same list; parity tests cover every allowed code and reject unknown ones

title: Epic: Protocol drift guardrails
description: Implement a repo-level system to prevent protocol drift across schemas, docs, core expectations, and the TS/Go/Rust frontends. This epic covers canonical protocol definitions, schema/fixture validation, cross-frontend conformance tests, Rust frontend quality-gate inclusion, Semgrep drift checks, and governance docs.

title: Go malformed JSON response
description: The Go frontend currently drops malformed JSON requests silently in protocol.Handler.Run(). On json.Unmarshal failure it logs and continues without emitting an invalid_request response, which can leave the caller hanging until timeout. The TypeScript frontend returns an explicit invalid_request error for the same case.
acceptance_criteria: Add a failing automated test first that sends malformed JSON to the Go frontend and asserts an invalid_request response is emitted instead of silence; verify the test fails before the fix; implement the fix; confirm Go handler behavior matches the documented protocol contract

title: Quality script entrypoints
description: Add repository-owned quality scripts under scripts/quality/ plus Makefile entrypoints for local use and future CI. The scripts should cover Rust, TypeScript, Go, docs, repo meta checks, tooling inventory, aggregate checks, and pre-completion.
acceptance_criteria: scripts/quality/ contains documented entrypoints for Rust, TS, Go, docs, meta, aggregate, and pre-completion checks; Makefile exposes matching targets; shell syntax validation passes for the new scripts
close_reason: Quality script entrypoints merged to main

title: CI integration docs
description: Document how the repository quality scripts should be used in a future CI environment without assuming a specific CI platform. Include stage layout, strict-mode behavior, and how hooks/agents should call the same scripts.
acceptance_criteria: A CI integration document exists in docs/ explaining script usage for PR, main-branch, and scheduled CI jobs; documentation references scripts/quality/ entrypoints; hook and agent usage is described without duplicating script internals
close_reason: CI integration docs added to main

title: Epic: Process guardrails rollout
description: Roll out repository-native process guardrails for local development, Claude/agent workflows, hooks, and future CI. The repository should expose stable script entrypoints, documentation, analyzer configs, and hook/agent integration so process enforcement relies on executable checks instead of prose alone.
notes: Plan: docs/plans/process-guardrails-rollout.md

title: Concolic report input loss

This causes report rendering to show empty call examples in concolic mode.
acceptance_criteria: Write a regression test first that fails by asserting concolic conversion/report path preserves non-empty inputs for discovered executions; verify the new test fails before code changes; implement fix so conversion maps inputs correctly and test passes; confirm concolic report formatting shows actual argument examples
close_reason: Fixed From<ExploreResult> for ObservationOutput to build new_path_executions from raw_results (which carry inputs) instead of executions (which don't). Added branch-path deduplication to preserve unique-path semantics. Regression test concolic_conversion_preserves_inputs verifies inputs are non-empty.

title: Walkthrough step 10 hangs
description: Walkthrough step 10 (Explore Rust Functions) hangs indefinitely before the per-step timeout was added. Even with the 120s timeout, the step produces no output. The root cause is likely the `&str` deserialization issue (see sibling issue) — the Rust frontend hits a CompilationError on classify_string, and the explore loop may retry or block. The per-step timeout (added in the latest commit) prevents infinite hangs but the underlying Rust frontend issues need fixing. Once the &str and trait-object issues are resolved, this step should complete normally.
notes: Root causes: str-jxap (&str deserialization) and str-e127 (trait object params). Fixing those should resolve the hang.

title: Rust frontend &str deserialization
description: The Rust frontend cannot handle `&str` parameters. When a function takes `&str`, the generated harness tries `serde_json::from_value::<&str>(inputs[0].clone())` which fails because `&str` implements `Deserialize<'a>` for a specific lifetime, not `'static`. This causes CompilationError for classify_string, classify_greeting, parse_config_line, parse_key_value, validate_pattern, and other string-parameter functions. The harness should deserialize to `String` and then borrow, or use `serde_json::from_value::<String>` with an intermediate binding. This blocks walkthrough steps 10 and 11 (Rust explore/scan). See /tmp/shatter-walkthrough-2026.03.10-00.03.45.out for full error output.

title: CLI user output on stderr
description: The CLI writes user-facing output (explore reports, analyze summaries, scan plans, stale/fresh lists, dry-run plans) to stderr via eprint!/eprintln! instead of stdout. This means `make walkthrough > file` captures empty steps — the actual output goes to stderr. ~12 call sites in main.rs need to switch to print!/println!. Affected lines: 1126-1135 (analyze), 1180-1200 (incremental plan), 1238 (explore banner), 1423/1432 (explore reports), 1535 (explore footer), 2001 (scan dry-run plan), 3650 (test prioritization report). Diagnostic/progress messages and errors should remain on stderr.

title: shatter-ts tests reference deleted example files after standalone refactor
description:
## Problem

6 tests fail across 2 test suites (\`handlers.test.ts\` and \`executor.test.ts\`) because they reference \`examples/typescript/src/01-arithmetic.ts\` which no longer exists.

## Root Cause

Commit af3509c ("refactor: separate standalone examples from project examples") moved standalone TS files from \`examples/typescript/src/\` to \`examples/standalone/ts/\` but did not update the test file paths in \`shatter-ts\`.

## Failing Tests

- \`executor.test.ts\`: "reports plausible metrics for instrumented execution" — ENOENT on \`01-arithmetic.ts\`
- \`handlers.test.ts\`: 5 tests that analyze/instrument/execute real functions — all get "error" status because the example file can't be found

## Fix

Update the \`EXAMPLES_DIR\` or file paths in \`handlers.test.ts\` and \`executor.test.ts\` to point at \`examples/standalone/ts/\` instead of \`examples/typescript/src/\`.

title: shatter-ts build fails: react-component.tsx missing JSX config and @types/react
description:
## Problem

\`make test\` fails at the \`build-ts\` step because \`src/__fixtures__/react-component.tsx\` cannot compile:

1. **No \`jsx\` compiler option** in \`tsconfig.json\` — TSX files need \`"jsx": "react-jsx"\` or similar
2. **No \`@types/react\` dependency** — the fixture imports from \`react\` but no type declarations are installed

## Errors

```
src/__fixtures__/react-component.tsx(16,42): error TS2307: Cannot find module 'react' or its corresponding type declarations.
src/__fixtures__/react-component.tsx(28,12): error TS17004: Cannot use JSX unless the '--jsx' flag is provided.
```
(13 errors total)

## Root Cause

Commit 4ce7a8c added the React shim feature (str-sunh) including this fixture but didn't update tsconfig.json or package.json to support TSX compilation.

## Fix Options

1. **Add JSX support**: Add \`"jsx": "react-jsx"\` to tsconfig.json and \`@types/react\` to devDependencies
2. **Exclude fixtures from build**: Add \`src/__fixtures__\` to the \`exclude\` list in tsconfig.json (if fixtures don't need to be compiled)

Option 2 is simpler if the fixture is only used at test time by jest (which has its own ts-jest transform).

title: SymExpr builder parity properties (buildSymExpr vs buildSymExprWithFlow)
description:
The str-ho1d issue explicitly requested parity testing between buildSymExpr() and buildSymExprWithFlow() in shatter-ts instrumentor.ts. This was not delivered — only structural validity tests landed.

Needed: for arbitrary AST-like inputs that both functions handle, verify they produce compatible SymExpr outputs. When buildSymExprWithFlow returns a non-unknown result, buildSymExpr should handle the same node type (and vice versa). This is the primary source of silent concolic degradation — if one function returns unknown for a node the other handles, variables become invisible to the solver.

Approach: generate synthetic AST nodes (BinaryExpression, PropertyAccessExpression, CallExpression, etc.) and run both builders, asserting that neither returns unknown when the other returns a concrete SymExpr.

title: Deep proptest coverage for solver bridge (solve_for_new_path, to_z3_expr, overlay pipeline)
description:
The solver bridge is the most bug-prone code in shatter-core (str-6ayh class: wrong Z3 sorts for params). Current PBT has only 5 shallow infer_sort checks. Needed:

1. solve_for_new_path: arbitrary constraint sets with known-type params → solved values match ParamInfo types. Especially: string params never get Int solutions, nullable params get null or inner-type solutions.
2. to_z3_expr: arbitrary well-typed SymExpr trees → Z3 context accepts without sort mismatch errors. Requires spinning up Z3 context in test.
3. Full solve→overlay pipeline: arbitrary (constraints, base_inputs, param_names) triples → overlay output types match input types, length preserved, solved indices are valid.
4. Negation index properties: for any valid constraint list, negate at each index → either solves or returns unsat (never panics, never produces wrong-type values).
5. String theory integration: arbitrary string constraints (indexOf, contains, startsWith) → solutions are valid strings (not integers).

This is high-value PBT — these are the exact invariants that have caused real bugs.

title: Rust executor generates duplicate main() when source file contains fn main()
description:
## Bug

After fixing the stale binary issue (str-ss79), Rust function execution still fails with a compilation error:
```
error[E0428]: the name `main` is defined multiple times
```

## Root Cause

`generate_harness()` in `shatter-rust/src/executor.rs:173-177` concatenates:
1. The entire instrumented source (which includes `fn main()` from the original file)
2. Its own `fn main() {` harness wrapper

This produces a `main.rs` with two `main()` definitions.

Example source files affected:
- `examples/standalone/rust/01_arithmetic.rs` — has `fn main() { ... }`
- `examples/standalone/rust/02_strings.rs` — likely same
- `examples/standalone/rust/04_errors.rs` — likely same

## Reproduction

```bash
cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml
echo '{...handshake...}
{...instrument file=examples/standalone/rust/01_arithmetic.rs...}
{...execute function=classify_number inputs=[42]...}' | ./shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust
# → compilation_error: the name `main` is defined multiple times
```

## Fix Options

1. **Strip main()**: Before embedding, remove or rename `fn main()` from the instrumented source. Could use syn to parse and filter, or a regex to strip it.
2. **Module wrapping**: Include the instrumented source as a module (`mod user_code { ... }`) and call the target function via `user_code::function_name()`. This avoids name collisions entirely.
3. **Attribute gating**: Add `#[cfg(not(shatter_harness))]` around the original main and compile with `--cfg shatter_harness`.

Option 2 (module wrapping) is the cleanest — it also handles other potential name collisions (not just main).

title: Walkthrough steps 10-11 fail: shatter-rust binary not rebuilt after str-rv0k fix
description:
## Bug

Walkthrough steps 10 (Explore Rust Functions) and 11 (Scan Rust Examples) fail with:
```
execute error (InvalidRequest): execute command requires a file path
```

## Root Cause

Commit 09bba12 (fix for str-rv0k) added last_file fallback to shatter-rust handler.rs, but the binary at `shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust` was never rebuilt. The CLI picks up the stale binary which still has the old code without the fallback.

Verified by:
- `strings shatter-rust/target/debug/shatter-rust | grep 'execute command requires'` shows the short error message (pre-fix)
- Binary timestamp is older than the source commit timestamp
- After `cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml`, the fallback works correctly

## Fix

Add `cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml` to the walkthrough preamble (before Rust steps). A no-op build takes ~0.2s so there's no meaningful cost when the binary is already current. This prevents stale-binary class of bugs permanently.

No file paths, source code, or personal information is collected. To disable: shatter telemetry off (or set SHATTER_TELEMETRY=off)'. Create config file with enabled: true, first_notice_shown: true. Runs before command dispatch, after consent check. If SHATTER_TELEMETRY=off or DO_NOT_TRACK=1 is already set, skip notice entirely and don't create config file.

title: CLI bad-args capture via try_parse_from
description: In shatter-cli/src/main.rs, change Cli::parse() (line 2874) to Cli::try_parse_from(std::env::args_os()). On Err(clap_err): if telemetry enabled, sanitize args with path enrichment metadata, queue bad_cli_args event, then call clap_err.exit(). Map clap::error::ErrorKind to error_kind field: UnknownArgument→unknown_flag, InvalidSubcommand→unknown_subcommand, EmptyValue→missing_value, MissingRequiredArgument→missing_required, ValueValidation→invalid_value, WrongNumberOfValues→wrong_count, Other→other. Must not delay error output — queue is a fast local append. Sanitized args include path_meta probes (exists, kind, parent_exists, parent_writable, depth) for path-like values.

Opt-out with first-run notice, local JSONL queue, no network in Phase 1. PostHog integration deferred to Phase 2. Full design: docs/plans/telemetry-anonymous-usage-bad-args.md
notes: Epic for issues: str-1k1g (core module), str-iw38 (bad-args capture), str-jhks (command events), str-wxf9 (telemetry subcommand), str-2usm (first-run notice), str-rtyl (PostHog Phase 2). Plan: docs/plans/telemetry-anonymous-usage-bad-args.md

title: Rust frontend execute handler requires file field not sent by core, breaking Rust scan
description:
## Bug

Scanning Rust examples fails with:
```
execute error (InvalidRequest): execute command requires a file path
```
for every function (8/8 skipped).

## Root Cause

The core's `Command::Execute` (protocol.rs:68-78) does NOT include a `file` field — it only sends `function`, `inputs`, `mocks`, and optional `setup_context`. The protocol design assumes the frontend remembers the file from the prior Analyze/Instrument step.

The Rust frontend's `handle_execute()` (handler.rs:303-312) requires `req.file` to be `Some` and returns an InvalidRequest error when it's `None`. Since the core never sends `file` in Execute, this always fails.

The TS frontend handles this correctly: it stores `lastAnalyzedFile` from the Analyze/Instrument steps and uses it as a fallback in `resolveFileForExecute()` (handlers.ts:346-358).

## Fix

The Rust frontend should store the file path from Analyze and Instrument requests in handler state (e.g., `self.last_file: Option<String>`), then use that as the fallback in `handle_execute()` when `req.file` is absent. This matches the TS frontend's approach.

title: Go frontend omits constraint field in BranchDecision, causing Rust deserialization failure
description:
## Bug

Running Go examples fails with:
```
[error] Exploration error for ClassifyNumber: frontend error: failed to deserialize response: missing field `constraint` at line 1 column 1785
```

## Root Cause

Go's `BranchDecision` struct in `protocol/types.go:170` has:
```go
Constraint *SymConstraint `json:"constraint,omitempty"`
```

When no symbolic constraint exists (the common case — Go doesn't do symbolic analysis), `Constraint` is `nil`, and `omitempty` causes Go to omit the field from JSON entirely.

Rust's `BranchDecision` in `execution_record.rs:36` has:
```rust
pub constraint: SymConstraint,  // required, no Option, no serde(default)
```

So Rust's serde rejects the response as missing a required field.

## Fix

Two-sided fix for robustness:

1. **Go (sender)**: In `convertBranchPath()` (`handler.go:320-338`), when `ConstraintJSON` is empty, emit an `Unknown` constraint with a descriptive hint instead of `nil`. Remove `omitempty` from the `Constraint` field tag.

2. **Rust (receiver)**: Add `#[serde(default)]` to the `constraint` field in `BranchDecision`, with a `Default` impl on `SymConstraint` that returns `Unknown { hint: String::new() }`. This makes the protocol resilient to any frontend that omits the field.

Both changes together ensure the protocol is robust in both directions.

title: Epic: Assemble genetic search pipeline in orchestrator
description:
Three sophisticated modules exist but are completely disconnected from the exploration loop:

1. genetic_fitness.rs — 4-axis fitness scoring (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty), tested, never called outside tests
2. strategy.rs — InputStrategy trait + MetaStrategy with adaptive selection, feedback, hit-rate scoring, tested, never imported by orchestrator
3. input_gen.rs — type-aware mutate_inputs() and crossover_inputs(), tested, never called by orchestrator (which uses a naive hardcoded fuzz_inputs instead)

The orchestrator uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource enum and a 30-line hardcoded fuzzer. Assembling these into a genetic search pipeline means: (a) fitness-scored worklist ordering so deeper/higher-coverage executions get priority, (b) adaptive strategy selection so budget flows to whichever generation method works best, (c) type-aware mutation and crossover between high-fitness parents. This is the difference between directed exploration and random walking.

Sub-issues: str-yvp4 (depth axis), str-hoyl (fitness→worklist), str-lebv (MetaStrategy integration), plus naive fuzzer replacement.
close_reason: Wired genetic_fitness, strategy, input_gen into orchestrator. Fitness-scored WorklistEntry, FitnessContext sharing, MetaStrategy hooks, type-aware mutation for unknowns. 1664 unit + 5 E2E tests pass, clippy clean. Branch str-oi1d commit 514d1ba

title: Solver declares string params as Int — pass ParamInfo to Z3 variable creation
description:
Root cause of str-omrx. infer_sort() in solver.rs:162 defaults SymExpr::Param to Sort::Int. When a string param appears inside .length (which returns Int), the param gets declared as Int in Z3. Z3 then solves for an integer, overlay_solved_values writes it back as json!(42), and the frontend crashes with 'email.indexOf is not a function'.

Fix: thread &[ParamInfo] into solve_for_new_path → to_z3_expr. When creating a Z3 variable for SymExpr::Param, look up the param name in ParamInfo and use its TypeInfo to select the correct Sort (Str→Sort::Str, Int→Sort::Int, Float→Sort::Real, Bool→Sort::Bool). Fall back to infer_sort only for sub-expressions like object paths that aren't top-level params.

Key files: solver.rs (infer_sort, to_z3_expr, solve_for_new_path), orchestrator.rs (calls solve_for_new_path).

Reproduction test already written: e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only

title: Add ExploreConfig.candidate_inputs field and wire into random explorer input loop
description:
This is the implementation task for str-emw6 (random explorer ignores --inputs).

Changes needed:
1. shatter-core/src/explorer.rs: Add 'candidate_inputs: Vec<Vec<serde_json::Value>>' field to ExploreConfig (line ~48, alongside pool_seeds)
2. shatter-core/src/explorer.rs: Insert candidate_inputs into the input generation priority chain (line ~343) between pool_seeds and custom generators. They should have priority above pool seeds but below literal seeds.
3. shatter-cli/src/main.rs: In the random explorer branch (~line 1132), convert resolved.candidate_inputs to Vec<Vec<Value>> (same pattern as concolic path at lines 1094-1097) and set explore_config.candidate_inputs.
4. Add a test in explorer.rs that verifies candidate inputs are executed before random generation kicks in.

Acceptance: running 'shatter explore 15-email-validator.ts --inputs seeds.json' where seeds.json contains {"args": ["test@example.com"]} must execute that input and discover the 'valid: true' path.

title: Parity enforcement: buildSymExprWithFlow must handle all node types that buildSymExpr handles
description:
instrumentor.ts has two functions that build symbolic expressions: buildSymExprWithFlow() (lines 278-352, used during data flow analysis) and buildSymExpr() (lines 860-951, used during branch condition analysis). They handle different sets of AST node types, causing silent data loss.

buildSymExpr() handles: Identifier, PropertyAccessExpression, CallExpression, PrefixUnaryExpression, BinaryExpression, ParenthesizedExpression, StringLiteral, NumericLiteral, and more.

buildSymExprWithFlow() handles: Identifier, PropertyAccessExpression, PrefixUnaryExpression, BinaryExpression, StringLiteral, NumericLiteral, and more — but NOT CallExpression.

This gap means any variable assigned from a method call becomes unknown in the flow map, and all subsequent references to it in branch conditions also become unknown. This is the root cause of str-sg5d.

Fix approach: Either refactor into a single shared function with a flag for context, or add a test that programmatically verifies both functions handle the same set of node types. The test approach is more robust since the two functions have legitimately different contexts (flow analysis vs. branch rewriting).

Add a unit test in instrumentor.test.ts that instruments a function with 'const x = s.indexOf("@"); if (x === -1) { ... }' and asserts the branch condition is NOT unknown.

title: E2E concolic test for string-heavy function (email validator)
description:
Add an integration test in shatter-core/tests/e2e_concolic.rs that runs the full concolic pipeline on examples/standalone/ts/15-email-validator.ts. This is the primary regression gate for the string ops → Z3 solver path.

The test should:
1. Spawn the real TS frontend
2. Analyze validateEmail to get 19 branches and string-method conditions
3. Instrument the function for symbolic tracking
4. Run concolic exploration with seed inputs including structurally valid emails (e.g. 'test@example.com', '@domain.com', 'user@', '.dot@x.com', 'user+tag@x.com')
5. Assert minimum path coverage — initially modest (e.g. ≥6 distinct paths) given known limitations (str-sg5d, str-omrx), with TODOs to raise the bar as those are fixed

Why this test matters:
- str-sg5d (data flow drops CallExpression) means most branch conditions are unknown → solver can't help
- str-omrx (cold-start problem) means concolic gets stuck without good seeds
- str-emw6 (candidate inputs ignored) means hand-crafted seeds don't reach the random explorer
- This single test would have caught ALL THREE bugs before they accumulated

The test should be tagged with #[ignore] initially if it requires str-sg5d to be fixed first, with a clear comment explaining why and which issue unblocks it. Pattern after existing concolic_classifynumber_discovers_all_branches test.

When str-sg5d and str-omrx are fixed, the threshold should be raised to ≥12 distinct paths (the 12 branches reachable without regex/for-loop analysis).

title: Data flow analysis drops CallExpression assignments to unknown
description: buildSymExprWithFlow() in instrumentor.ts:278-352 has no case for ts.isCallExpression(), so any local variable assigned from a method call (e.g. 'const local = email.slice(0, atIdx)') falls through to {kind: 'unknown'} at line 351. Since visitStatementsForDataFlow() filters out unknowns (line 236), these variables are never stored in the dataFlowMap. All subsequent branch conditions referencing these locals (local.length, domain.startsWith, labels.length, etc.) also become unknown, making them invisible to the Z3 solver. This is the primary reason the email validator gets only 4/20 branches — 13 of 19 branch conditions contain unknown nodes. Fix: add CallExpression handling to buildSymExprWithFlow() that mirrors buildSymExpr()'s call handling (lines 936-948), producing symbolic call nodes for method invocations on tracked expressions.

title: Random explorer ignores --inputs candidate inputs
description: The --inputs CLI flag loads candidate inputs (logged as 'N candidate input(s) from config') but the random explorer path in main.rs:1132 never passes them to explore_function(). Only the concolic orchestrator wires candidate_inputs into its worklist. The ExploreConfig struct in explorer.rs has no field for candidate inputs, and the input generation loop (explorer.rs:343-386) only uses literal seeds, pool seeds, custom generators, and random — never user-provided candidates. Fix: add candidate_inputs field to ExploreConfig, convert and pass them in main.rs, insert them into the input priority chain between pool seeds and generators.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 0f656e7 — user_seeds field added to ExploreConfig, wired in main.rs

title: Wire string method calls to Z3 string theory in solver
description:
The z3 Rust crate (v0.19) fully supports the Seq sort with contains, prefix, suffix, substr, length, at, concat, and indexof operations. Frontends already capture string method calls as SymExpr::Call { name, receiver, args }. The solver rejects all Call expressions at solver.rs line 248 with SolverError::Unsupported.

Implement ~10 match arms in to_z3_expr() mapping Call names to Z3 string operations:
- contains/includes → z3 String::contains
- indexOf/Index → z3 indexof (with -1 sentinel for not-found)
- startsWith/HasPrefix → z3 String::prefix (note: arg order flip)
- endsWith/HasSuffix → z3 String::suffix
- length/len → z3 String::length
- charAt/at → z3 String::at
- slice/substring/substr → z3 String::substr
- concat → z3 String::concat

Each frontend uses different method names for the same operation. Initially hard-code mappings for TS and Go (the two working frontends). This unblocks Z3-guided input generation for string-heavy functions like validateEmail.

This is the prerequisite for build-time table generation (separate issue) but delivers immediate value.

title: Line coverage denominator always 1 in explore output
description: When exploring, line coverage always reports X/1 lines (100%). E.g. 'Line coverage: 7/1 lines (100%)'. The total_lines is computed from end_line - start_line + 1, so if both are 0 or equal, total_lines=1. Need to investigate whether frontends are returning incorrect start_line/end_line, or whether the value isn't propagated correctly through the orchestrator→pipeline path.

title: Rust frontend: implement execute command
description: The Rust frontend's handle_execute() in handler.rs:305 returns 'not yet implemented'. This is the blocker for Rust exploration — without execute, the engine can't run instrumented functions with generated inputs. Needs: compile instrumented source, load and call the target function with provided inputs, capture return value/panic/side effects, report branch coverage via instrumentation hooks. This is the most complex piece — likely requires a subprocess or dylib-loading approach since Rust is compiled.
close_reason: Implemented: executor.rs subprocess pipeline, handle_execute wired up, 155 tests pass

title: Graceful error recovery when frontend crashes on unresolvable imports
description: During scan, files like 11-opaque-types.ts and 12-external-deps.ts crash the TS frontend process (broken pipe / closed stdout) when they try to require external modules like 'pg', 'net', etc. that aren't installed. Instead of the frontend process dying and leaving a broken pipe, it should catch module resolution errors during loadModule/executeInstrumented and return a structured protocol error (e.g. ErrorCode::InternalError with a clear message about the missing module). This would let the scan orchestrator skip that function gracefully and continue with the remaining functions, rather than losing the entire frontend subprocess. Currently causes ~68 errors in the walkthrough, all from scan steps exercising these files.
close_reason: Implemented async execution support, .catch() safety net, and dead frontend respawn

title: Phase 1: Update PrefetchedValues and generation pipeline for recipes/composite IDs
description: In input_gen.rs: PrefetchedValues stores (value, recipe, composite_id) tuples. prefetch_custom_values() captures id+recipe from Generate responses, computes composite IDs. generate_inputs_with_custom() returns generator metadata. Explorer passes metadata through to behavior map.
close_reason: PrefetchedValues now stores GeneratedEntry(value, generator_id, recipe, composite_id). prefetch_custom_values captures full metadata from protocol responses. 895 tests passing, clippy clean.

description: In protocol.rs: Add recipe: Option<Value> to Command::Generate, add id: String and recipe: Option<Value> to ResponseResult::Generate. Add round-trip serde tests. Update protocol/schemas/*.json for recipe and id fields.
close_reason: Protocol types extended: recipe on Command::Generate, generator_id+recipe on ResponseResult::Generate. Schemas updated. 895 tests passing, clippy clean.

description: New module shatter-core/src/canonical_json.rs with: canonicalize_json(Value)->String (sort keys, normalize numbers/unicode), recipe_hash(Value)->String (SHA-256 truncated to 6 hex), composite_id(id, recipe)->String. Uses sha2 crate (already a dep). Property tests for key reordering, whitespace, number normalization.

title: Generator Plugin System: WASM + Custom Build (Epic)

title: Z3 solver timeout
description: Both solve_for_new_path and solve_constraints in solver.rs create Config::new() with no params. Z3 crate 0.19 provides Config::set_timeout_msec(ms) — context-level timeout. When Z3 hits deadline, solver.check() returns SatResult::Unknown with reason 'timeout'; check_and_extract already handles this as SolverError::Unknown. Add solver_timeout_ms: Option<u64> param to both solve functions. Apply cfg.set_timeout_msec(ms) when Some. Add solver_timeout_ms to ExploreConfig (explorer.rs) and thread through orchestrator.rs:396. Add --solver-timeout CLI flag (optional, seconds) to Explore/Scan/Run. Add test: 1ms timeout on satisfiable constraint → either Sat or Unknown (no panic).
notes: Plan: docs/plans/str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md section 4

title: Implement --timeout-total for scan command
description: --timeout-total is parsed in CLI (default 300s) but stored as _timeout_total (unused). Add timeout_total: Option<Duration> to ScanConfig. In parallel_scan, record scan_start = Instant::now() before layer loop. At top of each layer iteration, check elapsed >= total; if exceeded, push remaining functions as skipped with reason 'total scan timeout exceeded' and break. Layer-boundary check preserves partial results. Rename _timeout_total to timeout_total in run_scan. Pass Some(Duration::from_secs(timeout_total)) to ScanConfig (0 = None). Add test: parallel_scan with timeout_total=Duration::ZERO → all functions skipped.
notes: Plan: docs/plans/str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md section 5

title: TS frontend: read SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT and apply vm.runInContext timeout
description: The TS frontend ignores SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT entirely. Add getExecTimeoutMs() helper that reads the env var (default 15s). Apply { timeout: N } to both vm.runInContext calls in executor.ts (lines 86, 397). Add unit tests: default value, env var parsing, invalid value handling, module-level infinite loop aborted by timeout. Export getExecTimeoutMs for testing.
notes: Plan: docs/plans/str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md sections 1-2

title: Epic: Resilience to pathological target code
description: Umbrella for timeout enforcement, memory limits, Z3 solver timeout, and scan timeout-total implementation. Plan: docs/plans/str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md

title: Frontend feature parity matrix documentation
description: There is no documentation that tracks which protocol features each frontend implements. Create a PARITY.md (or a section in PLAN.md) that lists every protocol command and feature with implementation status per frontend (TypeScript, Go, Rust). Columns: Feature | TS | Go | Rust | Notes. Cover at minimum: handshake, analyze, instrument, execute, setup, teardown, generate, shutdown, side-effect capture (console, file, network, env, global mutation, thrown error, global state change), symbolic constraint reporting, literal extraction, perf metrics, mock support, truncation policy. Update this matrix whenever a frontend adds or changes a feature. This serves as the acceptance criteria for frontend completeness.

title: Fix qualified-name bugs in core_sample.rs
description: Three name-mismatch bugs: depth_map lookup uses bare names vs qualified names from topological_layers(), compute_dependency_closure passes bare names to callees_of(), and CLI retain filter uses ambiguous bare names.

description: Add --output <path> flag to explore command. When combined with file-level targets, writes FileSpecBundle JSON to specified path. Files: shatter-cli/src/main.rs

title: Per-file spec output (FileSpecBundle I/O)

title: TS frontend: intra-package module resolution fails (Cannot find module)
description:
The TS frontend uses ts.transpileModule per-file + vm.createContext with the host process's require. Relative imports to sibling modules within the target package fail with 'Cannot find module'. This is the #1 error source — 365 of 366 total errors across all TS holdout targets. Causes complete failure for ts-pattern (0/35 functions) and partial failure for remeda (48/561) and es-toolkit.

Root cause: there is no bundling step. Target files are transpiled individually and executed in a VM sandbox. Relative require() calls fail because module resolution doesn't account for the target project's directory structure.

Design questions that need answering before implementation:
- How does bundling interact with per-function instrumentation?
- Would bundling break the per-function isolation model?
- How to handle tsconfig.json paths/aliases?
- Should we bundle at analysis time, execution time, or both?

Needs design work before implementation.

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the 'Cannot find module' failure for relative imports in the VM sandbox). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
design:
## Root Cause

The TS frontend uses ts.transpileModule per-file + vm.createContext with the **host process's require**. Three distinct bugs:

1. **executeInstrumented** (executor.ts ~line 289): sandbox is missing __filename and __dirname entirely, and uses host require. The vm.runInContext filename is 'instrumented.js' (fake).
2. **loadModule** (executor.ts ~line 69): sets __dirname correctly but still uses the host require — so require('./sibling') resolves against shatter-ts install dir.
3. **loadAndTranspile** (setup-loader.ts ~line 45): same issue as loadModule.

The instrumented code preserves all imports (ts.transpileModule with CommonJS converts them to require() calls), so require('./purry') etc. always fails.

## Fix Design

Use Node.js `module.createRequire(filename)` (stable since Node 12) to create a require anchored to the target file's directory.

### Changes Required

**executor.ts:**
- Add `import { createRequire } from 'node:module'`
- loadModule: replace `require` with `createRequire(absolutePath)` in sandbox
- executeInstrumented: add `sourceFilePath: string` parameter; use `createRequire(sourceFilePath)` + set __filename/__dirname in sandbox; pass sourceFilePath to vm.runInContext filename option; add `fileName: sourceFilePath` to ts.transpileModule options

**handlers.ts:**
- Pass `fileForExec` (already resolved at line ~161) as 5th argument to executeInstrumented

**setup-loader.ts:**
- Add `import { createRequire } from 'node:module'`
- Replace host require with `createRequire(absolutePath)` in loadAndTranspile sandbox

### Test Plan
- Existing fixture `__fixtures__/dependencies.ts` imports from `./deps-helper` — verify it works after fix
- Add integration test: target file importing sibling module in a temp directory outside shatter-ts
- Verify ts-pattern-style scenarios (deep internal imports like './internals/helpers')
close_reason: Fixed with module.createRequire() in executor.ts, handlers.ts, setup-loader.ts

title: Go frontend: omits functions field for files with no function definitions
description:
Go doc-only files (e.g. doc.go with only a package comment) cause 'missing field functions' deserialization error, aborting the entire batch scan — not just the empty file.

Fix: in Go frontend, always emit "functions": [] for files with no functions. Alternatively, add #[serde(default)] to the functions field in the core Rust struct. Frontend fix preferred.

Reproduction: xstrings doc.go — package-only file with no functions.

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the deserialization failure for a file with no function definitions). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
close_reason: Go frontend now always emits functions:[] for empty files; added serde(default) on Rust side

title: Go frontend: select statement not in core BranchType enum
description:
The Go frontend emits BranchType: 'select' for Go select statements (analyzer.go ~line 482), but the core BranchType enum in protocol.rs only has: if, else_if, switch, ternary, logical_and, logical_or, while, for. Deserialization fails with 'unknown variant select', aborting the entire scan.

Fix: either add Select variant to BranchType in shatter-core/src/protocol.rs, or map select→switch in the Go frontend (loses semantic fidelity but avoids core change). Adding the variant is preferred.

Reproduction: any Go file with select{} (e.g. samber/lo channel.go) triggers the error.

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the deserialization failure). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
close_reason: Added Select variant to BranchType enum in protocol.rs with reproduction test

title: Go frontend: missing operator mappings for bitwise ops, shifts, and bit-clear
description:
The Go frontend's tokenToOp() in analyzer.go returns tok.String() for unmapped operators, producing raw tokens (&^, &, |, ^, <<, >>) that fail core deserialization. The core BinOpKind enum already has bitwise_and/bitwise_or/bitwise_xor but the Go frontend doesn't map to them. Additionally, &^ (bit-clear/AND-NOT) has no core variant at all. Bonus bug: unary - maps to 'sub' instead of 'neg' in the analyzer path.

Fix locations:
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go tokenToOp() ~line 642: add cases for token.AND→bitwise_and, token.OR→bitwise_or, token.XOR→bitwise_xor, token.SHL→shl, token.SHR→shr, token.AND_NOT→bit_clear
- shatter-go/instrument/symextract.go tokenToOp() ~line 154: same mappings (currently returns empty string for unknowns)
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go buildUnOp() ~line 531: unary token.SUB should map to 'neg' not 'sub'
- shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs BinOpKind: add BitClear, Shl, Shr variants

Reproduction: any Go file with &^ operator (e.g. go-humanize comma.go) causes 'unknown variant &^' deserialization error, aborting the entire scan.
