**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the deserialization failure). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
close_reason: Added Shl, Shr, BitClear to BinOpKind; fixed tokenToOp in analyzer.go and symextract.go; fixed unary minus to neg

title: Fingerprint comparison and merging
description: Compare fingerprints against existing specs, identify changed functions, and merge exploration results for incremental exploration.

title: Per-function fingerprinting
description: Hash source text, parameter types, branch structure, and store in spec files for incremental exploration tracking.
close_reason: Already fully implemented on main — fingerprint.rs, spec.rs, scan_orchestrator.rs, cache.rs all in place with 11 passing tests

title: Fix protocol inconsistencies across frontends
description: Audit found protocol drift between Rust core and TS/Go frontends: (1) FunctionAnalysis.exported field missing in TS and Go, (2) ErrorInfo.stack is non-nullable in Go but Option<String> in core, (3) ParamInfo field name mismatch (typ vs type) needs verification, (4) Go SideEffect struct missing payload fields (content, body, value, before, after), (5) ParamInfo.type_name optional field missing in Go/TS.

title: Fix failing test: frontend_config_passes_timeout_env_vars
description: The test panics with 'failed to set permissions on go frontend binary: No such file or directory'. The test relies on the embedded Go frontend binary existing at extraction time but the binary may not be present in all test environments. Needs to either mock the extraction or ensure the binary is available.

title: Make-friendly per-file specs with fingerprint-based incremental exploration
description:
## Problem

Shatter's CLI currently bundles discovery, analysis, and exploration into monolithic commands (scan, run) with outputs going to hidden .shatter/cache/ or shatter-report/. This makes it unusable with Make, which needs predictable input→output file relationships with timestamp-based staleness.

The lasting output of Shatter is behavioral specifications — not human reports. Specs are the precise, complete, machine-diffable records of observed behavior. They should be first-class project artifacts, not hidden cache.

## Design

### Two first-class artifacts, per-source-file granularity

1. **Spec files** (FunctionSpec[]) — the lasting, diffable behavioral specification. Versioned in git. Used by spec-diff for regression detection.
2. **Behavior map files** (BehaviorMap[]) — input→output mappings for compositional mocking. Stored separately, may or may not be versioned.

Both are per-source-file: specs/src/math.ts.json contains specs for all functions in math.ts.

### Internal function fingerprinting for performance

When a source file changes, Shatter doesn't blindly re-explore all functions:
1. Run cheap analyze phase → extract function signatures/ASTs
2. Compute per-function fingerprint (hash of source text, parameter types, branch structure)
3. Compare against fingerprints stored in existing output
4. Only re-explore functions with changed fingerprints
5. Merge results: preserve unchanged specs, update changed ones

Fingerprints should compose across the call graph: if B's behavior changes, A (which calls B) needs re-exploration even if A's source is unchanged.

### CLI changes needed

1. **explore --output <path>** — write spec to explicit path (Make target)
2. **explore accepts file-level targets** — shatter explore src/math.ts explores all functions in the file
3. **shatter stale** — lightweight staleness check (exit code 0/1) using fingerprints, for non-Make incremental workflows
4. **explore --clean** — ignore existing specs/fingerprints and force full re-exploration from scratch. Escape hatch for when fingerprints are stale, cache is corrupted, or exploration heuristics have changed.
5. **explore --dry-run** — run analyze + fingerprint comparison, print what would be explored (which functions are stale, which would be skipped), but don't actually run the concolic engine. Useful for understanding what Make would trigger, debugging fingerprint behavior, and CI cost estimation.

### Makefile usage

```makefile
SPECS_DIR := specs

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/math.ts.json: src/math.ts
    shatter explore src/math.ts -o $@

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/utils.ts.json: src/utils.ts
    shatter explore src/utils.ts -o $@
```

### What stays the same

- scan and run remain as batteries-included commands for users not using Make
- .shatter/cache/ remains for internal caching (behavior maps for compositional mocking during exploration)
- export-tests unchanged — reads from specs or cache

### Key files that will need changes

- shatter-core/src/cache.rs — fingerprint storage, staleness checks
- shatter-core/src/spec.rs — per-file spec bundles (array of FunctionSpec)
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — per-file behavior map bundles
- shatter-cli/src/main.rs — --output flag, --clean flag, --dry-run flag, file-level targets, stale command
- shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs — fingerprint-aware incremental exploration
close_reason: All features implemented in commit 1560cd5: --output, --clean, --dry-run, stale command, fingerprinting, incremental merge. Only str-w8d (call-graph fingerprints) remains.

title: Date/DateTime/Duration support end-to-end
description:
Full support for temporal types: generators, analyzer recognition, reconstruction, and integration test.

## What
1. Core generators (input_gen.rs): generate_date and generate_duration with boundary-biased values
   - Date boundaries: epoch 0, negative epoch, Y2K38 (2038-01-19), 2024-01-01, month/year boundaries, far future (9999-12-31), far past (0001-01-01)
   - Duration boundaries: 0, negative, 1ms, 1s, 1min, 1hr, 1day, MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
   - Wire format: {"__complex_type": "date", "value": <epoch_ms>}, {"__complex_type": "duration", "ms": <millis>}

2. TS analyzer (shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts): Recognize Date type in convertType/convertObjectType by checking type symbol name
3. TS reconstruction (new shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts): reconstructValue() infrastructure + Date case (new Date(epoch_ms))
4. Go analyzer (shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go): Recognize time.Time and time.Duration
5. Go reconstruction (new shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go): time.UnixMilli(ms) and time.Duration(ms)*time.Millisecond

6. Integration test: isWeekend(date: Date): boolean — function with two paths (weekday/weekend), verify solver generates dates hitting both paths

## Solver
ConstValue::Complex { kind: Date, repr: Int(epoch_ms) } — solver unwraps to integer comparison. Already handled by the generic Complex unwrap from str-t8b.

## Priority
Highest real-world impact — Date appears in nearly every business application.

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts (new)
- shatter-ts/src/executor.ts (call reconstructValue before execution)
- shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go
- shatter-go/reconstruct/reconstruct.go (new)
- examples/typescript/src/ (isWeekend test function)
close_reason: Date/DateTime/Duration generators added to input_gen.rs with boundary-biased values. TS analyzer recognizes Date, RegExp, URL, Error, Buffer, BigInt, Symbol via complexKindFromSymbol(). Go analyzer recognizes time.Time, time.Duration, url.URL, regexp.Regexp, net.IP, big.Int/Rat/Float via complexKindFromNamed(). reconstruct.ts and reconstruct.go created with full value reconstruction. Both frontends declare complex_type capabilities in handshake. 433 core + 142 TS + all Go tests pass. Clippy and go vet clean.

title: Wire invariant detection into pipeline
description: The invariants.rs module exists but is not wired into the exploration pipeline. Implement Daikon-style invariant detection: after collecting execution records for a function, derive candidate properties (e.g. 'for all x > 100, shipping is free', 'return value is always non-negative when input is positive'). Use proptest to validate candidate invariants against the observed execution set. Properties should be classified as: (1) proven (Z3-confirmed necessary and sufficient), (2) observed (held for all samples, not formally verified), (3) likely (held for >95% of samples). Output feeds into the specification format.
close_reason: Wired invariant detection into pipeline: ClassifiedInvariant struct, detect_classified_invariants, records_from_raw_results, format_invariant_label, build_spec_with_invariants, --invariants CLI flag, spec markdown/JSON output, 16 new tests, walkthrough step 25

title: Behavioral equivalence classes for path grouping
description: Replace return-value-based path deduplication with branch-path-based behavioral equivalence classes. Executions that follow the same branch sequence belong to the same class, regardless of output value. Within each class: pick the simplest concrete example as the canonical representative, derive common preconditions from all inputs in the class. This fixes the fundamental issue in str-798 (every distinct return value treated as unique path) at the conceptual level and is prerequisite for meaningful specification generation.

title: Design specification output format
description: Define the concrete output artifact for behavior specifications. Each function gets a spec with: (1) behavioral equivalence classes grouped by branch path, (2) preconditions for each class derived from constraints, (3) postconditions (return value pattern, side effects), (4) at least one concrete example per class, (5) distinction between proven properties (Z3-confirmed) and observed properties (seen in all samples but not formally verified). The format must be both human-readable (markdown) and machine-readable (JSON). This is the core deliverable users interact with — build backward from this.

title: E2E concolic test on real TS function
description: Add an integration test that runs the FULL concolic pipeline on a real TypeScript example function (e.g. classifyNumber): analyze → instrument → explore with concolic orchestrator → Z3 solve → verify that Z3 discovers a branch that random alone cannot. This is the single most important validation that the core value proposition works end-to-end. Must use the real TS frontend subprocess, not a mock.

title: path_hash fallback treats every distinct return value as a unique path
description: When branch_path is empty, path_hash() in explorer.rs falls back to hashing the serialized return value. For numeric functions like safeDivide, every different input pair produces a different float result, so 100 iterations yield 68 'unique paths' through a 4-path function. Fix: use lines_executed as the fallback path signature instead of return value. All executions through the same code path hit the same lines regardless of input values.

title: Explorer should instrument functions before executing
description: explore_function() in explorer.rs sends Analyze then immediately loops Execute commands, but never sends an Instrument command. Without instrumentation, the TS frontend (and Go frontend) return empty branch_path arrays, so the explorer never gets branch decision data. Fix: after receiving FunctionAnalysis, send an Instrument command and store the result before entering the execution loop.

title: Single-command repo exploration: shatter run <repo-url>
description: Add a top-level CLI command that takes a repo URL (or local path), clones it if needed, discovers all supported files (TS, Go), runs the full pipeline (analyze → explore → report), and outputs a specification report. This is the 'just give me a URL' UX goal. Steps: clone/validate repo, discover files by language, batch analyze all files, build call graph, run concolic exploration (respecting dependency order), generate markdown specs, output combined report. Should support --output-dir for writing per-function spec files.

title: Behavior map accumulation and composition
description: After exploring a function, distill its results into a behavior map: a mapping from input patterns to output values and side effects. When exploring a caller, inject these behavior maps as mocks for the callee — so the caller is tested with realistic (discovered) behavior rather than arbitrary stubs. Store behavior maps in .shatter-cache/ for reuse across runs.

title: Scan orchestrator: drive exploration in dependency order
description: Build the scan orchestrator in shatter-core that: (1) takes the topologically-sorted exploration plan, (2) spawns N frontend subprocesses (multi-process parallelism, default=CPU count), (3) assigns functions to workers in dependency order, (4) collects results and feeds behavior maps forward to the next layer, (5) handles timeouts and errors per-function without aborting the whole scan. CLI flag: --parallelism N and --timeout-per-fn SECONDS (default 30).

title: Scan orchestrator: drive exploration in dependency order
close_reason: Implemented scan orchestrator module, CLI command, and walkthrough step. All 157 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Implement symbolic instrumentation in Go frontend
description: Implement code instrumentation in shatter-go that rewrites Go source to inject branch recording callbacks and symbolic constraint capture. Uses go/ast rewriting. The instrumented code must record branch decisions and path constraints, and be compilable+executable. Parallel to the TS instrumentation work.
close_reason: Instrumentation fully implemented: AST transformation (if/else/switch/for/range), symbolic constraint extraction, runtime recorder, executor with harness generation, and protocol integration. All 29 instrument tests + protocol tests pass. go vet clean.

title: Implement symbolic instrumentation in TypeScript frontend
description: Implement code instrumentation in shatter-ts that rewrites the target function to inject __branch() callbacks at every branch point and __sym() wrappers around symbolic values. The instrumented code must: (1) record which branches are taken with their conditions, (2) capture symbolic path constraints as SymExpr trees, (3) be executable via Node.js worker thread. This connects the TS frontend to the concolic loop.
close_reason: Implemented symbolic instrumentation: instrumentor wraps branch conditions with __shatter_branch() callbacks capturing SymExpr constraints, executor collects BranchDecision records during instrumented execution, handlers wire up instrument/execute pipeline. Fixed TS protocol types to match Rust serde format. 92 tests passing.

title: Complete Z3 solver constraint translation
description: The solver (shatter-core/src/solver.rs) has Z3 infrastructure but incomplete constraint-to-Z3 AST conversion. Complete translation for all SymExpr variants: BinOp (arithmetic, comparison, logical), UnOp, Param references, Const values, and string constraints. Add tests with known-solvable constraints from the example functions. This unblocks the concolic loop.

title: Extract branches from Go AST in analyzer
description: The Go analyzer (shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go) needs to extract branches from if/else, switch/case, and select statements using go/ast. Each branch needs: id, line number, condition expression as string. Parallel to the TS branch extraction work.

title: Extract branches from TypeScript AST in analyzer
description: The TS analyzer (shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts) currently returns an empty branches array. Implement branch extraction from if/else, switch/case, ternary, and logical &&/|| expressions using the TypeScript Compiler API. Each branch needs: id, line number, condition expression as string. This is a prerequisite for concolic execution — without branch info, the solver has nothing to target.

title: Codebase scan mode: explore all functions in a project
description: A shatter scan <directory> command that discovers all functions in a codebase, orders them by dependency depth, and explores them bottom-up using concolic execution. Results from leaf functions become behavior maps used to mock those functions when testing callers. Supports both TS and Go frontends, multi-process parallelism, sensible auto-mocking defaults, and machine-readable output for agent consumption.

title: Core: concolic execution loop with Z3
description: Implement the full concolic loop in orchestrator.rs: execute a function with inputs, collect branch constraints, negate each branch constraint and solve with Z3, add solutions to the worklist, repeat. Use rayon to parallelize Z3 solver calls across branches. Implement priority queue ordering (Z3-solved > fuzzed). Include integration test with a simple multi-branch function where random inputs alone cannot find all paths but Z3 can.
close_reason: Implemented orchestrator.rs with concolic execution loop, worklist priority queue, Z3 solver integration, and fuzzing fallback. 23 unit tests including integration test verifying Z3 finds x=42 for exact equality branch.

title: TS frontend: symbolic constraint extraction from branches
description: Extend instrumentor.ts to emit symbolic constraints alongside line recording. For each branch condition (if, switch, ternary, &&, ||), analyze the AST to determine if the condition references function parameters. If so, emit a SymExpr representation per the protocol schema. Implement lightweight data flow tracking: when a local variable is assigned from a parameter expression, track the symbolic relationship. Fall back to Unknown for complex flows. Include tests with functions that have parameter-dependent branches.

title: End-to-end: shatter explore with random inputs (no Z3)
description: Wire the Rust core to the TS frontend for end-to-end exploration. The core sends analyze to get function metadata, generates random inputs using proptest based on the TypeInfo, sends execute requests, collects ExecutionRecords, and prints an exploration log showing discovered execution paths. No symbolic solving yet — just random/type-aware input generation. This is the first externally demonstrable milestone.
close_reason: Implemented end-to-end explore command with random input generation, TS execution, and path discovery

title: Create example test suite for validation
description: Create examples/ directory with TypeScript functions of increasing complexity for validating shatter capabilities: (1) pure arithmetic with branches, (2) string manipulation with conditionals, (3) object parameter with field access in conditions, (4) function with error throwing, (5) function with enum/union parameter. Each function should have known branches that serve as ground truth for testing shatter coverage.
close_reason: Created 5 example files with documented branches: arithmetic, strings, objects, errors, unions

title: TS frontend: execute instrumented code in worker
description: Implement executor.ts: take instrumented code and a set of input values (as JSON), execute the code in a worker thread, capture the return value (or error), lines executed, and basic performance metrics (wall time). Respond to the execute protocol command. Return results conforming to the ExecuteResponse JSON schema. Include tests that execute a simple instrumented function with known inputs.
close_reason: Implemented executor.ts with VM-based sandboxed execution, timeout support, line tracing, and performance metrics. Wired execute and instrument handlers. All 71 TS tests pass.

title: TS frontend: basic code instrumentation (line-level)
description: Implement instrumentor.ts: use the TS Compiler API to rewrite a target function, inserting __record(lineNumber) calls at each statement and branch point. This is the v1-style instrumentation — no symbolic constraints yet. The instrumented code should be compilable and executable. Include tests that instrument a sample function and verify the recorded lines match expectations.
close_reason: Implemented instrumentor.ts with TS Compiler API. Inserts __shatter_record(line) at each statement and branch point. 10 tests covering linear code, if/else, else-if, loops, switch, arrow functions.

title: TS frontend: analyze function types and signatures
description: Implement analyzer.ts: given a file path and function name, use the TypeScript Compiler API to extract parameter names, types (as TypeInfo per the protocol schema), return type, and source location. Handle primitive types, arrays, objects, unions, enums, and nullable types. Respond to the analyze protocol command. Include tests with sample .ts files covering various type signatures.
close_reason: Implemented analyzer.ts with TypeScript Compiler API to extract function signatures. Handles primitives, arrays, objects, unions, nullable/optional types, and arrow functions. Wired into handlers.ts analyze command with file_not_found and function_not_found error handling. 45 tests passing.

title: Initialize TypeScript frontend project
description: Create the shatter-ts/ directory with package.json, tsconfig.json (strict mode), and a main.ts entry point. The entry point reads newline-delimited JSON protocol messages from stdin and writes responses to stdout. Implement the handshake and ping commands per the protocol spec. Validate incoming messages against the JSON schemas from the protocol task. Include jest config and a basic test. Wire up npm test and npm run build.
close_reason: TypeScript frontend initialized with protocol handlers, strict TS config, jest tests (20 passing), and NDJSON stdio entry point

title: Implement basic CLI with clap
description: Implement the shatter explore subcommand in shatter-cli. Parse <file>:<function> target syntax. Auto-detect language from file extension (.ts -> typescript, .go -> go). Wire up to the frontend subprocess manager (spawn the appropriate frontend). Add --max-iterations and --timeout flags. For now, just print the exploration log to stdout.
close_reason: Implemented explore subcommand with target parsing, language auto-detection, frontend spawning, --max-iterations and --timeout flags. 14 unit tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Implement frontend subprocess manager
description: Implement frontend.rs: spawn a frontend process, send JSON messages via stdin, read JSON responses from stdout. Handle process lifecycle (start, health check, shutdown). Support configurable frontend binary paths. Use tokio for async I/O. Include tests using the no-op reference frontend from the protocol task.
close_reason: Implemented frontend.rs with FrontendConfig, Frontend struct, spawn/handshake/send/shutdown lifecycle, FrontendError types, and 16 tests against noop reference frontend. All 88 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Define frontend protocol as JSON Schema with documentation
description: Define the JSON protocol for communication between the Rust core and language frontends. Create formal JSON Schema files (one per message type) under protocol/schemas/. Implement matching Rust types for all protocol messages: AnalyzeRequest, AnalyzeResponse, ExecuteRequest, ExecuteResponse, InstrumentRequest, InstrumentResponse, MockConfig, plus error responses. Include a protocol version field in every message. Write serde round-trip tests for every message type. Write PROTOCOL.md documenting: the wire format (newline-delimited JSON over stdio), handshake sequence, every message type with examples, error handling conventions, and versioning/compatibility rules. Include a small reference implementation of a no-op frontend that responds to all commands with stubs for use in testing and as a guide for frontend authors.
close_reason: Implemented protocol types, JSON schemas, PROTOCOL.md, and no-op reference frontend

title: Integrate Z3 via the z3 crate
description: Add the z3 crate dependency. Implement solver.rs with: to_z3_expr() to convert SymExpr to Z3 AST nodes, solve_for_new_path() to negate a branch constraint and find a satisfying assignment, extract_concrete_values() to read values from a Z3 model. Support Int, Real, String, Bool sorts. Include unit tests with known-satisfiable and known-unsatisfiable constraint sets.
close_reason: Implemented solver.rs with Z3 integration: to_z3_expr(), solve_for_new_path(), solve_constraints(), extract_concrete_values(). Supports Int, Real, String, Bool sorts. 16 unit tests covering sat/unsat, path negation, multi-variable, arithmetic, and all sort types.

title: Define SymExpr and core data types in Rust
description: Implement the core type definitions in shatter-core: SymExpr enum (Param, Const, BinOp, UnOp, Call, Unknown), ConstValue, BinOpKind, UnOpKind, TypeInfo, ParamInfo, ExecutionRecord, BranchDecision, SymConstraint. All types derive Serialize/Deserialize. Include unit tests verifying JSON round-trip for each type.
close_reason: All types defined with serde derives, 28 unit tests pass for JSON round-trip of every variant

title: Initialize Rust workspace with cargo
description: Create the Cargo workspace with two crates: shatter-core (library) and shatter-cli (binary). Set up basic dependencies: serde, serde_json, clap, thiserror. Add a placeholder main.rs that parses CLI args with clap and prints help. Add CI-ready cargo test and cargo clippy commands. Verify the binary builds and runs.
close_reason: Workspace initialized: cargo build, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings all pass. ./target/debug/shatter --help prints usage.

title: Epic: Concolic execution for TypeScript
description: Add symbolic constraint extraction to the TS frontend and Z3-based constraint solving to the core. This is the key innovation over v1 — directed input generation instead of brute-force guessing.
close_reason: all substantive children landed; deferred stretch goals detached to standalone issues

title: Epic: TypeScript frontend (basic)
description: Get a working TypeScript frontend that can analyze functions, instrument code, execute tests, and communicate with the Rust core via the JSON protocol. Start simple: line-level instrumentation, type-aware value generation, no symbolic constraints yet.

title: Epic: Rust core scaffold
description: Set up the Rust workspace, core data types, Z3 integration, frontend subprocess protocol, and CLI skeleton. Foundation for everything else.
close_reason: all children landed; epic is a completed container

title: Rust clippy baseline failures
description:
`shatter-rust` currently fails the repository's warning-as-error clippy gate before unrelated feature work can complete pre-completion.

## Smallest Reproduction

From a Shatter checkout, run:

```bash
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/shatter-target-clippy CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 \
SHATTER_RUNTIME_PATH=$PWD/shatter-rust-runtime \
cargo clippy --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml --all-targets -- -D warnings
```

Observed failures:

- `shatter-rust/tests/codegen_parity.rs:48`: `clippy::unnecessary_to_owned` reports unnecessary `.copied()` in `for (name, values) in cases.iter().copied()`.
- `shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs:1145`: `clippy::approx_constant` reports literal `3.14` in a round-trip test and suggests using the standard PI constant.

## Desired Behavior

The clippy command above should pass without weakening lint settings or changing production behavior.

## Scope

In scope:

- Make the smallest mechanical source/test changes needed to satisfy the two reported clippy diagnostics.
- Preserve the intent of the existing tests.
- Verify the focused clippy command passes.

Out of scope:

- Broad rustfmt cleanup.
- Refactoring unrelated protocol or codegen behavior.
- Changing lint configuration or suppressing these lints without a clear reason.

## Acceptance Checks

- `cargo clippy --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes with `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` set outside the repo.
- Existing `shatter-rust` tests still pass or remain unchanged in behavior.

title: Axum path defaults shallow
description:
Title: Axum path defaults shallow

Type: bug
Priority: 2
Labels: rust,axum,input-generation,coverage,tests

## Symptom

The recent Axum path route fix generates current Axum brace-style route
patterns and tuple path segment counts, but coverage is still mostly
string-level. The implementation also falls back to placeholder strings for
several common parseable path parameter shapes, which can still cause Axum to
reject the request before handler entry.

A read-only review of the Rust coverage fix flagged two concrete gaps:

- Tuple path support has a string-generation unit test but no execution-level
  regression through `execute_axum_handler`.
- Path defaults handle direct numeric and UUID-ish types but fall back to values
  like `p0` for aliases, newtypes, struct path params, and common parseable
  primitives such as `bool`.

## Smallest Known Evidence

Current affected area:

```text
shatter-rust/src/executor.rs
```

Relevant current tests include:

```text
generate_axum_harness_with_path_extractor_uses_path_route
generate_axum_harness_with_tuple_path_uses_matching_route_segments
```

Those tests assert generated source text such as `/test/{p0}/{p1}`, but they
do not execute a tuple-path Axum handler end to end.

## Current Code Facts

- Axum route generation now uses brace syntax instead of legacy `:param`
  syntax.
- Tuple `Path<(Uuid, Uuid)>` route patterns now generate multiple segments.
- Default path values are selected by helper logic in
  `shatter-rust/src/executor.rs`; unknown shapes fall back to placeholder
  strings.

## Desired Behavior

Supported Axum path extractor shapes should have parseable default path values
and execution-level tests that prove requests enter the handler. Unsupported or
ambiguous path shapes should fail with a clear unsupported/skip reason instead
of silently producing unparseable URL segments.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add an execution-level Rust frontend test for an Axum tuple path handler,
  preferably `Path<(uuid::Uuid, uuid::Uuid)>`, proving the generated request
  reaches handler code.
- Extend path default generation or classification for at least one currently
  shallow common shape such as `bool`, type aliases, or simple newtype path
  parameters.
- Preserve existing brace-style route generation and existing path extractor
  behavior.

## Scope

In scope: Rust Axum path route/default generation and focused execution tests.

Out of scope: JSON body generation, DB-backed state replay, or broad Pickpackit
corpus validation.
acceptance_criteria: Execution-level tuple Path handler test reaches handler code; one shallow path default shape is improved or classified; brace route behavior is preserved.

title: Shatter target manifest and reports
description:
## Problem

Kapow currently maintains a repository-local wrapper around Shatter to do broad source-set work that looks like it should be a Shatter-owned capability. The wrapper discovers source files, applies include/exclude policy, runs per-file `shatter explore`, writes per-file status rows, writes a markdown rollup, and uses ad hoc policy-hash/staleness checks. This creates duplicated target-selection and reporting behavior outside Shatter.

Shatter already has several adjacent primitives, but they do not yet replace the Kapow wrapper cleanly:

- `shatter.config.json` supports scan-global `include` and `exclude` glob patterns.
- `shatter explore` and `properties` accept quoted glob path targets.
- `shatter scan` supports `--include`, `--exclude`, and `--dry-run` planning.
- Shatter has run manifest/status export concepts for broad runs.

The missing product is a first-class way to ask Shatter: "Given this policy, what targets/files are selected, what files were excluded or unsupported, what likely source files are outside policy, and where are the per-file/per-target artifacts and outcomes?"

## Current Code Facts

- `/home/ketan/project/shatter/README.md` documents `shatter.config.json` as the scan-global configuration file. It includes `include`, `exclude`, `language`, `output`, cache, and resource-limit fields.
- `/home/ketan/project/shatter/README.md` and `/home/ketan/project/shatter/QUICKSTART.md` document quoted glob path support for `shatter explore` and `properties`, and `shatter scan --include ... --exclude ...` for repository-wide discovery.
- `/home/ketan/project/shatter/SPEC.md` documents scan checkpoints whose scan ID is derived from the sorted source file set, and scan resume metadata including `config_hash`.
- `/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-core/src/run_manifest.rs` defines `RunManifest`, which captures repo/cwd/git metadata, a scope/config hash, and per-source snapshots including size, mtime, SHA-256 content hash, and line count. It also defines end-of-run diffs for added, removed, and changed files.
- `/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-core/src/status_export.rs` defines `run-status.json` and `run-status.tsv` exports with stable per-run, per-file, and per-target status rows.
- `/home/ketan/project/kapow/scripts/shatter-source-set.sh` currently duplicates broad-run behavior by writing `shatter-artifacts/source-code-status.tsv`, `shatter-artifacts/source-code.md`, and one markdown/spec/log artifact per eligible source file.

## Desired Behavior

Add a Shatter-owned target/reporting feature that can replace Kapow's wrapper-level discovery/reporting logic.

The feature should provide:

1. A command or mode that resolves source files from include/exclude policy without executing exploration.
2. Machine-readable output listing selected files, excluded files when explainable, unsupported files/languages, and candidate files that may deserve inclusion.
3. Broad-run output that includes per-file and per-target status rows, artifact links, config/source identity, and a human-readable markdown summary.
4. A stable source-set/config identity based on resolved selected files and relevant execution config, not a raw wrapper YAML hash.
5. Clear semantics for stale-source detection: source changes during a run should be reported by Shatter's manifest/status output, not by repository wrappers.

## Acceptance Checks

- A maintainer can run a Shatter command against a repository and get a dry-run target manifest from include/exclude policy without exploring functions.
- The manifest is machine-readable and includes enough information for automation to distinguish selected, excluded, unsupported, and candidate/uncovered source files.
- A broad Shatter run emits a stable machine-readable status artifact and a markdown summary that make Kapow's `source-code-status.tsv` and `source-code.md` unnecessary.
- The run identity/staleness model uses Shatter's resolved source manifest and relevant config, avoiding raw wrapper-file hashes.
- Documentation explains how downstream repos should use the native feature instead of writing their own target-set wrappers.

## In Scope

- Shatter CLI/API design for target listing or target suggestion.
- Shatter-native manifest/status/report fields needed by downstream automation.
- Documentation and tests for include/exclude resolution, unsupported language reporting, and stale-source behavior.

## Out of Scope

- Kapow-specific Makefile cleanup.
- Rewriting Kapow's wrapper in the Shatter repo.
- Changing Shatter's exploration algorithms except where needed to surface existing status information.

## Size / Risk

This is likely medium-sized. Shatter has much of the underlying machinery, but the public CLI shape and reporting contract need design care so downstream repos can rely on it.

title: Failed functions miss error metadata
description:
Title: Failed functions miss error metadata
Type: bug
Priority: P2
Labels: scan,json,reporting

## Problem

Mixed scan JSON records failed functions, but the per-function failure entries are not self-contained. Important diagnostic fields are `null`, even though `failed_details` contains the actual failure reason.

This makes downstream consumers parse multiple report sections and prevents simple grouping by language, status, error type, or failed stage.

## Evidence

Observed with a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `949f5fe5f2d1` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

Repro command:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed 20260521 \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --request-timeout 20 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --progress \
  --format json \
  -o /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-mixed-scan.json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

The final summary reported:

```json
{
  "attempted_functions": 24,
  "completed_functions": 22,
  "failed_functions": 2
}
```

The failed entries had mostly null metadata:

```json
{
  "function_name": "pickpackit_catalog",
  "file_path": "/home/ketan/project/pickpackit/api/src/catalog.rs",
  "language": null,
  "status": null,
  "error_type": null,
  "error_message": null,
  "failed_at": null
}
```

But `failed_details` had actionable reasons:

```json
[
  {
    "function_name": "pickpackit_catalog",
    "qualified_id": "/home/ketan/project/pickpackit/api/src/catalog.rs::pickpackit_catalog",
    "reason": "error: exploration error: frontend error: request timed out after 20s"
  },
  {
    "function_name": "run_devserver",
    "qualified_id": "/home/ketan/project/pickpackit/api/src/server.rs::run_devserver",
    "reason": "timed out after 20s"
  }
]
```

## Desired Behavior

Every failed function record in scan JSON should include enough metadata to be useful on its own:

- `language`
- `status`
- `error_type` or category
- `error_message`
- failed stage, if known

`failed_details` may remain as a summary list, but it should not be the only place where the reason exists.

## Acceptance Checks

- Add or reuse a scan fixture with one failed Rust target.
- Run `shatter scan --format json`.
- Assert the failed function entry includes non-null `language`, `status`, `error_type`, and `error_message`.
- Assert `failed_details` and per-function failure metadata agree on the failure reason.
- Preserve existing completed-function JSON shape.

## In Scope

- JSON report population for failed scan targets.
- Error category/message propagation from frontend/orchestrator failures into per-function records.

## Out of Scope

- Changing terminal output formatting.
- Fixing the underlying Rust timeout itself.

title: Go all private HTTP adapter parity
description:
Type: bug
Priority: 2
Labels: go,frontend,http,scan

## Symptom
`--all` exposes private Go functions, but private HTTP helpers with `http.ResponseWriter` still appear as unexecutable parameter skips instead of benefiting from the net/http adapter path.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --all --dry-run .
```

## Evidence
Dry-run output reports:

```text
Including 195 unexported function(s) in scan scope (--all set)
Skipped 66 function(s) (unexecutable parameter types)
272 function(s) across 46 file(s), 7 layer(s)
```

The skipped set includes private HTTP helpers and functions involving `http.ResponseWriter`.

Current code fact: `shatter-go/protocol/nethttp_recognizer.go` recognizes exact `func(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request)` signatures as `go/http-handler`, but partial/private helper shapes such as `func(w http.ResponseWriter)` are only hints or remain unexecutable.

## Diagnosis
Private discovery works, but HTTP adapter eligibility and skip filtering are not applied consistently to private functions and partial handler helpers exposed by `--all`.

## In Scope
Make private functions discovered by `--all` use the same HTTP adapter recognition and skip behavior as exported functions.

## Out of Scope
Making every private helper executable, and adapting non-handler `ResponseWriter` utilities that do not have enough request context to run under `go/http-handler`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A private `func handle(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request)` is included in `--all --dry-run` as adapter-owned rather than skipped for `http.ResponseWriter`.
- A private exact net/http handler executes through `go/http-handler` when scanned with `--all`.
- A private partial helper such as `func write(w http.ResponseWriter)` remains skipped with a clear reason explaining that it is not an exact handler signature.
- Regression covers private exportedness separately from handler signature matching.

title: Go imported interface implementations
description:
Type: feature
Priority: 2
Labels: go,frontend,planner

## Symptom
Zolem provider constructors take imported interface types such as `response.Generator`, and Shatter does not use obvious constructors from the interface's defining package.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
- `internal/provider/openai/handler.go::NewHandler` has parameter `generator response.Generator`.
- `internal/response/generator.go` defines `type Generator interface`.
- `internal/response/faker.go` defines `func NewFakerGenerator() *FakerGenerator`.
- `internal/response/lorem.go` defines `func NewLoremGenerator() *LoremGenerator`.
- Scan inputs for provider `NewHandler` still include raw JSON values for `generator`, producing unmarshal errors.

## Diagnosis
The interface implementation planner covers discovered implementors, but Zolem shows a common case where the consumer package imports an interface whose concrete constructors live in the interface's package. Those imported constructors are not being selected for the provider constructor argument.

## In Scope
Discover and plan imported-package implementations for interface params when the interface package is already imported by the target package and the implementation has a parameterless constructor.

## Out of Scope
Searching the whole module for arbitrary implementors or choosing among implementations based on product semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- For a target param typed as an imported interface, Shatter can select a parameterless constructor from the interface's defining package.
- The selected plan is encoded as a runtime value plan and consumed by wrapper generation.
- Ambiguous multiple candidates are ranked deterministically by existing interface implementation ranking rules.
- Regression includes a consumer package that imports an interface whose defining package also provides a concrete implementation constructor such as `NewMemoryStore()`.

---

title: Go unresolved interface params use raw JSON
description:
Type: bug
Priority: 2
Labels: go,frontend,executability,input-gen

## Symptom
When a Go interface-typed parameter has no selected value plan for a candidate, Shatter still tries generated raw JSON scalar values, which cannot unmarshal into an interface type.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
Examples from `shatter-report/updated-scan.md`:

```text
NewMatcher: param selector: json: cannot unmarshal number/string/bool into Go value of type fixture.Selector
NewHandler: param generator: json: cannot unmarshal bool/string/number into Go value of type response.Generator
```

Current code facts:
- `shatter-go/planner/interface_impl.go` can plan interface params when concrete implementors and constructors are discovered.
- If no plan is available for a specific interface argument, raw generated JSON is still reaching wrapper decoding.

## Diagnosis
Interface params should be resolved through a value plan or classified unsupported. Raw JSON scalars are never valid concrete implementations of a Go interface and produce noisy setup failures.

## In Scope
Prevent raw JSON fallback candidates from being used for unresolved Go interface params.

## Out of Scope
Automatically discovering or synthesizing every possible interface implementation. Imported-package implementation discovery can be handled separately.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Interface-typed params with no selected value plan are marked unsupported before execution.
- Interface-typed params with a selected value plan continue to execute through that plan.
- Raw JSON scalar/object candidates are not sent directly into interface params.
- Regression includes an interface param with no implementation plan.

---

title: Go nested duration inputs invalid
description:
Type: bug
Priority: 2
Labels: go,frontend,input-gen

## Symptom
Top-level `time.Duration` handling appears improved, but nested duration fields are still generated in a JSON shape that the Go wrapper cannot unmarshal.

## Reproduction
From `~/project/zolem`:

```bash
shatter scan --progress --no-cache --no-seeds \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.md \
  -o shatter-report/updated-scan.json .
```

## Evidence
`internal/fixture/watcher.go::StartWatcher` fails with:

```text
param cfg: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field WatcherConfig.Debounce of type time.Duration
```

The generated input includes:

```json
{"Debounce":{"__complex_type":"duration","ms":-1}}
```

Current Zolem code fact: `WatcherConfig` has field `Debounce time.Duration`.

## Diagnosis
The duration special case is not applied consistently to struct fields. A nested `time.Duration` field still receives the complex-object representation while wrapper decoding expects the numeric representation used for direct duration params.

## In Scope
Apply Go `time.Duration` input representation consistently for nested struct fields.

## Out of Scope
Redesigning all time-like input generation or adding semantic duration distributions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Struct fields of type `time.Duration` use the same JSON representation as top-level duration params.
- Regression fixture covers `func F(cfg struct{ Delay time.Duration })`.
- Zolem `StartWatcher` no longer fails with `cannot unmarshal object ... time.Duration`.

---

title: Mixed scan progress totals reset by language
description:
Title: Mixed scan progress totals reset by language
Type: bug
Priority: P2
Labels: scan,progress,ux

## Problem

Mixed-language `scan --progress` output reports a global scan size, then resets progress denominators for separate language phases without labeling them. This makes progress output misleading in audits and automation logs.

## Evidence

Observed with a freshly built Shatter binary from commit `949f5fe5f2d1` against `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.

Repro command:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed 20260521 \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --request-timeout 20 \
  --parallelism 4 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --progress \
  --format json \
  -o /tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-mixed-scan.json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

The scan announced 24 attempted functions, then progress was emitted as two separate unlabeled totals:

```text
Scanning 24 function(s) in dependency order...
[progress] starting 1/2: pickpackit_catalog
[progress] failed 1/2: pickpackit_catalog
[progress] starting 2/2: run_devserver
[progress] failed 2/2: run_devserver
...
[progress] starting 1/22: bundleListSearch
...
[progress] completed 22/22: ...
```

The final JSON summary in `/tmp/shatter-pickpackit-updated-mixed-scan.json` reported:

```json
{
  "attempted_functions": 24,
  "completed_functions": 22,
  "failed_functions": 2,
  "skipped_functions_count": 81,
  "unsupported_functions": 81,
  "total_discovered_functions": 105,
  "overall_coverage": 16.0
}
```

The two Rust failures were:

```text
pickpackit_catalog: frontend error: request timed out after 20s
run_devserver: timed out after 20s
```

## Diagnosis

The scan likely batches work by language internally. That is reasonable, but progress output does not expose the distinction. A user sees a 24-function scan followed by `1/2` and later `1/22`, which looks like the denominator changed mid-run.

## Desired Behavior

Progress output should either use a stable global denominator for the whole scan or explicitly label phase/language-local progress.

Acceptable output shapes include:

```text
[progress] starting 1/24: pickpackit_catalog
```

or:

```text
[progress] rust 1/2, global 1/24: pickpackit_catalog
[progress] typescript 1/22, global 3/24: bundleListSearch
```

## Acceptance Checks

- Add or reuse a mixed Rust + TypeScript scan fixture with at least two functions in each language.
- Run with `--progress`.
- Assert progress output does not emit unlabeled denominator resets.
- If both phase-local and global progress are reported, assert the language/phase and global totals are explicit.
- Keep JSON event fields, if any, unambiguous: include `language` or `phase`, and distinguish `phase_total` from `global_total`.

## In Scope

- Terminal progress lines for mixed-language scan.
- Structured progress event fields if they back the terminal output.

## Out of Scope

- Changing scan scheduling or language batching semantics.
- Changing sampled function selection.

title: str-7vs-tests: Test suite for LLM seed oracle (unit + integration + E2E)
description:
# str-7vs-tests: Test suite for LLM seed oracle (unit + integration + E2E)

## Description

Part of str-7vs. Adds the required test coverage for the LLM oracle. All four predecessor
issues (str-7vs-core, str-7vs-orchestrator, str-7vs-llm-crate, str-7vs-cli) must be merged
before starting this issue — the types and modules being tested are defined there.

## Resolved API surfaces (from predecessor issues)

**MockSeedOracle** (`shatter-llm/src/mock.rs`):
```rust
MockSeedOracle::scripted(entries: Vec<(ConditionId, Vec<InputVector>)>) -> Self
MockSeedOracle::always_fail() -> Self
MockSeedOracle::always_empty() -> Self
```

**OracleSlotMap** (`shatter-core/src/oracle.rs`):
```rust
OracleSlotMap::new(oracle: Arc<dyn SeedOracle>, config: LlmConfig, runtime: Arc<Runtime>) -> Self
OracleSlotMap::poll(id: ConditionId, ctx: OracleContext) -> Option<InputVector>
OracleSlotMap::retire(id: ConditionId)
OracleSlotMap::record_accepted()
OracleSlotMap::stats() -> OracleStats  // { queries_fired, tokens_used, candidates_accepted }
```

**Provenance tag**: `InputSource::LlmOracle` variant in `shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs:215`.

**Post-run summary format**: `LLM oracle: {q} queries · {t} tokens · {a} candidates accepted  [budget: {t} / {max}]`

**zolem**: `{ git = "https://github.com/ketang/zolem" }` — HTTP mock server. Used in
`[dev-dependencies]` of `shatter-llm/Cargo.toml` and `shatter-core/Cargo.toml`.
Read zolem's README/docs to understand its server API before writing tests.

**`InputVector`** = `Vec<serde_json::Value>` (type alias from str-7vs-core).

## Files to create / modify

- Unit tests: inline in `shatter-core/src/oracle.rs` or `shatter-core/tests/oracle_unit.rs`
- Integration tests: `shatter-llm/tests/integration.rs`
- E2E tests: `shatter-core/tests/e2e_llm_oracle.rs`
- Add `zolem` to `[dev-dependencies]` in `shatter-core/Cargo.toml` and `shatter-llm/Cargo.toml`

## Unit tests (no network — use MockSeedOracle directly)

All 10 cases are required and must be individually named:

| Test name | What it asserts |
|---|---|
| `slot_idle_to_pending_on_poll` | First poll on a new condition transitions Idle → Pending |
| `slot_pending_to_ready_on_completion` | poll() returns candidate after Pending resolves |
| `slot_dedup_drops_attempted_duplicate` | candidate matching attempted input is dropped; slot stays active |
| `slot_exhausted_at_query_cap` | no Idle→Pending after `max_queries_per_function` reached |
| `slot_exhausted_at_token_budget` | no Idle→Pending after `max_token_budget` reached |
| `semaphore_caps_concurrent_requests` | N+1 conditions only fire N Pending slots simultaneously |
| `slot_exhausted_after_max_retries` | `MockSeedOracle::always_fail` → Exhausted, does not retry |
| `oracle_none_no_poll_called` | `Option<OracleSlotMap>` is `None`; no oracle code runs |
| `retire_cancels_pending` | `retire()` on a Pending slot aborts the handle and removes the slot |
| `record_accepted_increments_stats` | `record_accepted()` increments `OracleStats.candidates_accepted` |

### Semaphore concurrency test note

Construct an `OracleSlotMap` with `max_concurrent_requests = 2`. Create 3 distinct
`ConditionId`s. Poll all 3. Assert that after polling, exactly 2 slots are `Pending`
and 1 is still `Idle`.

## Integration tests (shatter-llm/tests/integration.rs — zolem HTTP mock server)

These test a real adapter (or a thin stub HTTP adapter if no real adapters have landed)
pointed at a zolem HTTP mock server. Required cases:

1. **Well-formed response** — zolem returns a valid JSON array; adapter returns correct `InputVector`s.
2. **429 rate limit** — zolem returns HTTP 429; `RateLimitedOracle` retries with exponential backoff and eventually succeeds on a scripted retry.
3. **Max retries exhausted** — zolem always returns 429; `RateLimitedOracle` returns `Err` after `max_retries`.
4. **Malformed JSON response** — zolem returns non-JSON body; `parse_response` returns empty vec (no panic).
5. **Auth error (401)** — zolem returns 401; adapter returns `Err`, no retry.

If no real adapter has landed when this issue is worked, implement a minimal stub HTTP
adapter inline in `tests/integration.rs` that makes a POST request and returns whatever
JSON array the server responds with. The stub need not be production-quality — it exists
only to exercise the HTTP + parse pipeline. Name it `StubHttpAdapter`, keep it
`#[cfg(test)]`, and add a comment that it should be removed after str-g5b lands.
Minimal contract: takes a base URL string, POSTs `{"prompt": "<text>"}` as a JSON
body, expects a JSON array response.

## E2E tests (shatter-core/tests/e2e_llm_oracle.rs — zolem HTTP mock server)

Read `shatter-core/tests/e2e_concolic.rs` for the existing E2E fixture pattern before writing.

Point the adapter at zolem via `config.llm.openai.base_url` (or equivalent) set to
`http://127.0.0.1:{zolem_port}`.

Required E2E cases:

1. **LLM candidate reaches new equivalence class** — zolem returns a candidate that covers
   a branch Z3 did not. Assert: behavior map entry has `source: InputSource::LlmOracle`.

2. **Post-run summary correct** — after a run with known zolem responses, assert the
   summary line matches `LLM oracle: {q} queries · {t} tokens · {a} candidates accepted  [budget: ...]`
   with expected counts.

3. **Token budget halts new requests** — set `max_token_budget` to a low value. Zolem
   returns `tokens_used` responses that exceed the cap after 2 queries. Assert that
   exactly 2 queries were fired (in-flight completes; no new ones start).

4. **Concurrency cap respected** — set `max_concurrent_requests = 2` with 4 unsolved
   conditions. Assert via zolem request log that no more than 2 simultaneous requests
   were in flight.

Use a known fixture function from `shatter-core/tests/fixtures/` (or create one following
the existing E2E fixture pattern).

## Acceptance checks

- `cargo test -p shatter-core --test oracle_unit` (or inline tests) passes — all 10 named cases present
- `cargo test -p shatter-llm --test integration` passes
- `cargo test -p shatter-core --test e2e_llm_oracle` passes
- `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` still passes (no orchestrator regressions)
- No test uses a real LLM API key
- `task test-standard` passes

## Out of scope

- Real provider adapters → str-0o8, str-9w8, str-w4c, str-g5b
- Testing adapter-specific protocol behavior

## Depends on

str-7vs-core, str-7vs-orchestrator, str-7vs-llm-crate, str-7vs-cli

title: str-7vs-cli: LlmConfig wiring, CLI flags, and post-run summary
description:
# str-7vs-cli: LlmConfig wiring, CLI flags, and post-run summary

## Description

Part of str-7vs. Wires the LLM oracle into shatter-cli: flag parsing, oracle construction,
tokio runtime, and post-run summary. `LlmConfig` is already added to shatter-core by
str-7vs-core; this issue wires it through to the CLI.

**Prerequisite:** str-7vs-core and str-7vs-llm-crate must be merged first.

## Files to modify

- `shatter-cli/Cargo.toml` — add `shatter-llm` dependency
- `shatter-cli/src/args.rs` — add `LlmOverrides` struct, wire into `Scan` variant
- `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` or `shatter-cli/src/helpers.rs` — oracle construction,
  tokio runtime, pass `Option<OracleSlotMap>` to orchestrator, post-run summary

## Resolved codebase facts

- **`Scan` variant** — read `shatter-cli/src/args.rs` before editing to see current fields and derive structure.
- **`LlmConfig`** — defined in `shatter-core/src/config.rs` (added by str-7vs-core). Fields include `enabled`, `adapter`, `max_concurrent_requests`, `max_token_budget`, `max_tokens_per_query`, etc.
- **`OracleSlotMap`** — `shatter-core/src/oracle.rs` (str-7vs-core). Constructor: `OracleSlotMap::new(oracle, config, runtime)`.
- **`MockSeedOracle`** — `shatter-llm/src/mock.rs` (str-7vs-llm-crate). For `--llm-adapter=mock`, gate behind `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "test-helpers"))]`.
- **`RateLimitedOracle`** — `shatter-llm/src/rate_limit.rs`. Wrap every adapter at construction time.
- **tokio runtime** — check `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` for existing `tokio::runtime` usage before creating a new one.
- **clap stack budget** — the `Cli` enum is near clap's stack limit (see str-1414). Do NOT add flat fields to `Scan`. Use `Box<LlmOverrides>` with `#[command(flatten)]` as shown below.

## What to implement

### CLI (shatter-cli/src/args.rs)

```rust
#[derive(Args, Default)]
pub struct LlmOverrides {
    /// Enable LLM oracle (adapter and settings from config; overrides llm.enabled)
    #[arg(long)]
    pub llm: bool,

    /// LLM adapter to use, overrides llm.adapter in config
    #[arg(long, value_name = "ADAPTER")]
    pub llm_adapter: Option<String>,

    /// Cumulative token cap, overrides llm.max_token_budget in config
    #[arg(long, value_name = "TOKENS")]
    pub llm_token_budget: Option<u32>,
}
```

In the `Scan` variant add (do NOT add flat fields):
```rust
#[command(flatten)]
pub llm: Box<LlmOverrides>,
```

### Oracle construction (shatter-cli/src/main.rs or helpers.rs)

When `--llm` is present OR `llm.enabled: true` in config:

1. Apply CLI overrides to `LlmConfig`:
   - `llm_adapter` → `config.llm.adapter`
   - `llm_token_budget` → `config.llm.max_token_budget`

2. Create or reuse a `tokio::runtime::Runtime` (check for existing runtime first).

3. Select adapter by `config.llm.adapter`:
   - `"mock"` → `MockSeedOracle::scripted(vec![])` — only available under `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "test-helpers"))]`; return a clear error in release builds if `"mock"` is requested.
   - Any other adapter → return `Err("adapter '{name}' not yet available; install str-0o8/str-9w8/str-w4c/str-g5b")` until child issues land.

4. Wrap with `RateLimitedOracle::new(adapter, config.llm.max_retries)`.

5. `OracleSlotMap::new(Arc::new(wrapped), config.llm.clone(), Arc::new(runtime))`.

6. Pass `Some(slot_map)` to the orchestrator.

When `--llm` is absent and `llm.enabled` is false: pass `None` to the orchestrator.

### Post-run summary

After the orchestrator returns, if oracle was active, emit (via existing CLI output path):

```
LLM oracle: {queries_fired} queries · {tokens_used} tokens · {candidates_accepted} candidates accepted  [budget: {tokens_used} / {max_token_budget}]
```

Use `oracle.stats()` for the counts. Follow the existing output path for explore results
(read `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` to find it — do not use bare `println!` or `eprintln!`).

## Acceptance checks

- `cargo check -p shatter-cli` passes
- `cargo test -p shatter-cli` passes (no regressions)
- `cargo clippy -p shatter-cli` passes with no new warnings
- `shatter explore --help` shows `--llm`, `--llm-adapter`, `--llm-token-budget`
- Running without `--llm` behaves identically to before this change
- Post-run summary line emitted only when `--llm` is active
- `LlmConfig` loads from `.shatter/config.yaml` under `llm:` key with correct defaults
- Requesting `--llm-adapter=mock` in a release build returns a clear error message

## Out of scope

- Real adapter implementations → str-0o8, str-9w8, str-w4c, str-g5b
- Tests → str-7vs-tests
- `LlmConfig` struct definition → str-7vs-core

## Depends on

str-7vs-core, str-7vs-llm-crate

title: str-7vs-llm-crate: Create shatter-llm crate with prompt, parse, mock, and rate-limit wrapper
close_reason: Landed in main: shatter-llm crate added with prompt/parse/mock/rate_limit. All gates pass.

title: str-7vs-orchestrator: Integrate OracleSlotMap into the concolic orchestrator
description:
# str-7vs-orchestrator: Integrate OracleSlotMap into the concolic orchestrator

## Description

Part of str-7vs. Wire the OracleSlotMap (from str-7vs-core) into the concolic orchestrator
so LLM-generated candidates are consumed during normal exploration. The LLM oracle
occupies priority position 4 in the input-selection order (between genetic/boundary search
and random fallback).

**Prerequisite:** str-7vs-core must be merged before starting this issue.
All oracle types (`OracleSlotMap`, `OracleContext`, `ConditionId`, `InputVector`, etc.) are
defined there in `shatter-core/src/oracle.rs`.

## Files to modify

- `shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`
- `shatter-core/src/coverage_metrics.rs` — add `LlmOracle` variant to `DiscoveryMethod` enum (line ~20)

## Resolved codebase facts

- **`InputSource` enum** — `shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs:215`. Add `LlmOracle` variant here. Used as `WorklistEntry.source: InputSource` (line ~236) to tag how each input was generated.
- **`DiscoveryMethod` enum** — `shatter-core/src/coverage_metrics.rs:20`. Add `LlmOracle` variant here too (mirrors `InputSource`).
- **`WorklistEntry`** — struct in orchestrator.rs ~line 236, has `inputs: Vec<serde_json::Value>` and `source: InputSource`.
- **LlmConfig fields used here**: `context_lines: u32` (source window size).
- **`OracleSlotMap` API** (from str-7vs-core): `poll(id, ctx) -> Option<InputVector>`, `retire(id)`, `record_accepted()`, `stats() -> OracleStats`.

## What to implement

### 1. Add `InputSource::LlmOracle` and `DiscoveryMethod::LlmOracle`

In `orchestrator.rs` add `LlmOracle` to `InputSource`.
In `coverage_metrics.rs` add `LlmOracle` to `DiscoveryMethod`.
Update any exhaustive match arms on these enums.

### 2. Constructor change

Add `oracle: Option<OracleSlotMap>` to the orchestrator's struct and constructor.
When `None`, all oracle code paths are no-ops with zero overhead.

Add per-condition attempted-input rolling buffers to orchestrator state:
```rust
// Track up to 5 recently-tried inputs per condition, for OracleContext.attempted
attempted_by_condition: HashMap<ConditionId, VecDeque<InputVector>>,
```
(Lives on the orchestrator struct, not inside OracleSlotMap.)

### 3. Input-selection priority order

Read `orchestrator.rs` to find the existing input-selection loop. The LLM oracle slot drain inserts at priority 4:

```
1. User-provided inputs
2. Concolic solver (Z3)
3. Genetic / boundary search
4. ← LLM oracle slot drain (only when oracle.is_some())
5. Random fallback
```

On each round, for every unsolved `FailedCondition` the orchestrator holds:

1. Build `OracleContext`:
   - `function_source`: source text around the target function, capped to `config.llm.context_lines` lines
   - `param_types`: current function's `Vec<ParamInfo>` descriptors
   - `condition`: the `FailedCondition`
   - `attempted`: up to 5 most-recent entries from `attempted_by_condition[condition_id]`

2. Call `oracle.poll(condition_id, ctx)`.

3. If a candidate is returned:
   - Type-validate each `serde_json::Value` against `param_types`; drop mismatches (no coercion)
   - Deduplicate against `attempted_by_condition[condition_id]` and existing candidate pool; drop duplicates
   - If the candidate survives, inject into the normal execution queue with `source: InputSource::LlmOracle`

4. After execution: if the candidate reaches a new equivalence class, call `oracle.record_accepted()`. Call `oracle.retire(condition_id)`. Update `DiscoveryMethod::LlmOracle` attribution in explore results.

5. If the candidate does not cover new ground: push it into `attempted_by_condition[condition_id]` (drop oldest if > 5). The slot transitions back to Idle on the next tick.

6. When any strategy solves a condition: call `oracle.retire(condition_id)`.

### 4. Post-exploration hook

After the main loop, if `oracle.is_some()`, expose `oracle.stats()` to the caller
(shatter-cli) for the post-run summary. Return it as part of the existing explore result
or as a separate field — follow the existing `ExploreResult` structure.

## Acceptance checks

- `cargo check -p shatter-core` passes
- `cargo test -p shatter-core` passes (no regressions)
- `cargo clippy -p shatter-core` passes with no new warnings
- `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` passes (E2E gate — orchestrator changes require this)
- When `oracle` is `None`, no oracle code executes (verifiable by inspection)
- `WorklistEntry` entries from LLM candidates have `source: InputSource::LlmOracle`
- `oracle.retire()` is called whenever a condition is solved, regardless of which strategy solved it
- `attempted_by_condition` buffers cap at 5 entries per condition

## Out of scope

- Trait definition, slot machinery, `LlmConfig` → str-7vs-core
- `shatter-llm` crate → str-7vs-llm-crate
- CLI wiring → str-7vs-cli
- Tests → str-7vs-tests

## Depends on

str-7vs-core

title: str-7vs-core: Define SeedOracle trait and slot machinery in shatter-core
Reexamine shatter-cli Cli enum for clap stack limitation
description:
The `Cli` enum is near the edge of clap's 2 MB test-thread stack budget. Adding fields to the `Scan` variant has previously caused SIGABRT from stack overflow (discovered in str-izhn). The current workaround is to fuse related flags into single arguments rather than adding flat fields.

## Problem

The constraint leaks into CLI design decisions — feature flags that naturally belong as independent arguments get fused purely to avoid a stack overflow, not because fused is the better UX. This is an invisible maintenance trap.

## Goal

Investigate and resolve the root cause so new flags can be added without hitting stack limits:

1. **Measure current headroom** — how close are we to the limit today?
2. **Internal redesign options:**
   - Move `Scan` and other large variants to flattened subcommand structs (reduces derive depth)
   - Box large fields that inflate variant sizes
   - Restructure the derive hierarchy to reduce generated match-tree depth
3. **Visible surface consideration:** If restructuring internals still leaves little headroom, evaluate whether the visible CLI surface should also be redesigned (e.g., subcommand grouping, flag namespacing)
4. **Regression guard:** Add a compile-time or test-time assert on the size of the `Cli` enum variants so future contributors get a clear error instead of a mysterious SIGABRT

Discovered constraint documented in session memory (2026-05-13, str-izhn).
close_reason: Landed in 27f4c8ec on main. CliCommand size: 728 -> 176 bytes (76% reduction); Cli: 832 -> 280 bytes. Regression guard added (size budget 320 bytes).

title: TS executor misses ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND (ESM error shape)
description:
## Problem

`isModuleNotFoundError` in `shatter-ts/src/executor.ts` only recognizes the CommonJS error shape — code `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` / message starting with "Cannot find module". It does not recognize the Node ESM resolver shape — code `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` / message starting with "Cannot find package".

The ESM shape fires when a bare specifier escapes into a package whose `package.json` carries `"type": "module"`, causing Node's ESM resolver to run. The error then bubbles up as `runtime_failed` instead of falling back to the unresolvable-module stub.

## Evidence

Observed in Kapow `web/` (has `"type": "module"`). Path-alias adapter failures produce "Cannot find package @/..." rather than "Cannot find module".

Original fix in `str-jeen.66-ts-path-aliases` branch was dropped after a narrower fix landed in 224d85e9. That commit handles absolute-TS-path dispatch but does not extend `isModuleNotFoundError`.

## Fix

In `shatter-ts/src/executor.ts`, extend `isModuleNotFoundError`:

```ts
const hasCode =
  code === "MODULE_NOT_FOUND" || code === "ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND";
const hasMessage =
  message.startsWith("Cannot find module") ||
  message.startsWith("Cannot find package");
```

Add a unit test covering both error shapes.

title: Rust opaque type guidance
description:
Title: Rust opaque type guidance
Type: feature
Priority: P2
Labels: rust,frontend,usability

## Problem

Rust scans skip many useful application functions because common framework and domain types are opaque, but the output does not give users a concrete next step such as generator/config snippets.

## Evidence

With the matching `shatter-rust` frontend on `PATH`, running a dry scan of `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit` reported 65 skipped functions due to unexecutable parameter types.

Examples from output:

```text
build_router: param "config" → Config (opaque type); param "pool" → PgPool (opaque type)
auth_layer: param "_" → State; param "req" → Request; param "next" → Next
assistant_message: param "_" → State; param "current" → CurrentAccount; param "_" → Json
derive_trip_kind: param "starts_on" → NaiveDate; param "ends_on" → NaiveDate
suggested_packing_items: param "items" → [] → Item; param "people" → [] → Person; param "trip" → Trip; param "supplies" → [] → DestinationSupply
```

The output explains that types cannot be automatically synthesized, but it does not provide an actionable recipe for common cases such as Axum extractors, SQLx pools, Chrono date types, or domain structs.

## Desired Behavior

When Rust functions are skipped for opaque types, Shatter should provide actionable guidance that helps users make progress. For common types, it should suggest known patterns; for project types, it should point to generator/config mechanisms with function/type names prefilled.

## Acceptance Checks

- Dry-run output for skipped Rust functions includes a concise “next step” section for opaque types.
- At least common framework categories have tailored hints: Axum `State`/`Json`/`Request`/`Next`, SQLx `PgPool`/transactions, Chrono date types, and project domain structs.
- Add a test or snapshot that verifies skipped opaque type diagnostics include concrete generator/config guidance.

## In Scope

- Diagnostics and guidance for Rust opaque parameter types.
- Examples or docs for configuring generators/adapters for Rust app types.

## Out of Scope

- Fully implementing automatic synthesis for every framework/domain type.
- Changing which functions are skipped.

title: Rust frontend setup warning
description:
Title: Rust frontend setup warning
Type: task
Priority: P2
Labels: rust,install,docs

## Problem

A freshly built `shatter` binary from the root workspace does not make Rust scanning available unless `shatter-rust` is built separately and placed on `PATH`. The current warning is technically accurate, but the setup path is easy to miss during local source builds and mixed-repo audits.

## Evidence

Built Shatter from `/home/ketan/project/shatter` at commit `ed999dfd6638`:

```bash
cargo build --release --bin shatter
```

Then ran in `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`:

```bash
/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter scan --dry-run --color never .
```

Output included:

```text
[warn] skipping 38 Rust file(s): shatter-rust frontend not found: install it on PATH or build with `cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml` (run will continue with available languages; pass --require-rust to fail instead)
```

After separately running:

```bash
cargo build --release --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml
```

and adding `/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release` to `PATH`, the same dry run discovered Rust functions too.

## Desired Behavior

Improve documentation and diagnostics for source-build users so it is explicit that Rust scanning requires a matching `shatter-rust` frontend on `PATH`. This issue is not asking to change release packaging.

The documentation update should target the root `README.md` source-build/install section. If the repo has a more specific distribution or Rust frontend setup document, link to that from `README.md`; otherwise add the local source-build instructions directly there.

## Acceptance Checks

- Source-build documentation includes the exact command to build `shatter-rust` and the exact `PATH` addition needed for local Rust scans.
- The missing-Rust-frontend warning distinguishes “main CLI is available” from “Rust frontend is missing from PATH,” and points to `README.md` or a stable doc path linked from `README.md`.
- Add or reuse a repo-local Rust-containing fixture for CLI verification. Do not depend on `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit`.
- Add an automated CLI test, or extend an existing CLI test, proving a dry-run scan of that Rust fixture with `shatter-rust` absent emits the improved warning.
- Verify, either in the same automated test if practical or as a documented manual check in the issue implementation notes, that a dry-run scan with the locally built `shatter-rust` directory on `PATH` discovers Rust files/functions.

## In Scope

- Source-build documentation for `shatter-rust`.
- User-facing diagnostics when Rust frontend is missing.
- CLI test coverage for the missing-frontend warning.

## Out of Scope

- Release packaging changes or bundling `shatter-rust` with the main CLI.
- Adding a new self-check command.
- Fixing Rust harness execution bugs.
- Improving Rust type synthesis.
notes:
9000df7c only improved the warning message in helpers.rs. Three AC items remain unmet: (1) README.md source-build docs with exact cargo build + PATH commands; (2) repo-local Rust fixture that does not depend on pickpackit; (3) automated CLI test proving dry-run scan with shatter-rust absent emits the improved warning. Issue was closed before these were verified.
Prior gap note superseded — d367abe7 landed by rust-warning covers all remaining AC: README source-build subsection with exact commands, CLI test extended, helpers test updated, warning reframed to distinguish main CLI present vs Rust frontend missing. All AC met.
Orphaned fixture commit (str-difh-rust-fixture branch) landed in c85cf21: adds examples/rust/01-abs-diff.rs, the repo-local Rust fixture AC item that was missing from the original close.

title: Core sample seed help unclear
description:
Title: Core sample seed help unclear
Type: bug
Priority: P2
Labels: cli,usability,scan

## Problem

`scan --seed` rejects non-numeric seed values, but the help text does not tell users that the seed must be numeric. Descriptive seed strings are common in audit workflows and currently fail with a low-level parse error.

## Evidence

Command shape:

```bash
/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  scan . \
  --core-sample 25 \
  --seed audit-pickpackit-20260521 \
  --max-iterations 5 \
  --timeout-total 180 \
  --timeout-per-fn 20 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds
```

Runtime error:

```text
error: invalid value 'audit-pickpackit-20260521' for '--seed <SEED>': invalid digit found in string
```

Help text says only:

```text
--seed <SEED>
    Seed for deterministic core sample selection. Default: hash of (directory + git HEAD)
```

## Desired Behavior

Keep `--seed` numeric-only for now, and update help/error messaging to say the value must be an integer. This is a usability/documentation bug, not a request to support arbitrary string seeds.

## Acceptance Checks

- `scan --help` says `--seed` expects an integer or numeric seed.
- Passing `--seed audit-pickpackit-20260521` fails with an actionable error that says to pass an integer seed, not just `invalid digit found in string`.
- Numeric seeds such as `--seed 20260521` continue to work unchanged.
- Add a CLI test for invalid non-numeric seed behavior and help text.

## In Scope

- CLI argument parsing/help/error text for numeric-only `scan --seed`.
- Tests for numeric accepted input and non-numeric rejected input.

## Out of Scope

- Accepting arbitrary string seeds.
- Changing the core sample selection algorithm.
close_reason: Updated --seed help text to require non-negative integer (u64); added value_parser yielding actionable error message instead of clap's default 'invalid digit found in string'.

title: Explore JSON stdout mismatch
description:
Title: Explore JSON stdout mismatch
Type: bug
Priority: P2
Labels: cli,docs,explore

## Problem

`shatter explore --help` advertises `--format json` as a stdout format, but `shatter explore --format json --stdout` rejects JSON stdout at runtime.

## Evidence

Help text for `shatter explore` says:

```text
--format <FORMAT>
    Format for stdout output. One of: markdown (default), json, html, text
```

Command shape:

```bash
/home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore web/src/domain/demoIds.ts:demoItemSlug \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 60 \
  --request-timeout 30 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --format json \
  --stdout \
  --color never
```

Runtime error:

```text
Error: explore --format json is not supported on stdout. Use `-o <file>.json` to write a spec bundle, or drop --format json to render Markdown on stdout.
```

## Desired Behavior

The CLI help and runtime behavior should agree. Either JSON stdout should be supported for `explore`, or `explore --help` should not advertise JSON as a stdout format.

## Acceptance Checks

- If JSON stdout is supported: the repro command exits successfully and emits parseable JSON to stdout.
- If JSON stdout is intentionally unsupported: `shatter explore --help` no longer lists `json` as a stdout format and clearly points users to `-o <file>.json`.
- Add a CLI test covering the chosen behavior.

## In Scope

- Explore command help text and/or JSON stdout support.
- CLI tests for help/runtime consistency.

## Out of Scope

- Changing scan command JSON output behavior.
- Redesigning the JSON schema.
close_reason: Fixed in str-explore-bugs merge: help text no longer advertises json as explore stdout format

title: Explore ignores iteration flag
description:
Title: Explore ignores iteration flag
Type: bug
Priority: P2
Labels: cli,config,explore

## Problem

`shatter explore --max-iterations` can be ignored when `.shatter/config.yaml` defines `defaults.max_iterations`, even though CLI flags are documented as higher precedence than project config. `shatter scan` honored the same flag in the same project.

## Evidence

Project config in `/home/ketan/project/pickpackit/.shatter/config.yaml`:

```yaml
defaults:
  max_iterations: 100
  timeout: 60
```

Command shape:

```bash
PATH=/home/ketan/project/shatter/shatter-rust/target/release:$PATH \
  /home/ketan/project/shatter/target/release/shatter \
  explore web/src/features/keyboardShortcuts.ts:shouldOpenShortcutsHelp \
  --max-iterations 3 \
  --time-limit 60 \
  --request-timeout 30 \
  --exec-timeout 10 \
  --no-cache \
  --no-seeds \
  --color never
```

Output included:

```text
[batch 1/1] shouldOpenShortcutsHelp: 100 iters, 3 paths, 2/3 branches, 1.4s (ok)
```

Control scan of the same file with `--max-iterations 3` reported `iterations: 3` for each completed function.

## Desired Behavior

`explore --max-iterations N` should override `.shatter/config.yaml defaults.max_iterations` just as `scan --max-iterations N` does.

## Acceptance Checks

- In a fixture project with `.shatter/config.yaml defaults.max_iterations: 100`, `shatter explore target --max-iterations 3` reports and executes no more than 3 iterations per function.
- Add a regression test for CLI precedence in `explore`.
