title: Go handler adapters
description: Add the Go-side substrate and handler harnesses for targets whose natural contract is request/response or framework context instead of plain arguments.
close_reason: All 4 children complete: substrate, recognizers, net/http adapter, Gin adapter

title: Rust async adapters
description: Add the Rust-side substrate and runtime/handler harnesses for async functions and framework-owned handler contracts.

title: Browser runtime adapters
description: Add browser-oriented transparent adapters for DOM globals and browser-dependent TypeScript execution without polluting the generic executor.
close_reason: All 2 children complete (browser DOM adapter, browser globals recognizer)

title: React hook adapters
description: Add React-hook-specific recognition and execution support as a semantic adapter layered on top of the TypeScript substrate.
close_reason: All 4 children complete (hook recognizer, callable return, rerender scenarios, effect flush)

title: Execution adapter reference
description: Write a long-term reference document for Shatter's execution-adapter framework. The document should define the core abstraction boundary between shatter-core and language/framework-specific extensions, adapter roles, heuristics and recognition policy, composition rules, config and protocol surfaces, and cross-language examples including TypeScript React hooks/browser environments, Go HTTP/framework handlers, and Rust async/runtime/framework handlers. It should live in durable docs rather than docs/plans and include guidance suitable for decomposing implementation into small issues.
acceptance_criteria: A durable docs reference exists outside docs/plans; docs/INDEX.md links to it; the document covers abstraction boundaries, adapter lifecycle/roles, heuristics, composition, TypeScript/Go/Rust cases, compatibility and rollout guidance, and issue-decomposition guidance

title: Prune JSON to scan-global
description: After subtree-safe settings move or are fixed in .shatter/config.yaml, narrow shatter.config.json to the fields that are truly scan-global. Update the docs and precedence model so users can tell which file controls command-level discovery/output/cache behavior and which file controls hierarchical analysis behavior.
acceptance_criteria: shatter.config.json documents only scan-global fields; README and CLI help describe the split clearly; precedence examples no longer imply duplicate ownership for migrated YAML fields

title: Swarm: Live output survives interruptions
description:
Swarm molecule orchestrating epic str-7pkp.

Epic: str-7pkp
Coordinator:

title: Config scope cleanup
description: Align Shatter's two config systems so subtree-local analysis settings live in .shatter/config.yaml and truly scan-global command defaults remain in shatter.config.json. This epic covers moving safe per-function defaults into hierarchical YAML, fixing layered YAML merge bugs for fields that already exist in the schema, and then shrinking the JSON file to global scan concerns only.
acceptance_criteria: Small follow-up issues land independently; hierarchical YAML fields merge correctly; shatter.config.json is narrowed to scan-global concerns only

title: Document live output behavior
description: Document progress output, partial artifact layout, and resume semantics for users and downstream tooling.
acceptance_criteria: README and SPEC describe live progress behavior; partial artifact locations and meanings are documented; resume behavior and limitations are documented; examples show both live and final outputs.

title: Live output survives interruptions
description: Add live progress and durable partial outputs to explore and scan so users can see liveness during long runs and keep useful results when execution fails or is interrupted.
acceptance_criteria: Users see progress during long runs; completed work is preserved on interruption; partial outputs are inspectable; interrupted runs can resume where practical.

title: TS stub proxy: full trap set (has, set, deleteProperty, ownKeys, getOwnPropertyDescriptor)
description:
Follow-up to str-rqvt. The createUnresolvableModuleStub Proxy in executor.ts was missing several traps. Without them, runtime code using 'in', property assignment, deletion, Object.keys(), or getOwnPropertyDescriptor() on a stub could throw or return unexpected results.

Changes committed on str-rqvt-proxy-traps:
- executor.ts: add has, set, deleteProperty, ownKeys, getOwnPropertyDescriptor Proxy traps
- executor.test.ts: unit tests for each new trap  
- property.test.ts: fast-check property tests for stub shape invariants

Acceptance:
- 'in' operator returns true for any property on a stub
- property assignment and deletion do not throw
- Object.keys() returns empty array
- Proxy invariants satisfied for inherited function properties

title: Go NegotiateLanguage: harness unmarshal error (number into []string)
description: Step 5 of the walkthrough fails with: 'Exploration error for NegotiateLanguage: harness error: unmarshal supported: json: cannot unmarshal number into Go value of type string'. The Go harness is receiving a number where it expects a string element in the supported []string parameter. Needs investigation in shatter-go input generation or harness template.

title: Scan report verbosity: Function Details dumps all clusters (cap to 5 or use explore table format)
description: The # Shatter Scan Report 'Function Details' section lists every discovered cluster per function (up to 100). A 37-function scan produces 287KB of output. The explore output correctly shows 4-5 representative rows per function. The scan report should either: (1) cap clusters shown to 5 per function, or (2) use the same compact table format as shatter explore output. Discovered during walkthrough review after the str-dvtn.3 swarm.

title: TS analyze catches type recursion
description:
## Summary

The TypeScript frontend throws `Maximum call stack size exceeded` during analyze on files with complex generic or conditional types. The error is logged as an unhandled error and the request then times out, adding latency instead of returning a structured analyze failure for the file.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior

- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected:
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

## Parity Impact

- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExprWithFlow` to handle the same node type
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExpr` to handle the same node type
- [ ] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) — verify concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) — verify random explorer (`explorer.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` — verify both `--concolic` and default explorer paths receive the new config or flag

## Default Behavior

- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it:

## Required Test Updates

- [ ] Unit tests added or updated
- [ ] Property tests (proptest / fast-check / rapid) added for any new public function
- [ ] E2E concolic tests verified (`cargo test --test e2e_concolic`)
- [ ] Conformance tests verified (`npx task conformance`) — required if protocol-visible
- [ ] Walkthrough updated (`demo/walkthrough.sh`) if the compact demo narrative changes
- [ ] Gauntlet updated (`demo/gauntlet.sh`) or equivalent targeted coverage added for non-demo CLI changes

## Contract Updates

- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? no
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them (Z3 FFI / subprocess JSON / cross-collection index):
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: trust boundary, type gap, silent corruption (see `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md`)

## Steps to Reproduce

Affected files from `github.com/ketang/kapow` include:

- `web/src/lib/graphql.ts`
- `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts`

```bash
shatter explore web/src/lib/graphql.ts --analyze-only
```

Observed failure:

```text
[shatter-ts] Unhandled error processing request ...: Maximum call stack size exceeded
[error] Analyze error (InternalError): Unhandled error: Maximum call stack size exceeded
Error: analyze failed: request timed out after 30s
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- Recursive type-analysis failures are caught and returned as structured analyze errors without timing out the request.
- The frontend does not log an unhandled error for this case.
- Regression coverage includes at least one complex-type file that previously triggered the stack overflow path.

title: Go analyze handles large files
description:
## Summary

## Protocol-Visible Behavior

- Is this change backward-compatible? yes

## Parity Impact

- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExpr` to handle the same node type
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) — verify concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) gets the same change
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) — verify random explorer (`explorer.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` — verify both `--concolic` and default explorer paths receive the new config or flag

## Default Behavior

- If no, what flag or config option enables it:

- [ ] Gauntlet updated (`demo/gauntlet.sh`) or equivalent targeted coverage added for non-demo CLI changes

## Contract Updates

- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? yes
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them (Z3 FFI / subprocess JSON / cross-collection index): subprocess JSON between Go frontend and Rust core
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: trust boundary, type gap, silent corruption (see `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md`)

## Steps to Reproduce

Example failures from `github.com/ketang/kapow/api/internal/search`:

- `field_name_gen.go` at column ~9153
- `registry.go` at column ~26495
- `synonym.go` at column ~15014
- `validate.go` at column ~19598

```bash
shatter explore api/internal/search/registry.go --analyze-only
```

Observed failure:

```text
Error: analyze failed: failed to deserialize response:
  missing field `args` at line 1 column 26495
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- Large Go analyze responses are emitted intact or converted into structured per-file failures instead of malformed JSON.
- The CLI error makes it clear whether the failure came from response size, truncation, or invalid payload shape.
- Regression coverage includes at least one large-file analyze case.

title: Fix pre-existing clippy warnings in explore.rs (str-6al)
description: Two 'if statement can be collapsed' clippy errors in shatter-cli/src/commands/explore.rs at lines 519 and 588 were introduced by str-6al but not caught before merge. These are pre-existing failures unrelated to batch-1 bug fixes.

title: Downstream review plugin
description:
# Summary
Create a distributable Codex plugin for downstream Shatter users that can run Shatter, review its output, and write a markdown report enumerating issues.

## Scope
- Ship the plugin as an installable artifact under the repo plugin layout.
- Include three user-facing skills: run Shatter, review Shatter output, and report Shatter issues.
- Include a shared shell script that collects relevant system and project context for issue reports.
- Keep the reporting path tracker-agnostic: write markdown instead of creating tracker issues directly.

## Acceptance
- The plugin exists with a manifest plus the three skills and shared context-collection script.
- The review skill identifies the most important cases in human terms and also preserves precise evidence such as concrete samples, exact outputs, or spec fragments.
- The report skill writes a markdown file that enumerates issues and includes relevant system and project context collected by the script.

title: TS serializer handles BigInt
description:
# Summary

title: Project layout doc
description: Add a dedicated user-facing document describing the directories and configuration files Shatter creates and uses in a project. Scope: add docs/PROJECT-LAYOUT.md covering .shatter/, .shatter-cache/, shatter-artifacts/, shatter.scope.yaml, .shatter/config.yaml, seed pools, reports, and legacy path notes; link it from README.md, QUICKSTART.md, and docs/INDEX.md. Acceptance criteria: README no longer mixes bd/dev workflow with user onboarding; QUICKSTART gives a copy-paste first run; docs index points users and contributors to different starting documents; command/examples referenced from README/QUICKSTART match the current CLI surface.

title: Epic: Walkthrough / gauntlet split
description:
Create a compact, parity-first demo walkthrough and move the broad coverage
script into a separate gauntlet track.

Goals:
- walkthrough becomes a concise demo narrative rather than an ever-growing CLI inventory.
- gauntlet becomes the place for broad command and flag coverage.
- The compact walkthrough maintains parity across TypeScript, Go, and Rust for the core user journey.
- Local and Docker walkthrough runners cover the same ground as closely as practical.

This epic coordinates policy, script, Docker, and docs/task updates required
to make that split durable.

title: fix(rust-frontend): harness generation partial move error for Result-returning functions
description:
The Rust frontend generates harness code that partially moves a Result<T, E> value and then tries to use it, causing a compilation error for functions that return Result types (e.g. safe_divide, negotiate_language in the Rust examples).

Error in generated src/main.rs:
  error[E0382]: use of partially moved value: `result`
  51 |     Ok(v) => (Some(serde_json::to_value(&v).unwrap_or(Value::Null)), None),
         - value partially moved here
  68 |     if let Err(ref msg) = result {
                                 ^^^^^^ value used here after partial move

Fix: change Ok(v) to Ok(ref v) in the match arm that serializes the Ok variant in the harness template.

Affects: Step 11 of the walkthrough (Explore Rust Functions). Discovered in walkthrough review after str-m38g merge.

title: Rust shared target binary lookup
description: After opting generated Rust harness manifests out of the repo workspace, standalone Rust explore in a worktree still fails on the default project-scoped harness cache path with 'compiled binary not found'. The same command succeeds when SHATTER_HARNESS_CACHE points to a temp directory outside the repo, so the remaining bug is in the shared target / binary lookup path rather than workspace membership. Scope this to the default shared-target harness build path for standalone Rust executes.
acceptance_criteria: Standalone Rust explore from a worktree succeeds on the default project-scoped harness cache path without requiring SHATTER_HARNESS_CACHE overrides.\nThe standalone harness build path finds the compiled shatter-exec-temp binary after a successful cargo build.\nThe fix preserves shared target reuse for standalone Rust harness builds.

title: Go walkthrough shrink overflow
description: The walkthrough Go explore step can panic in shatter-core::shrink::witness_complexity with 'attempt to add with overflow' while summarizing mixed Go example output. Reproduced in the walkthrough at step 9 during the grouped Go explore command. Scope this to the overflow in shrink witness complexity / summary generation so the walkthrough and normal explore runs do not panic on valid Go results.
acceptance_criteria: The grouped Go walkthrough explore step no longer panics with 'attempt to add with overflow'.\nshatter_core::shrink::witness_complexity handles the relevant witness shape without integer overflow.\nThe walkthrough completes the Go explore step without an unexpected non-zero exit code.

title: Rust harness workspace exclusion
description: Running the demo walkthrough from a worktree fails at the Rust explore step because generated harness crates under .shatter/cache/harness/rust/harnesses are treated as being inside the repo workspace, and Cargo rejects them with 'current package believes it's in a workspace when it's not'. This blocks walkthrough verification in worktrees even when the walkthrough change is unrelated. Scope this to the generated Rust harness package/workspace interaction: ensure generated harness manifests build cleanly from worktrees without requiring manual workspace edits.
acceptance_criteria: Generated Rust harness crates build successfully when Shatter runs from a git worktree under .claude/worktrees.\nThe walkthrough Rust explore step no longer fails with Cargo workspace-membership errors for generated harness crates.\nThe fix does not require adding per-harness paths to the repo workspace manifest manually.

title: Walkthrough wall times
description: Add explicit wall-clock timing to the demo walkthrough so humans can see how long each walkthrough step took and how long the full walkthrough took overall. Scope this to walkthrough presentation: measure elapsed wall time at the script layer, print one duration summary for each named step, and print one total duration summary at the end of the run. Keep the output concise and readable alongside any existing structured timing summaries.
acceptance_criteria: Each named walkthrough step prints its elapsed wall-clock duration after it runs.\nThe walkthrough prints one total wall-clock duration for the full run at completion.\nTiming output remains human-readable and does not overwhelm the walkthrough output.

title: Go harness: atomic mock counters, __shatter_get_results, EOF exit fix
description:
Three small improvements to Go harness mock recording and result collection, prototyped in str-9u7n-stdin-harness-loop but not merged to main.

1. __shatter_get_results() in recorder.go: encapsulates recorder state serialization (lines, branch path, scope events) as a named function, making the recorder independently testable.

2. Atomic int64 for mock call indices: generateLoopMockFile uses plain int++ for shatterMock%d_callIdx. Switch to int64 with atomic.AddInt64/atomic.StoreInt64 for correctness if mock functions are called from concurrent goroutines in the target code.

3. EOF exit code convention: generated harness main() currently falls off the scan loop (exit 0). Add os.Exit(1) after the loop so exit 1 = clean EOF. The executor ignores it now (_ = h.cmd.Wait()) but adopting the convention now makes future error-detection improvements straightforward.

Reference: commits 1525012, 4dfff2a, d874f1c, 5f22d34 on str-9u7n-stdin-harness-loop.

title: Go prepared harness reuse
description:
Implement warm reuse for the Go executor so repeated executes of the same function in one session reuse a previously built harness binary and persistent subprocess instead of paying cold-start build cost each time. Scope is Go only. Define the cache key from build-affecting identity inputs such as source path, function name, mock configuration, and any other inputs that change generated harness contents. Reuse must be safe: changed build inputs force a rebuild, and dead cached subprocesses are detected and recreated automatically.

Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

Parity Impact
- No cross-frontend parity update required; this issue is internal to the Go executor cache/reuse path.

Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

Required Test Updates
- [x] Unit tests added or updated

Contract Updates
- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? no
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them: N/A
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: N/A

First execute of a Go target builds and starts one harness binary.
Second execute of the same target with different inputs reuses the existing binary/process and does not rebuild.
Changing any build-affecting cache-key input forces a rebuild; dead cached harnesses are detected and rebuilt automatically.

title: Harness cleanup recovery
description:
Add cleanup and recovery behavior for semantic harness state.

Scope:
- prune orphaned harness registrations when sources disappear
- recover from missing generated files or cache metadata
- provide explicit cleanup for tool-owned cache without deleting preserved outputs by default
acceptance_criteria:
- Missing generated harness files or metadata recover cleanly.
- Cleanup removes tool-owned cache/state without deleting preserved artifacts by default.
- Orphaned harness entries are pruned when their backing sources disappear.

title: Standalone harness fallback
description:
Implement the explicit standalone-file fallback for Rust and Go when no parent crate/module exists.

Scope:
- generate minimal standalone build projects in tool-owned cache
- keep per-request scratch separate from reusable standalone cache
- avoid treating temp directories as durable cache even for standalone execution
acceptance_criteria:
- Standalone files execute through a dedicated fallback path instead of crate/module semantic mode.
- Reusable standalone build state lives in tool-owned cache, not temp.
- Preserved outputs for standalone execution remain separate from scratch and reusable cache.

title: Rust harness compat errors
description:
Add explicit compatibility analysis and user-facing errors for Rust harness mode selection.

Scope:
- detect when a target cannot execute in `bin_only` because its callable surface is not externally nameable
- fail early with specific reasons
- guide users to explicit `crate_bridge` opt-in instead of silently mutating the project
acceptance_criteria:
- `bin_only` incompatibilities are detected before opaque build failures whenever practical.
- Errors explain the specific signature or visibility problem.
- Errors point users to explicit `crate_bridge` opt-in and do not auto-escalate modes.

title: TS frontend: structural deep-equal, worker_threads, and efficient snapshotting
description:
Two performance improvements for the TypeScript frontend executor:

1. **Structural deep-equal instead of JSON.stringify comparison**: executor.ts lines ~1082-1093 use JSON.stringify(before) !== JSON.stringify(after) to detect global state changes. This is O(n) serialization on both sides just to check equality, and it's lossy (undefined, functions, circular refs are silently dropped or mangled by JSON.stringify). Replace with a structural deep-equal (Node's util.isDeepStrictEqual or a lightweight equivalent). This is both faster and more correct. (See also str-mro9 for the full structuredClone + snapshotting overhaul.)

2. **worker_threads for CPU-bound instrumentation**: Node is single-threaded, so instrumentation (AST parsing, transformation, SymExpr building) blocks the event loop. For scan mode with many functions, this serializes work that could be parallelized. Move instrumentation to a worker_threads pool. The main thread handles protocol I/O; workers handle CPU-bound AST transforms. This matters most when the TS frontend is processing many files during scan.
acceptance_criteria:
Instrumentation can run on worker_threads (at least for scan mode with multiple files).
All existing property tests and E2E tests pass.

title: Persistent harness subprocess for warm iteration speed
description:
Instead of spawning a new process per execute call, keep the compiled harness running as a long-lived subprocess that accepts input/output pairs over stdin/stdout in a loop. This eliminates subprocess spawn overhead (~10ms per call) and enables sub-5ms warm iterations.

The harness main() becomes a read-execute-write loop:
- Read JSON request from stdin (one line)
- Reset runtime state
- Execute function with deserialized inputs
- Write JSON result to stdout (one line)
- Loop until EOF (signals shutdown)

The frontend handler maintains a map of function → running subprocess. On execute:
- If subprocess exists and is alive → send inputs, read result
- If subprocess exists but died → restart it
- If no subprocess → compile (if needed) and start

Depends on stdin-based input passing (str-9u7n) being done first, since the loop protocol is an extension of stdin-based inputs.
acceptance_criteria:
Harness binaries run as persistent subprocesses, reused across execute calls for the same function.
Warm iteration (same function, different inputs) completes in <5ms.
Subprocess is cleaned up on teardown/shutdown.
Crash recovery: dead subprocess is detected and restarted transparently.

title: Debug builds by default for harness compilation
description:
Default harness compilation to debug mode instead of release. For concolic exploration, execution speed is irrelevant (functions are small, run briefly); compilation speed dominates. Debug builds are 2-5x faster than release.

Add a --release flag or SHATTER_HARNESS_RELEASE=1 env var for cases where execution performance matters (e.g., performance-sensitive target functions).

Applies to both Rust (cargo build vs cargo build --release) and Go (go build vs go build -ldflags='-s -w' or similar).
acceptance_criteria:
Rust harness compiles in debug mode by default.
Go harness compiles in debug mode by default.
Opt-in to release/optimized builds via flag or env var.
Cold start time measurably reduced.

title: Split .shatter lifecycles
description:
Separate the current `.shatter/` catch-all directory into distinct locations based on lifecycle and ownership.

Scope:
- Internal generated harness state and cache should move out of the single mixed-use `.shatter/` directory.
- Build artifacts and reusable cache should live in a tool-owned cache location, not alongside user-visible outputs.
- Disposable per-run output should live in temp-scoped storage.
- Preserved analysis outputs should live in a dedicated user-visible repo directory named `shatter-artifacts/`, not under hidden internal state.

This issue is only for establishing the directory split and ownership boundaries. It should not redesign the executor protocol or broader harness architecture beyond what is needed to separate storage lifecycles.
acceptance_criteria:
- Directory responsibilities are explicitly defined for internal state/cache, disposable temp output, and preserved artifacts.
- Preserved outputs are written under `shatter-artifacts/` instead of `.shatter/`.
- Internal cache/build state is no longer co-located with preserved artifacts.

title: Walkthrough: add HTML report output
description:
Update demo/walkthrough.sh to exercise HTML output in addition to existing console markdown output.

## Behavior

1. At the start of the walkthrough, create a temporary directory for HTML reports and print its location:
   ```
   HTML reports will be written to: /tmp/shatter-walkthrough-XXXX/
   ```

2. For each command that produces reports (scan, explore, export), pass `-o <tmpdir>/<step>.html --stdout` so the user gets both:
   - Console output (current markdown rendering, unchanged)
   - HTML report written to the temp directory

3. At the end of the walkthrough, print the directory again:
   ```
   HTML reports written to: /tmp/shatter-walkthrough-XXXX/
     scan-report.html
     explore-report.html
     ...
   ```

## Dependencies
- str-zt4v (multi-format output with -o flags and --stdout)

## Notes
- The walkthrough should NOT clean up the temp directory — user may want to inspect reports after
- Uses `mktemp -d` for the temp directory
- Existing console output is unchanged; HTML is purely additive

title: Multi-format output with extension-inferred format and additive -o flags
description:
Add support for multiple simultaneous output destinations on shatter commands (scan, explore, export, etc.), with format inferred from file extension.

## Output model

### Format inference from extension
| Extension | Format |
|---|---|
| .html | HTML report |
| .md | Markdown |
| .json | JSON |
| .txt | Plain text (markdown with formatting stripped) |
| other | Error: "unknown output format for extension '.foo' — supported: .html, .md, .json, .txt" |

Unknown/unhandled extensions are rejected with a clear error message.

### Flags
- `-o <path>` — write output to file, format inferred from extension. Multiple allowed; additive.
- `--stdout` — force console output in addition to file output. Format controlled by `--format` (defaults to markdown).
- `--format <fmt>` — controls stdout format only. One of: markdown, json, html, text.

### Behavior matrix
| Flags | Result |
|---|---|
| (none) | Stdout in markdown (current behavior, unchanged) |
| `-o report.html` | HTML file, no stdout |
| `-o report.html -o report.md` | Both files, no stdout |
| `-o report.html --stdout` | HTML file + markdown on stdout |
| `-o report.html --stdout --format json` | HTML file + JSON on stdout |
| `--stdout --format json` | JSON on stdout (no file) |

### Key principles
- No `-o` flags → stdout by default (backwards compatible)
- `-o` present → files only, stdout suppressed unless `--stdout` explicitly given
- Formats are additive: each `-o` is independent
- Same format to multiple files is allowed (no reason to prevent it)

## Affected files
- `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` — shared output flags on relevant subcommands
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/scan.rs` — current output logic → refactor to multi-destination
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/explore.rs` — same
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/export.rs` — same
- `shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs` — report renderers (HTML, JSON already exist; add plain text)
- New: plain text renderer (strip markdown formatting)

title: shatter init command
description:
Add a `shatter init` subcommand for project initialization.

## Behavior

### Standalone
`shatter init` prepares a project for Shatter analysis:
1. Create `.shatter/` directory structure
2. Generate `.shatter/config.yaml` with sensible defaults (detected language, frontend path, etc.)
3. Run static analysis to pre-populate opaque types (when str-io5d is available)
4. Report what was set up

### Implicit on first use
When any primary subcommand (`analyze`, `explore`, `scan`) is run in an uninitialized project (no `.shatter/` directory), run `init` implicitly before proceeding. Inform the user: "No .shatter/ found — initializing project" with the same output.

### Idempotent
Running `init` on an already-initialized project should be safe — preserve existing config, report what (if anything) was updated.

## Value
- Gives users a clear starting point instead of config appearing implicitly from various commands
- Natural place to run static opaque type detection and pre-populate the config
- Discoverable entry point for new users

## Affected files
- `shatter-cli/src/main.rs` — add `init` subcommand
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/` — new `init.rs` module
- Primary command handlers — check for initialization, run implicitly if needed
- `demo/walkthrough.sh` — exercise the new command

title: Learning mode: suggest opaque types from execution failures
description:
After a scan or explore run, suggest types that should be added to the opaque types config based on execution evidence, optionally confirmed by static analysis heuristics.

## Two tiers of suggestions

### Tier 1: High confidence (execution + static confirmation)
Types where:
- Every generated input for the type caused consistent failures (constructor throws, undefined method, type assertion failure — not branch-dependent errors)
- Static analysis heuristics (str-io5d) also flag the type (no public constructor, transitive opacity, no implementors)

Both signals agree → high confidence the type is genuinely opaque.

### Tier 2: Execution-only (no static confirmation)
Types that consistently failed execution across multiple functions/inputs, but static analysis did not flag them. These may be opaque for reasons static analysis can't detect (requires async init, needs env vars, returns useless stub without real connection, etc.) or may be false positives (missing test setup, env issue).

Reported separately with lower confidence.

## Detection logic

1. During execution, track parameters whose generated values consistently fail. Tag failures by parameter type and failure pattern (exception type, message pattern).
2. After the run, aggregate: types that failed across multiple functions or ≥N inputs for the same function are candidates.
3. Filter candidates through static heuristics (str-io5d): match → tier 1, no match → tier 2.
4. Report both tiers with the failure pattern and (for tier 1) the static reason.

## User-facing output

### CLI text
```
Suggested opaque types (high confidence — static + execution):
  pg.Client        no public constructor; 4/4 executions failed
  redis.RedisClient  constructor requires net.Socket (opaque); 3/3 failed

Suggested opaque types (execution failures only):
  MyOrmModel       4/4 executions threw TypeError; no static signal

To add all high-confidence suggestions:
  shatter config add-opaque pg.Client redis.RedisClient

To add all suggestions:
  shatter config add-opaque pg.Client redis.RedisClient MyOrmModel
```

The `shatter config add-opaque` command accepts multiple type names and writes them to `.shatter/config.yaml`. The printed command is explicit and editable — the user can remove types before pasting.

### HTML report
- Two sections: high confidence and execution-only
- Each type has a checkbox: high-confidence checked by default, execution-only unchecked by default
- All/none bulk selectors per section
- Dynamically-updated `shatter config add-opaque ...` command string at the bottom reflecting current checkbox selections — user copies and pastes

## Dependencies
- str-asnl (diagnostics format — categories and nesting paths used in suggestion output)
- str-io5d (static analysis heuristics — used for tier 1 confirmation)

## Affected files
- `shatter-core/src/executability.rs` — candidate tracking during execution
- `shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs` — aggregation and reporting
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/scan.rs` — CLI output formatting
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/explore.rs` — CLI output formatting
- Report templates (HTML) — checkbox UI, dynamic command generation
- Config CLI — `shatter config add-opaque` subcommand

title: Static analysis heuristics for opaque type detection (high confidence)
description:
Detect opaque types through static analysis without execution. These are high-confidence heuristics where the type system provides strong evidence that a type cannot be synthesized.

## Heuristics

### 1. No exported constructor or factory
If a type has no public constructor and no exported `create*`/`new*`/`open*` factory function in its module, it cannot be instantiated. Both TypeScript's type checker and Go's AST expose this information.

### 2. Constructor requires opaque arguments (transitive opacity)
If the only constructor takes another opaque type (e.g., `new TLSSocket(socket: net.Socket)`), the type is transitively opaque. This is recursion on the same analysis — walk constructor parameter types and check each.

### 3. Interface with no concrete implementors in scope
A Go interface like `io.Reader` with no visible struct implementing it in the analyzed package. For TypeScript, an interface or abstract class with no concrete class implementing/extending it in the analyzed files.

### 4. Abstract class / private constructor
TypeScript `abstract class` or classes with `private`/`protected` constructor — no direct instantiation possible.

## Integration points

- Frontends (TS analyzer, Go analyzer) perform the static analysis during the `analyze` command
- Results feed into `TypeInfo` — a new field or variant indicating the static detection reason
- `check_executability()` in `shatter-core` consumes these signals alongside the existing hardcoded opaque lists
- Works in `analyze` and `--dry-run` modes (no execution needed)
- Detected types should carry the reason for detection (feeds into str-asnl diagnostics)

## Value
Today, opaque type detection relies entirely on hardcoded lists of known types per language. This means any third-party or application-specific type that wraps external resources goes undetected until execution fails. Static analysis catches these at analysis time, before any execution budget is wasted.

## Affected files
- `shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts` — add constructor/factory analysis
- `shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go` — add constructor/implementor analysis
- `shatter-core/src/types.rs` — extend `TypeInfo` to carry static detection metadata
- `shatter-core/src/executability.rs` — consume static signals

title: Improved opaque type diagnostics: explain WHY types are opaque
description:
When Shatter skips a function due to opaque parameter types, the current diagnostic is minimal:

```
Skipped 3 function(s) (unexecutable parameter types):
  processWithSocket: param "sock" has opaque type net.Socket
```

This tells the user *what* was skipped but not *why* the type is opaque or where in a complex type the opacity lives.

## Requirements

### Opacity categories
Each opaque type should be tagged with a category explaining why it can't be synthesized:

| Category | Examples | Explanation shown |
|---|---|---|
| Network handle | `net.Socket`, `net.Conn` | "requires live network binding" |
| I/O stream | `stream.Readable`, `io.Reader`, `os.File` | "wraps OS file descriptor or pipe" |
| Database connection | `database/sql.DB`, `pg.Client` | "requires live database connection" |
| Concurrency primitive | `chan T`, `Worker` | "runtime scheduling state" |
| Process handle | `child_process.ChildProcess` | "wraps OS process" |
| User-configured | (from config) | user-supplied reason, or "user-configured opaque type" |

Built-in opaques get categories at definition time in `OPAQUE_NODE_TYPES` (TS) and `opaqueGoTypes` (Go). User-configured opaques in `.shatter/config.yaml` should accept an optional reason:

```yaml
opaque_types:
  - DatabaseConnection                        # falls back to "user-configured opaque type"
  - name: HttpClient
    reason: "requires live HTTP connection"   # shown in diagnostics
```

### Structural nesting path
Show where in the type tree the opacity lives:
- Full path for nesting depth ≤ 3: `param "config" → field "db" → pg.Client (database connection handle)`
- Collapsed beyond 3 levels: `param "config" → ... → field "connection" → pg.Client (database connection handle)`
- HTML reports should allow expansion of collapsed paths via interaction (click-to-expand or hover)

### SkipReason changes
Extend `SkipReason` in `executability.rs` to carry:
- `category: OpaqueCategory` (enum for the categories above)
- `nesting_path: Vec<PathSegment>` (e.g., `[Param("config"), Field("services"), MapValue, Field("connection")]`)
- `user_reason: Option<String>` (for user-configured types)

### Output changes
- CLI text: `processWithSocket: param "sock" → net.Socket (network handle — requires live network binding)`
- CLI text (nested): `initApp: param "config" → field "db" → pg.Client (database connection — requires live database connection)`
- HTML report: full path expandable, category shown as badge/tag
- JSON report: structured `skip_reasons` with all fields

### Tips (secondary)
Where actionable, append a brief tip: "consider adding a generator or mocking this dependency". Low priority — diagnostics come first.

## Affected files
- `shatter-core/src/executability.rs` — `SkipReason` struct, `check_executability()`
- `shatter-core/src/types.rs` — `find_opaque_label()` → needs to return path + category
- `shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts` — `OPAQUE_NODE_TYPES` → add category tags
- `shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go` — `opaqueGoTypes` → add category tags
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/explore.rs` — skip reporting format
- `shatter-cli/src/commands/scan.rs` — skip reporting format
- `shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs` — report formatting (text, HTML, JSON)
- Config parsing — support new `opaque_types` object format

title: Per-input path highlighting in HTML reports
description:
Highlight execution paths for specific sample inputs through the displayed source code in HTML reports.

## Use case

Users want to select a specific input from the paths table and see exactly which source lines that input executed, highlighted in the source code view. This enables tracing execution for individual test cases.

## Scope

1. Add interactive path selection (clicking a row in the paths table highlights its executed lines in the source code view)
2. Use lines_executed data already present in ExecutionSummary
3. CSS classes for per-input highlighting (distinct from overall coverage)
4. JavaScript for toggling highlights when a path row is selected
5. Multiple highlight colors or a clear active-path indicator to distinguish from general coverage

## Design notes

- The source code display (str-y8gq) already shows line-level coverage. This adds a second layer: which specific lines a particular input traversed.
- Keep the report self-contained (inline JS, no external deps)

## Acceptance criteria

- Clicking a path row highlights its executed lines in the source view
- Highlight is visually distinct from general coverage annotation
- Clicking another row switches the highlight
- Works without external JS dependencies (inline script)
acceptance_criteria: Interactive per-input path highlighting in source code view; self-contained inline JS; visually distinct from coverage annotation

title: Source code in HTML reports
description:
Include the tested source code in HTML exploration and scan reports, displayed alongside coverage data.

## Use case

Users need to understand coverage overall and on a per-input basis, and trace execution through the source. The report should be a standalone artifact readable without checking out the code.

## Scope

1. Read source files during report generation using the file path + line range already available in ObservationOutput/FunctionReport
2. Display source code in a <pre><code> block within each function's <details> section
3. Annotate lines with coverage status (covered/uncovered) using CSS classes
4. Show line numbers alongside source
5. Pass project_root through the report generation pipeline so source files can be resolved
6. Handle missing/unreadable source files gracefully (show location string only, as today)

## Non-goals (follow-up issues)

- Syntax highlighting (can use a JS library later)
- Per-input path highlighting (separate issue)
- Interactive source navigation

## Acceptance criteria

- Source code appears in HTML reports for each function
- Lines are annotated with covered/uncovered CSS styling
- Line numbers displayed
- Missing source files degrade gracefully
- Askama templates used (depends on str-heiu)
acceptance_criteria: Source code displayed in HTML reports with line-level coverage annotation; graceful fallback for missing files

title: Askama HTML templating refactor
description:
Replace hand-built String concatenation in report.rs with Askama compile-time templates for HTML report generation.

This is a strict refactor: the HTML output must be byte-for-byte identical (modulo whitespace normalization). No new features, no layout changes.

## Scope

1. Add askama as a dependency to shatter-core
2. Create HTML template files for:
   - Per-function exploration detail block (the <details> fragment)
   - Full explore report page wrapper
   - Full scan report page
3. Create Askama view model structs with #[derive(Template)]
4. Replace render_explore_fn_html(), wrap_explore_html(), generate_html_scan_report() internals to render via Askama
5. Add exact-output snapshot tests that compare Askama-rendered HTML against the current hand-built output for representative inputs
6. Existing tests must continue to pass

## Acceptance criteria

- cargo clippy clean, no new warnings
- All existing report tests pass
- New snapshot tests lock down the exact HTML structure
- No functional change to CLI behavior or report content
- HTML_CSS constant moves into the template or a shared include
acceptance_criteria: Askama templates produce identical HTML output to current hand-built code; snapshot tests lock down output; all existing tests pass

title: Add MC/DC coverage demonstration to walkthrough
description:
The walkthrough (demo/walkthrough.sh) has a single --mcdc explore invocation added in str-6wmm.9, but it doesn't showcase the MC/DC-specific output. Add a dedicated walkthrough section that:
1. Runs explore --mcdc --concolic on 13-mcdc-compound.ts (compoundAnd and compoundOr)
2. Shows the MC/DC Coverage report section with condition independence results
3. Runs export with --mcdc to show annotated test output
4. Briefly explains what MC/DC means in the walkthrough output (one-liner)

This makes MC/DC a visible, documented feature in the demo rather than a silent flag.

title: Add smoke task to catch pipeline regressions before walkthrough
description:
The walkthrough (48 steps, ~minutes) breaks too often because agents skip it — too slow to run automatically. pre-completion runs check but not the walkthrough, so agents declare issues done without verifying the full pipeline works.

Recent example: Go instrumentation broke on standalone files with func main() (redeclaration error), only caught when walkthrough was run manually.

Fix: add npx task smoke — a fast (~15s) end-to-end pipeline check running 2-3 explore calls across TS and Go frontends. Include in pre-completion so agents always run it.

Acceptance criteria:
- Completes in under 30s
- Covers one TS function + one Go standalone file with func main() + one Go library function
- Fails fast with a clear error on pipeline breakage
- pre-completion depends on smoke
- CLAUDE.md updated: run npx task smoke before closing any issue; npx task walkthrough for CLI output review

title: Acceptance criteria
description:
1. `npx task smoke` runs in under 30s
2. Covers at least: one TS function, one Go standalone file (with `func main()`), one Go library function
3. Fails fast with a clear error if the pipeline is broken
4. `npx task pre-completion` depends on `smoke`
5. CLAUDE.md updated: agents must run `npx task smoke` before closing any issue; run `npx task walkthrough` for CLI output review

description: Add `npx task smoke` — a fast (~15s) end-to-end pipeline check that runs 2-3 `explore` calls covering all three frontends (TS, Go, Rust). Include it in `npx task pre-completion` so agents always run it.

title: feat: HTML report output format
description:
## Summary

Add `--format html` output option to CLI commands (explore, scan, export, spec). Generate self-contained HTML reports from exploration and scan results.

## Architecture

**Shared data model**: Same format-agnostic Rust structs used by the Markdown output (str-nyle) feed into HTML-specific MiniJinja templates. No conversion between formats — each has its own templates optimized for its strengths.

```
Rust structs ──→ MiniJinja ──→ scan.md.j2  ──→ termimad ──→ terminal  (str-nyle)
                           └──→ scan.html.j2 ──→ file
```

## Scope

1. Add HTML templates for each command (`scan.html.j2`, `explore.html.j2`, etc.)
2. Generate self-contained HTML (inline CSS, no external dependencies)
3. Use full HTML/CSS capabilities: collapsible `<details>` sections, syntax-highlighted code, styled tables, summary stats
4. `--format html` writes to stdout or `--output report.html`

## Libraries

| Crate | Role | License |
|---|---|---|
| MiniJinja | Templating engine (shared with str-nyle) | Apache-2.0 |

## Dependencies

- Depends on str-nyle for the shared data model and MiniJinja infrastructure
- Templates are independent — HTML templates use full HTML/CSS, not derived from Markdown

## Notes

- Self-contained output: single HTML file with embedded CSS, no CDN links
- Suitable for CI artifacts, sharing with non-CLI users, embedding in dashboards

title: feat: Markdown output format with terminal rendering
description:
## Summary

Replace the current ad-hoc terminal formatting with Markdown as the primary output format, rendered to the terminal via termimad. This becomes the **default** output format — the current plain ANSI output is deprecated.

## Architecture

**Shared data model**: All CLI commands (explore, scan, export, spec) produce format-agnostic Rust structs (e.g., `ScanReport`, `ExploreResult`). These structs feed into format-specific MiniJinja templates. This issue covers the Markdown templates and terminal rendering; HTML templates are tracked in str-gkqy.

```
Rust structs ──→ MiniJinja ──→ scan.md.j2  ──→ termimad ──→ styled terminal
                           └──→ scan.html.j2 ──→ file     (str-gkqy)
```

## Scope

1. Define format-agnostic report structs (shared with str-gkqy)
2. Add MiniJinja dependency and Markdown templates for each command
3. Add termimad dependency for terminal rendering of the generated Markdown
4. Make `--format md` the default output format
5. Deprecate the current plain ANSI output (keep behind `--format plain` temporarily)
6. `--format md --no-color` outputs raw Markdown (suitable for piping to files)

## Libraries

| Crate | Role | License |
|---|---|---|
| MiniJinja | Templating engine | Apache-2.0 |
| termimad | Terminal Markdown rendering | MIT |

## Notes

- Markdown is the single source for terminal output — no more hand-coded ANSI formatting
- termimad handles tables, code blocks, headings, and colors
- Raw Markdown output is useful for piping (`shatter scan . --format md --no-color > report.md`)
- Shares the data model and templating infrastructure with str-gkqy (HTML output)

title: fix: TS frontend crashes with EPIPE during scan timeouts
description:
## Problem

The walkthrough script produces numerous EPIPE stack traces during step 5 (Scan Standalone TypeScript). When the scan orchestrator times out a function exploration, it `drop(frontend)` which kills the child process and closes the pipe. If the TS frontend is mid-write of its response, Node.js emits an unhandled `EPIPE` error on stdout and crashes with a full stack trace.

## Root Cause

`shatter-ts/src/main.ts` has no error handler on `process.stdout`. When the Rust core drops a timed-out frontend (`scan_orchestrator.rs:901`), the pipe reader is closed. The TS frontend's next `process.stdout.write()` emits an `'error'` event with code `EPIPE`, which Node treats as an unhandled error.

## Fix

Add a `process.stdout.on('error')` handler in `main.ts` that catches `EPIPE` and exits silently (code 0). Re-throw non-EPIPE errors to preserve crash visibility for real bugs.

## Files

- `shatter-ts/src/main.ts`

title: Test case prioritization: order tests by marginal coverage per unit time
description:
Order tests by bug-catching ROI to maximize coverage reached per second of execution. Composes with TIA (str-zwgc) as a downstream stage: TIA selects affected tests, prioritization orders them, optional budget cutoff stops early.

## Algorithm

Greedy submodular maximization ("additional greedy"):
1. Score each test: (lines not yet covered by selected tests) / execution_time
2. Pick highest-scoring test, add to ordered list, mark its lines as covered
3. Recalculate scores for remaining tests (marginal coverage decreased)
4. Repeat until all tests ordered

O(n^2) with n=1,487 tests is about 2M comparisons, well under a second. Provably within (1 - 1/e) of optimal; in practice near-optimal at this scale.

## Data requirements

Extends the TIA coverage map (str-zwgc) with:
- Per-test execution time (every runner already reports this)
- Line-level coverage per test (not just file-level)
- Unique line count per test (lines no other test covers)
- Precomputed priority score: unique_lines / duration_s

Priority scores recomputed whenever the map refreshes (after any coverage-instrumented test run). Single pass over the map.

## Change recency weighting

Bias scores toward recently-changed code where bugs concentrate. Weight covered lines by days_since_last_change of their file (git log -1 --format=%ct -- file). Lines in recently-changed files get a multiplier. This makes the ordering adapt to what the developer is actively working on.

## Pipeline composition with TIA

All tests (1,487) -> TIA (str-zwgc): filter to change-affected -> Affected tests (~50-200) -> Prioritize: order by marginal coverage/time -> Ordered sequence -> Budget cutoff (optional): stop after N seconds -> First k tests that fit budget

The coverage map serves both stages. TIA uses file-to-test for selection, prioritization uses line-level coverage plus timing for ordering. One data structure, two optimizations.

## Flags

- shatter test --budget=10s: run prioritized tests until time budget exhausted
- shatter test --prioritize: run all affected tests but in priority order (fail-fast: most likely to catch bugs runs first)

## Prerequisites

- TIA coverage map infrastructure (str-zwgc)
- Baseline coverage measurement (never been run on this codebase)
- cargo-nextest for per-test filtering and timing
close_reason: Test case prioritization. 26 tests pass. Branch str-j0hu commit 2b9e984

title: Test impact analysis: coverage-based selective test execution
description:
Implement test impact analysis (TIA) to accelerate local development by running only the tests affected by changed files. The system maintains a coverage map recording which tests touch which source files, then uses that map to select the minimal test set for a given set of changes.

## Design

**Coverage map** stored at `.shatter/test-markers/coverage-map.yaml`:
- Forward map: test → {file: blob_hash} (update direction)
- Reverse map: file → [tests] (query direction, derived from forward)
- Blob hashes via `git hash-object` for content-addressable comparison

**Query flow**: `git diff --name-only` → changed files → reverse lookup → union of affected tests → format runner-specific filter

**Update flow**: after test run with coverage instrumentation, parse coverage output → update forward map entries for tests that ran → rebuild reverse map. Atomic write via temp file + rename.

**Staleness tracking**: each file entry records the blob hash at recording time. Changed hash = stale = must re-run associated tests. Import-change detection: if a file's imports changed (not just function bodies), broadly invalidate its dependents.

**Cold start**: empty map treats all files as unmapped → falls back to crate/package-scoped testing. Map populates incrementally as tests run with coverage.

**Tiered test markers**: tree hash (from `git write-tree`) stored at `.shatter/test-markers/<tier>` after a tier's tests pass. Pre-commit hook compares current tree hash against the required tier's marker.

## Flags

- `shatter test` (or wrapper script) — run only affected tests based on coverage map
- `shatter test --all` — run all tests regardless, bypassing TIA selection (always available as escape hatch)
- `shatter test --record` — force coverage recording to refresh the map
- `shatter test --tier=<quick|standard|full|e2e>` — run the specified tier and write its marker on success

## Runner integration

- Rust: `cargo-nextest` with `-E 'test(=name)'` filter expressions (requires installing cargo-nextest). Falls back to `cargo test -- name1 name2` for module-level filtering.
- TypeScript: Vitest with explicit file paths or `--testNamePattern`
- Go: `go test ./package/ -run 'TestA|TestB'`

## Coverage collection

LLVM source-based coverage via `cargo-llvm-cov --json` for Rust (~10-20% overhead). Vitest `--coverage` for TS. `go test -coverprofile` for Go. For local dev loops where tests take 5-15s, always-on coverage is acceptable (~1-3s overhead). Alternatively, periodic recording with `--record` flag.

## Current test landscape (for reference)

- 1,487 Rust tests across ~60 modules + 3 integration test files
- 195 Go tests across protocol/instrument/generators packages
- 6 TS test files
- No cargo-nextest installed yet
- No cross-cutting test groups; filtering is by module/crate/package name
- Existing tiers: Quick (cargo test), Standard (+clippy), Full (+TS+Go), E2E (+e2e_concolic), Walkthrough (+demo script)
notes: Plan saved to docs/plans/str-zwgc-test-impact-analysis.md. Exploration confirmed reuse of scm.rs (GitProvider, run_git), file_lock.rs (FileLock), existing serde_yaml dep. Three phases: P1 core data model + CLI, P2 coverage recording, P3 pre-commit integration.

title: Add remaining high-value contracts to solver bridge and protocol boundary
description:
The contracts crate was added in str-xlo9 with 3 annotations. The contracts policy (shatter-core/CLAUDE.md) identifies 5 qualifying sites. Two are annotated; three remain:

1. solve_for_new_path() postcondition: solved value JSON types match ParamInfo::type_info. The invariant str-6ayh violated. Proptest only catches this if someone writes a type-checking property; the contract catches it on every call path including E2E.

2. to_z3_expr() precondition: all SymExpr::Param nodes reference valid indices in the param list. Invalid index causes Z3 to use the wrong variable, producing silently wrong solutions.

3. Protocol validation wrappers: create validate_execute_result() and validate_analyze_result() with postconditions — branch_path non-empty, param_values length matches arity, function signatures have valid param counts. These functions don't exist today (serde types are used directly); creating them is the deliverable.

NOT in scope: expanding beyond the 5 qualifying sites, orchestrator state, pure functions, or anything proptest covers.

title: Fuzz targets for shatter-core Rust deserialization boundaries
description:
Go has 10 fuzz targets finding real bugs. Rust shatter-core has zero fuzz targets — only proptest (which uses structured generation, not byte-level mutation). Add cargo-fuzz or proptest bytes-based fuzzing for:

1. Protocol JSON parsing: arbitrary bytes → serde_json::from_slice::<Request>() never panics.
2. SymExpr deserialization: arbitrary bytes → serde_json::from_slice::<SymExpr>() never panics.
3. TypeInfo deserialization: same for TypeInfo (recursive type, high surface area).
4. YAML spec parsing: arbitrary bytes → serde_yaml::from_slice never panics on spec types.
5. String ops YAML loading: malformed string-ops.yaml content → build.rs/solver gracefully errors.

The Go fuzz tests found a real timeout overflow bug. Rust deserialization has similar untested boundaries.

title: Go semantic property tests beyond roundtrips
description:
All Go PBT is roundtrip serialization. No behavioral properties on:

1. Analysis: arbitrary Go source snippets (valid function signatures) → analyzer extracts correct parameter count and types. Use rapid to generate simple Go function signatures.
2. Instrumentation: instrumented code preserves original function behavior — for simple functions, executing instrumented vs original with same inputs produces same output.
3. Mock generation: sanitizeMockName is idempotent (sanitize(sanitize(x)) == sanitize(x)) and always produces valid Go identifiers (starts with letter/underscore, contains only alphanumeric/underscore).
4. Executor: for any valid function + valid inputs, executor either returns a result or a structured error (never panics, never hangs past timeout).
5. Protocol handler: for any sequence of valid protocol messages, handler produces valid responses in the same order (no reordering, no drops).

title: Proptest coverage for input_gen mutation and shrink at vector level
description:
Current PBT on input_gen has only 4 trivial type-preservation checks (mutate int→number, str→string, bool→bool, vec length). No properties on:

1. mutate_inputs (vector-level): arbitrary (Vec<Value>, Vec<ParamInfo>) → output vec same length, each element matches its ParamInfo type, at least one element differs from input (mutation actually mutates).
2. crossover_inputs: arbitrary parent pairs → child has same length as parents, each element type-compatible with ParamInfo.
3. shrink_candidates: arbitrary (Value, TypeInfo) → all candidates are valid for the TypeInfo, none equal the original, candidates are strictly simpler (for numeric: abs(candidate) <= abs(original), for strings: len(candidate) <= len(original), for arrays: len(candidate) <= len(original)).
4. Generator-aware mutation: when generators provide value_sources, mutated values should still satisfy generator constraints.
5. Idempotent shrinking: shrinking an already-minimal value (0, empty string, false, null) produces empty candidates vec.

title: Proptest coverage for orchestrator state machine and worklist ordering
description:
The orchestrator has only 3 properties on overlay_solved_values. No PBT coverage on:

1. Worklist ordering: arbitrary WorklistEntry insertions → entries dequeue in priority order (UserProvided > Z3Solved > Fuzzed > Seed).
2. Path deduplication: executing the same branch path twice → covered_paths set prevents re-exploration (second execution doesn't add to worklist).
3. Constraint accumulation: after N executions with symbolic constraints, the constraint set grows monotonically (constraints are never lost).
4. Budget exhaustion: with budget=N, explore loop terminates after at most N executions.
5. Convergence: with a finite set of reachable paths, explore eventually stops finding new paths (no infinite loops).

These properties test the core exploration loop invariants that unit tests can't easily cover.

title: Proptest coverage for spec and export roundtrips
description:
Spec/export was listed in str-ho1d but not delivered. Zero PBT on:

1. format_spec_yaml roundtrip: arbitrary FunctionSpec → YAML serialize → deserialize → assert structural equality.
2. format_spec_json roundtrip: same for JSON output.
3. format_spec_markdown: arbitrary FunctionSpec → markdown output is non-empty, contains function name, contains all class labels.
4. Export test generation: arbitrary BehaviorMap → generated test cases reference valid function names and use valid input values.
5. Invariant conversion: arbitrary ClassifiedInvariant → SpecInvariant conversion preserves label text, maps confidence correctly (≥0.95→High, ≥0.75→Medium, else Low), maps kind to valid InvariantCategory.

title: Document formal methods policy in CLAUDE.md
description: After implementing proptest and contracts, add a Formal Methods section to the root CLAUDE.md documenting: when to use proptest/fast-check/rapid (liberal — any non-trivial invariant), when to use contracts (high bar — only where types can't express the invariant), when to consider Kani (deferred — only highest-stakes properties), the relationship between these tools, per-component guidance (proptest in shatter-core, fast-check in shatter-ts, rapid in shatter-go), and anti-patterns (contracts that restate types, proptest for trivial functions). Could be folded into str-ho1d and str-xlo9 rather than a separate issue.

title: Fallback boundary search for unknown/opaque constraints
description: When Z3 returns unknown or timeout for a constraint, and Shatter has inputs on both sides of a branch, use coverage-guided binary-search-style shrinking to find the boundary concretely. Currently falls back to naive fuzz_inputs() which is blind. Requires one input on each side (true-path witness, false-path witness). Interpolate/mutate between them, re-execute, track which side each result takes. More effective than random fuzzing because it has directional signal. Uses same shrink_candidates infrastructure. Relates to str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation), str-e0uy (hybrid concolic + fuzzing).

title: Minimal witness shrinking
description: After discovering an input that triggers a behavior, shrink it to the simplest input that still triggers the same behavior. Analogous to QuickCheck's shrinking but executed via frontend subprocess protocol. Try removing characters, reducing numbers toward zero, shortening arrays, removing optional object fields. Re-execute via frontend to confirm behavior is preserved. Stop when no simpler input preserves the behavior. Produces cleaner test cases, better seeds for interesting pool, more readable behavioral specs. Shares shrink_candidates(value, type_info) -> Vec<Value> function with boundary refinement. Depends on str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation infrastructure).
close_reason: Implemented minimal witness shrinking with QuickCheck-style algorithm and orchestrator integration

title: Boundary refinement phase with separate budget
description: After the discovery phase (find distinct behaviors), run a separate refinement phase that binary-searches between known inputs on opposite sides of each branch to find precise transition points. Separate budget from discovery (e.g. --refine-budget 20 per boundary, distinct from --max-executions 100). Takes discovered behaviors + witness inputs as input. For each pair of adjacent behaviors, searches for the minimal input that transitions between them. Produces precise boundary descriptions in the behavioral spec. Uses type-aware shrink candidates (same infrastructure as type-aware mutation from str-e0a4). Depends on str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation provides shrink_candidates).
close_reason: Implemented boundary refinement with separate per-boundary budget and --refine-budget CLI flag

title: Shrink candidates function (shared infrastructure)
description: Implement shrink_candidates(value: &Value, type_info: &TypeInfo) -> Vec<Value> — the inverse of mutate_inputs. Given a value, produce progressively simpler variants: Numbers: halve toward zero, try 0, 1, -1. Strings: remove last/first char, try empty, single char. Booleans: try false. Arrays: remove last/first element, try empty. Objects: remove optional fields one at a time. Nullable: try null. Shared by both minimal witness shrinking and boundary refinement. Related to but distinct from str-e0a4's mutation — mutation goes toward novel, shrinking goes toward simpler.

title: Formal methods and property-based verification
description: Parent epic grouping all formal methods work: proptest/fast-check/rapid (str-ho1d), contracts (str-xlo9), Kani (str-7c56), boundary refinement, minimal witness shrinking, shrink candidates infrastructure, fallback boundary search, invariant export, CLI subcommand for properties, and documentation.

title: Epic: Formal methods and property-based verification
description:
Parent epic for all formal methods work in Shatter.

## Shatter's own correctness
- str-ho1d: proptest (Rust), fast-check (TS), rapid (Go) — liberal usage policy
- str-xlo9: contracts crate on solver bridge — high bar, only where types can't express invariant
- str-7c56: Kani bounded model checking — deferred (P4)
- str-aslo: Go native fuzz tests — deferred (P4)

## Shrinking and boundary detection
- str-87k2: Shrink candidates function (shared infrastructure)
- str-o3hd: Minimal witness shrinking
- str-6dtq: Boundary refinement phase with separate budget
- str-ix7y: Fallback boundary search for unknown constraints

## Property output
- str-2q5h: Enrich behavioral spec with inferred invariants
- str-yfv1: CLI subcommand for properties (name TBD)

## Excluded
- TLA+: no distributed coordination in Shatter
- Alloy: deferred indefinitely
- Standalone test file generation: spec is canonical output
- Branded types in TS: not enough type confusion risk

title: Fallback boundary search for unknown/opaque constraints
description:
When Z3 returns unknown or timeout for a constraint, and Shatter has inputs on both sides of a branch, use coverage-guided binary-search-style shrinking to find the boundary concretely. Currently falls back to naive fuzz_inputs() which is blind.

Prerequisites:
- Need one input on each side (true-path witness, false-path witness)
- Need shrink_candidates infrastructure

Algorithm:
1. Start with input A (true branch) and input B (false branch)
2. Interpolate/mutate A toward B using type-aware candidates
3. Re-execute each candidate
4. Track which side of the branch each result takes
5. Converge on transition point

More effective than random fuzzing because it has directional signal. This is essentially delta debugging applied to input space.

No code reuse from proptest — shared algorithmic pattern but entirely different execution model (subprocess protocol vs in-process function calls).

Related: str-e0a4 (type-aware mutation), str-e0uy (hybrid concolic + fuzzing)
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Boundary refinement phase with separate execution budget
description:
After the discovery phase (find distinct behaviors), run a separate refinement phase that binary-searches between known inputs on opposite sides of each branch to find precise transition points.

Key design:
- SEPARATE budget from discovery (e.g. --refine-budget N per boundary, distinct from --max-executions)
- Discovery benefits from breadth (spread inputs across the domain)
- Refinement benefits from focus (narrow in on a known boundary)
- Mixing them in the same loop with the same budget means one steals from the other

Algorithm per boundary:
1. Take two inputs: one triggering behavior A, one triggering behavior B
2. Interpolate/mutate between them using shrink_candidates
3. Re-execute, track which behavior each result triggers
4. Converge on the precise transition point

Output:
- Precise boundary descriptions in behavioral spec
- Minimal inputs at each transition point
- Higher-quality seeds for the interesting pool

Shatter should prioritize finding precise boundaries — this is a distinct phase, not mixed into discovery.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Minimal witness shrinking: simplify discovered inputs
description:
After discovering an input that triggers a behavior, shrink it to the simplest input that still triggers the same behavior.

Algorithm:
1. Take an input that triggers behavior B
2. For each parameter, try shrink candidates (simpler values)
3. Re-execute via frontend; if still triggers B, keep the simpler version
4. Stop when no further simplification preserves B

Benefits:
- Cleaner, more readable test cases in export output
- Better seeds for the interesting pool (simpler seeds mutate more productively)
- More readable behavioral specs
- Minimal witnesses are the most informative examples of each behavior

Uses shrink_candidates function. No code reuse from proptest — shared algorithmic pattern (directed search via execution feedback) but execution goes through Shatter's frontend subprocess protocol, not in-process.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Shrink candidates function: type-aware input simplification
description:
Implement shrink_candidates(value: &Value, type_info: &TypeInfo) -> Vec<Value> — given a value and its type, produce progressively simpler variants. This is the inverse of mutation (toward simpler, not toward novel).

Shrink strategies by type:
- Numbers: halve toward zero, try 0, try 1, try -1
- Strings: remove last char, remove first char, try empty, try single char
- Booleans: try false
- Arrays: remove last element, remove first element, try empty
- Objects: remove optional fields one at a time
- Nullable: try null

Shared infrastructure for minimal witness shrinking and boundary refinement. Related to but distinct from str-e0a4's type-aware mutation.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)
close_reason: Implemented shrink_candidates() with type-aware shrink strategies and proptest coverage. Merged to main.

title: Add contracts crate to shatter-core solver bridge and trust boundaries
description:
Add the contracts crate (design-by-contract) to shatter-core with a HIGH bar for usage — only where Rust's type system genuinely cannot express the invariant.

## Where contracts are warranted

### Solver bridge (primary target)
Crosses Rust/Z3 FFI boundary where Rust types can't help — Z3 sorts are runtime values.

- solve_for_new_path(): Pre: all Param nodes reference valid param indices. Post: solved values have types matching ParamInfo.
- infer_sort(): Post: returned Sort matches the TypeInfo of the referenced parameter.
- overlay_solved_values(): Pre: solved_values keys are valid param indices. Post: output length equals input length. Post: each output value's JSON type matches ParamInfo's TypeInfo.
- to_z3_expr(): Pre: SymExpr tree is well-formed (no dangling Param references).

### Protocol deserialization boundary
JSON from frontend subprocesses — types can't guarantee structural validity:

- parse_execute_result(): Post: branch_ids are non-empty, param_values length matches arity.
- parse_analyze_result(): Post: function signatures have valid parameter counts.

### Orchestrator state transitions

- add_to_worklist(): Post: worklist is non-empty after add.
- mark_path_covered(): Post: covered_paths contains the new hash.

### Where contracts are NOT warranted

- Functions where the type signature already encodes the contract
- Simple transformations where proptest is more valuable
- Internal helpers with a single call site
- Any function where the pre/postcondition restates the type signature

## Relationship with Kani

Complementary: contracts document intent inline and catch violations at runtime (debug builds). Kani proves contracts hold for ALL inputs statically. Write the contract first, optionally add Kani harness later for the most critical ones.

## Documentation

Add section to shatter-core/CLAUDE.md explaining the contracts usage policy with the high bar. Include examples of good contracts (solver bridge) vs bad contracts (restating types).
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Add proptest to shatter-core, fast-check to shatter-ts, rapid to shatter-go
description:
Add property-based testing across all Shatter components. Liberal usage policy — use proptest/fast-check/rapid for any non-trivial function where interesting invariants exist.

## Rust (proptest in shatter-core)

Add proptest as a dev-dependency. Priority targets:

1. Protocol roundtrips: arbitrary Request/Response → serialize → deserialize → assert equality.
2. SymExpr sort inference: arbitrary SymExpr trees with known param types → assert infer_sort() returns correct Z3 sort. Prevents str-6ayh class of bug.
3. Input mutation type preservation: arbitrary (TypeInfo, Value) pairs → mutate → assert output matches type contract.
4. Path hash determinism: arbitrary BranchOutcome sequences → assert path_hash is deterministic.
5. Overlay solved values: arbitrary param lists and solved value maps → assert overlay preserves vector length and type compatibility.
6. Spec/export roundtrips.

### When to use proptest (guidance for contributors)

Use proptest when a function has:
- Roundtrip property (serialize/deserialize, encode/decode, parse/format)
- Invariant that should hold for ALL inputs (length preservation, type preservation, monotonicity)
- Multiple code paths that should produce equivalent results (parallel path parity)
- Boundary behavior hard to enumerate by hand
- Any function touching the solver bridge

Don't bother for: simple getters/setters, thin wrappers, tests easier as specific examples.

## TypeScript (fast-check in shatter-ts)

Add fast-check as a devDependency. Priority targets:
1. Protocol message roundtrips.
2. SymExpr builder consistency (buildSymExpr and buildSymExprWithFlow parity).
3. Instrumentor output validity.

fast-check properties are executable TypeScript using fc.property() with vitest. No DSL.

## Go (rapid in shatter-go)

Add rapid as a test dependency. Priority targets:
1. Protocol message roundtrips.
2. Executor timeout parsing (arbitrary env var strings → valid duration or default).
3. Analysis result consistency.

Also use native Go fuzzing (testing.F) for parsing/deserialization code.

## Documentation

Add sections to each component's CLAUDE.md explaining the PBT usage policy with examples of good properties and anti-patterns.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Distributable container image
description: Docker image + GH Action so users can run shatter via docker without local install

title: CLI command_run and command_error telemetry events
description: After the match cli.command block (lines 2897-3152) in main.rs: Record duration via Instant before command dispatch. On Ok(()): queue command_run event with subcommand name, detected language, function count, duration_ms, flags used (names only, extracted from parsed Cli struct not raw args). On Err(_): queue command_error event with subcommand, error_category (file_not_found | permission_denied | dir_not_found | frontend_spawn | timeout | io | config_parse | other), optional path_meta for path-related errors, duration_ms. Error categorization derived from error chain (std::io::ErrorKind::NotFound → file_not_found, PermissionDenied → permission_denied). Never include error message text (could contain paths/code).

title: Add demo/walkthrough-docker.sh for container-based walkthrough
description:
Add a container mode for the walkthrough that runs inside the distributable Docker image. This validates the actual artifact users receive, and is suitable for CI where installing Rust+Node+Go+Z3 is impractical.

Approach:

Depends on the distributable container image (str-7in9) being built first.
