Add Go flow-map primitive used for data-flow tracking. Pure data structure + merge logic, no analyzer integration yet.

Files:
- shatter-go/instrument/flow.go (new) — flowMap type (string -> *symExpr), snapshot(), mergeFlowMaps(cond, thenMap, elseMap, baseMap) that emits ite SymExpr where values diverge.
- shatter-go/instrument/flow_test.go (new) — unit tests for: simple if/else divergence, if-only (no else), nested if, chained else-if, no-divergence (no ite emitted).

No callers yet; this is the building block for str-1hlk.17.2.

See /home/ketan/.claude/plans/partitioned-orbiting-riddle.md for the full analysis.

Protocol-Visible Behavior: no (internal only)
Parity Impact: none yet (no emission until analyzer wires it up)
Default Behavior: N/A
Required Test Updates: shatter-go/instrument/flow_test.go

title: Loop snapshot locals
description: Go and Rust now emit loop_body_states with the shared loop_id and zero-based iteration contract, but their locals maps are empty. Add symbolic local snapshot contents for supported loop bodies, matching TypeScript's locals map semantics where feasible.
acceptance_criteria: Go emits locals for at least one supported counted-loop accumulator case. Rust emits locals or documents a precise unsupported reason with tests. Parity docs and conformance coverage reflect the final local snapshot behavior.

title: Go OS-level side effects
description: Go does not capture file_write, network_request, or environment_read side effects. Plan and implement OS/runtime interception or sandbox-backed observation for these side-effect kinds, then narrow or retire the remaining Go side-effect divergence.
acceptance_criteria: A design chooses the interception mechanism for file_write, network_request, and environment_read. Go execute emits supported OS-level side-effect entries or documents any permanent limitations in the parity matrix. task parity and task conformance pass.

title: Go per-call console side effects
description: Go currently captures stdout/stderr as bulk console_output entries. Add per-call console output capture with distinct log levels (log/info/warn/error/debug) to match TypeScript granularity and retire the side-effect-console-coverage divergence.
acceptance_criteria: Go execute emits per-call console_output side effects with distinct log levels. protocol/parity-matrix.yaml and protocol/PARITY.md no longer track side-effect-console-coverage for Go. task parity and task conformance pass.

title: Go+Rust execute emit loop_body_states
description: loop-body-states-typescript-only: TypeScript emits loop_body_states; Go and Rust round-trip the field but do not populate it. Add loop snapshot emission with the same loop_id/iteration contract.

title: Go+Rust frontends emit preflight_failed
description: error-code-preflight-failed-typescript-only: Add environment preflight in Go and Rust frontends that emits preflight_failed for missing dependencies/toolchain.
close_reason: both children str-1hlk.18.1 and str-1hlk.18.2 landed on main

title: TS+Rust execute consume InvocationPlan
description: ts-rust-execute-plan-not-implemented: TS and Rust accept Command::Execute.plan on the wire but do not consume it. Implement plan-aware execute when planners are added in those frontends.
notes: Deferring after inspection: TS/Rust still have no planner output path or receiver-construction dispatch surface, and this issue's own scope says to implement Execute.plan consumption when planners are added. Keeping it out of ready until those prerequisites exist.

title: Go data flow tracking emits ite SymExpr
description: ite-symexpr-production-partial: Go and Rust deserialize ite but do not produce it. Add data flow tracking to Go (Rust deferred until full analyze).

comments.author: Ketan Gangatirkar
comments.text: Decomposed: Go has no flow-map subsystem. Implementing requires building one (~260 LOC + tests across ~5 files), exceeding AGENTS.md issue sizing. See /home/ketan/.claude/plans/partitioned-orbiting-riddle.md for the original analysis and proposed plan. Filing child issues to split the work.

title: Go main target classification
description:
Zolem broad scan attempted CLI `main` entrypoints and reported target subprocess exits as scan failures.

Evidence from Zolem:
- `cmd/zolem/main.go::main` and `cmd/zolemc/main.go::main` failed with `launcher: subprocess exited unexpectedly`.
- Target output included CLI validation text such as `choose either -local-admin-addr or -local-provider`.

Exploring `func main()` entrypoints is usually low-value because they parse process flags, call `log.Fatal`, or call `os.Exit`. Shatter should classify these targets more honestly instead of treating process termination as an infrastructure failure.
acceptance_criteria:
- Go `func main()` targets are unsupported by default, or subprocess exits from CLI entrypoints are classified as target process exits rather than frontend/internal failures.
- Add Go target-language regression fixtures with a simple `func main()` and a main that calls `os.Exit` or `log.Fatal`; assert scan classification is stable and not reported as generic launcher failure.
- Non-main helper functions in the same package remain discoverable/explorable.
- Zolem main functions no longer show `launcher: subprocess exited unexpectedly` as broad-run scan failures.

title: Extend validate_artifact_references to run-shaped artifacts
description:
Follow-up to str-jeen.5. The new report_validity layer uses an invalid_artifacts_missing reason driven by a narrow per-function .md self-check, because the existing validate_artifact_references validator (in shatter-cli/src/commands/explore.rs) operates on ExploreSummary arrays and the run command does not currently emit explore-shaped TSV/JSON with artifact references.

Goal: extend validate_artifact_references (or factor a shared validator) so it also covers run-shaped artifacts. Either:
(a) have run emit explore-shaped artifact-reference rows alongside its existing per-function .md output and validate them, or
(b) parametrize the validator over an artifact-reference iterator so both commands feed in their respective shapes.

Acceptance: invalid_artifacts_missing in run's report_validity reasons fires on a richer signal than the per-function .md presence check, including missing TSV/JSON references when applicable. New tests cover at least one missing-reference scenario per command.
close_reason: Landed in merge 1fffcec9. Refactored validate_artifact_references into shape-agnostic validate_artifact_refs; run command now feeds the shared validator to surface missing per-function .md, exploration failures with empty reason token, and stale unreferenced .md extras.

title: Add -buildvcs=false to remaining go build invocations
description:
shatter-go/setup/loader.go and shatter-go/launcher/launcher.go already pass -buildvcs=false (with a regression-guard test in launcher_buildvcs_test.go) so go's VCS stamping doesn't panic when builds run outside a normal git checkout. Several other go-build sites still use the bare default and panic with 'error obtaining VCS status: exit status 128' when invoked from preview worktrees under /tmp or other partial-git contexts. Discovered during str-jeen.42 land-work preview verification: the preview worktree under /tmp tripped this and made the preview gate unusable for go-touching changes.

Sites missing -buildvcs=false (audit before fix):
- shatter-go/Taskfile.yml:11 (`go build ./...`)
- shatter-go/wrapper/wrapper_test.go (lines ~203, 273, 344, 553, 667)
- shatter-go/wrapper/wrapper_receiver_arity_test.go:214
- any other shatter-go test that invokes `exec.Command("go", "build", ...)` without the flag

Pattern already established; the fix is mechanical. The launcher has a regression test (launcher_buildvcs_test.go) that asserts the flag is present in argv — consider extending that style to other wrapper-build callers, or add a shared helper that always sets the flag.

title: Go E2E cache race
description: Default parallel cargo test can race the two Go method planner E2Es over the same .shatter-cache/go-workspace/generated path. During str-jeen.43 preview verification, after building shatter-ts, cargo test failed in go_method_planner_driven_e2e with a rename/write overlay manifest error while go_method_planner_driven_via_orchestrator ran concurrently. Re-running the same preview with -- --test-threads=1 passed. This appears unrelated to the run-summary line bucket change but makes the default Rust gate flaky.
acceptance_criteria: Default cargo test no longer races the Go method planner E2Es. The fix has a regression test or isolates generated Go cache paths per test/process. Serial test-thread workaround is no longer needed for these tests.
close_reason: Per-test SHATTER_GO_WORKSPACE_ROOT isolates the two go_method_planner_driven_* E2Es; race fixed without --test-threads=1.

title: Function name display schema
description: Clean up report schema semantics after qualified_id exists. Decide whether FunctionResult.function_name should remain a display name or become the qualified identifier with a separate display_name field, then apply the schema consistently across reports and progress-facing output.
acceptance_criteria: report.rs, batch_state.rs, html_templates.rs, progress events, and JSON tests agree on qualified_id versus display_name/function_name semantics. Backward compatibility impact is explicit in tests or release notes. Duplicate-named functions render human-readable labels without losing stable identity.

title: Flaky test: setup_manager::tests::env_override_applies_to_all_levels races on env vars
description: Under `cargo test -p shatter-core` (parallel runs), `setup_manager::tests::env_override_applies_to_all_levels` intermittently fails with `assertion left == right failed; left: 120s, right: 42s` — passes in isolation. Suggests two tests in the suite mutate the same process env var concurrently. Either serialize env-mutating tests (serial_test crate) or scope env reads through an injected provider.
close_reason: Deflaked via injected env-reading provider; 30/30 iterations pass

title: Go multi-literal case A, B: only emits first literal as constraint
description:
shatter-go/instrument/visitor.go constraintForCase (and the analyzer's mirror) only emits the first literal of a multi-literal case clause as the symbolic constraint. Example: case A, B: produces a constraint for A but not B, so the solver cannot drive a path that enters via B specifically.

Discovered while fixing str-qo1.11 (which made each clause one branch obligation; multi-literal clauses still report as one clause but lose constraint fidelity).

Acceptance: For case A, B, ...: constraints reflect the disjunction so the solver can target each literal. Decide whether the clause stays one branch obligation (current str-qo1.11 contract) or becomes multiple — likely separate sub-decision.

comments.author: Ketan Gangatirkar
comments.text: Fixed: emit disjunction tag==L1||tag==L2||... over every case literal in both instrumentor (constraintForCase) and analyzer (switchCaseConditionText). Per-clause branch obligation count unchanged (str-qo1.11 contract preserved). Added regression unit tests + examples/go/multi-literal-switch fixture. test-standard + e2e green. Landed to main.

title: Remove deprecated --timeout alias on explore
description:
Follow-up to str-qe7w: remove the now-deprecated `--timeout` alias on the `explore` subcommand. The canonical flag is `--per-function-timeout`.

Files: shatter-cli/src/args.rs (drop the hidden `timeout` field, drop `conflicts_with`, drop `resolve_per_function_timeout` helper and `DEPRECATED_TIMEOUT_WARNING` const, drop the alias-related tests). shatter-cli/src/main.rs (stop destructuring `deprecated_timeout`, inline `per_function_timeout` as `timeout` directly into `resolve_mcdc_budgets`).

Acceptance: `--timeout` no longer accepted on `explore`; `--per-function-timeout` works as before; tests pass.

title: explore --timeout flag doc-string lies (says whole-run, actually per-function default)
description:
## Summary

`shatter explore --timeout <secs>` is documented as "Timeout in seconds for the entire exploration" in `shatter-cli/src/args.rs:255-257`, but the value is actually used as the per-function default budget (passed through `resolve_function_config_with_inputs(..., cli_timeout, ...)` in `shatter-core/src/config.rs:1110-1137` which falls back to `cli_timeout` for each function's per-function `timeout` when no `.shatter/config.yaml` provides one).

A separate flag `--time-limit` (`args.rs:265-269`) handles the actual whole-run wall-clock cap. So users reading `--help` for `--timeout` get misleading information.

## Offending code

`shatter-cli/src/args.rs:255-257`:

```rust
/// Timeout in seconds for the entire exploration.
#[arg(long)]
timeout: Option<u64>,
```

The actual semantic is per-function default, used via `cli_timeout` in `shatter-core/src/config.rs:1119`.

## Acceptance criteria

Pick exactly one of the following options. Either:

(A) **Rename to match semantics**: introduce `--per-function-timeout` as the new spelling, keep `--timeout` as a deprecated alias (warn on use), and rewrite the doc to "Per-function exploration timeout in seconds (default applied when no `.shatter/config.yaml` per-function timeout is set)". Note relationship to `--timeout-explore`: today they overlap — `--timeout-explore` is the wall-clock per-function exploration timeout, while `--timeout` is the per-function execution config default. Document the distinction or merge them. (Probably worth a short brainstorm.)

(B) **Fix the doc only**: rewrite `--timeout`'s doc to "Per-function default timeout in seconds (applied when .shatter/config.yaml does not specify one)". No CLI surface change, no deprecation. Faster to ship.

Either way:
- `--help` output makes it clear what's per-function vs whole-run.
- Cross-reference `--timeout-explore` and `--time-limit` so the three timeout flags are disambiguated together.

## Notes

- Surfaced during shatter str-gz8j scope investigation. Out of scope for that issue.
- Low priority: the bug is documentation drift, not incorrect behaviour. Users who hit it via `--help` will be misled but the system still works as intended.
- See `shatter-cli/src/main.rs:228` for the call site that wires `timeout` into `resolve_mcdc_budgets` then onward to per-function config.
acceptance_criteria: Either rename --timeout to --per-function-timeout (with deprecated alias) or fix the help text to accurately describe per-function semantics. --help output disambiguates --timeout, --timeout-explore, and --time-limit.

title: Concurrency CLI knobs
description:
Expose concurrent exploration controls after the core behavior exists.

Candidate controls include observer pool size, generator thread count, and queue/batch behavior. Names should be consistent with existing Shatter parallelism/config conventions and should avoid implying concurrent requests on one frontend subprocess.

Not in scope: implementing the concurrency primitives themselves.
acceptance_criteria: Users can configure observer pool size, generator threads, and candidate queue/batch behavior through CLI/config using names consistent with existing parallelism settings. Defaults preserve current behavior. CLI parsing and config precedence tests cover the new knobs.
close_reason: Surfaced --observer-pool and --candidate-queue-capacity with CLI>config>default precedence; defaults preserved. Generator-thread knob deferred — see follow-up.

title: Candidate queue backpressure
description:
Add the bounded candidate queue policy for concurrent exploration.

The queue sits between candidate producers/generators and observer workers. It should define capacity, duplicate suppression, shutdown/drain behavior, timeout behavior, and how iteration budgets count queued versus executed candidates.

Not in scope: adding user-facing flags before the core behavior is tested.
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent exploration uses a bounded candidate queue with documented capacity defaults. Producers stop or block cleanly on timeout/cancellation. Tests cover queue draining, duplicate handling, and budget accounting.

title: Concolic solver offload
description:
Move concolic generator work out of the synchronous feedback path.

Today `Z3SolverStrategy.feedback()` solves synchronously and queues candidates before the next observe step. Introduce a scheduling seam that can run solver/generator work concurrently with frontend observation, while preserving candidate attribution and existing single-thread behavior.

Not in scope: observer pool implementation or public CLI flags.
acceptance_criteria: Z3 solving/generator work no longer blocks the observer execution hot path when the async mode is enabled. Single-threaded concolic behavior remains available. Regression tests cover generated candidate attribution and current coverage behavior.

title: Explore observer pool
description:
Add an observer pool for single-function random exploration.

Observers are frontend subprocesses. Each worker executes one candidate at a time and reports the completed observation to the shared aggregation path. This should reuse existing frontend process-pool ideas where practical, but it must stay scoped to one function's exploration loop.

Not in scope: concolic solver offload, CLI/config exposure beyond internal config needed for tests, or frontend protocol changes.
acceptance_criteria: Single-function random exploration can run with N frontend subprocess observers. `observer_pool=1` preserves current behavior. Tests cover worker checkout/return, timeout or failed worker handling, and no concurrent Execute on one frontend.

title: Observation aggregation seam
description:
Extract or introduce parallel-safe aggregation primitives for exploration observations.

The implementation should make it possible for multiple observer workers to report completed executions into shared analysis state without duplicating path, branch, progress, or raw-result accounting. Keep this independent from actually spawning multiple frontend subprocesses where practical.

Not in scope: CLI flags, async generators, or concolic solver offload.
acceptance_criteria: Observation/result aggregation is isolated from the sequential explorer loop. Aggregation handles out-of-order observations without double-counting paths, branches, iterations, or discovery attribution. Existing single-worker exploration tests continue to pass.
close_reason: Landed in merge f0fa2a5d. ObservationAggregator introduced; explore_function rewired through it. orchestrator::explore deferred to str-frc.4 (concolic offload owns it naturally — fuzz sub-loop has narrower side effects than main observation step).

title: Dedupe Go wrapper builds across frontends
description: Today N go-frontend subprocesses each invoke 'go build' on the same wrapper directory; they serialize on the workspace GOCACHE lock but still re-run the toolchain. Move wrapper compilation to a single up-front step in scan (or a per-package shared build) and have frontends exec the prebuilt artifact. Collapses N redundant toolchain runs to one and removes the cross-product that motivates str-eam2/per-language defaults. Architectural fix; tactical fixes (str-eam2, GOMAXPROCS cap) ship first.

title: Per-language parallelism defaults
description: Replace single global parallelism default with a per-language table once needed_langs is known in commands/scan.rs. Rationale: TS frontend is a light Node process; Go and Rust frontends each shell out to a multi-process toolchain (go build / cargo). A blanket cap is the wrong granularity. Suggested table: ts=nproc, go=min(nproc,8), rust=min(nproc,8), mixed=worst-case-wins. Coexists with str-eam2's [4,16] global clamp.

title: Parallelism floor/ceiling overrides
description: Add CLI flags (or config keys) for users to override the global parallelism floor and ceiling set in str-eam2. Example: --parallelism-min 2 --parallelism-max 32, with matching shatter.config.json fields. Useful for users on tiny CI runners (lower floor) or large dedicated machines with tuned GOMAXPROCS (higher ceiling). Defer until str-eam2 lands.

title: Go frontend: skip generated files by default
description: Add a default heuristic to skip generated Go files (detect via the 'Code generated ... DO NOT EDIT.' header per Go tooling conventions, and/or common filename patterns like *.pb.go, *_gen.go, zz_generated_*.go). Currently generated files are in-scope by default, producing tests for code that will be overwritten on the next regeneration. Origin: docs/go-frontend-scope-limits.md (str-hy9b.H5 scope-limits doc).

title: Go frontend: build-tag-aware analysis
description: Honor Go build tags when analyzing files and functions. Analyzer currently parses with default tags only; conditional compilation can hide functions or change function bodies, producing analyses that do not match the build the user actually ships. Origin: docs/go-frontend-scope-limits.md (str-hy9b.H5 scope-limits doc).

title: Go frontend: go.work workspace support
description: Support analyzing Go projects that use go.work multi-module workspaces. Analyzer currently assumes a single-module root; cross-module imports across workspace members are not resolved. Origin: docs/go-frontend-scope-limits.md (str-hy9b.H5 scope-limits doc).

title: Go frontend: vendor/ directory support
description: Support analyzing Go projects that resolve dependencies through vendor/ directories. Currently analyzer uses module cache / GOPATH only; imports from vendored packages are not resolved. Origin: docs/go-frontend-scope-limits.md (str-hy9b.H5 scope-limits doc).
close_reason: vendor resolution verified and documented; regression test added

title: Symbolic stub returns research
description: Research: stub return values become symbolic inputs the solver can drive. Out of v1 — implementation depends on findings. Spec: docs/specs/2026-04-17-go-frontend-redesign-v2.md §I3.
acceptance_criteria: Research note exists with viability assessment + proposed follow-up issues.

title: Loop stall fallback runs bounded unroll
description:
Run bounded symbolic unrolling only as a fallback for stalled loop frontiers. Reuse existing stall/convergence signals so ordinary branch negation remains the primary path and bounded unroll is invoked only when standard solving is not making progress on loop branches.

Cap unroll depth from named limits and solver budget rather than raw literals.
acceptance_criteria: Standard solving still runs first for loop branches; bounded unroll is attempted only for eligible stalled loop frontiers; timeout or budget caps prevent runaway solver queries; tests show fallback can produce a new input on a supported loop case that ordinary solving leaves stalled

title: shatter explore: sensible default budget for unbounded runs
description:
Follow-up surfaced during str-umw3 verification. shatter explore with no flags has no default iteration or time budget — the first verification smoke ran 8+ minutes before being killed, and had to be re-run with --max-iterations 200 to terminate in reasonable time. This is bad for a first-time user running the tool against their own code (and especially bad inside the new container image, where runaway concolic loops are harder to observe).

Possible defaults (pick one, or a combination):
- Hard iteration cap (e.g. N iterations by default, overridable)
- Wall-clock time cap (e.g. M seconds by default, overridable)
- Coverage-plateau detection: stop when branch coverage has not improved for K consecutive rounds

Coverage-plateau detection is conceptually related to str-e0uy (hybrid concolic + coverage-guided fuzzing handoff heuristic), so whatever lands here should be reusable there.

Acceptance:
- shatter explore /path/to/file.ts with no extra flags terminates in bounded time on typical inputs.
- Existing flags (--max-iterations etc.) still override the default.
- The chosen default is documented in CLI --help output and in the README / walkthrough.
- The walkthrough continues to pass.

Related: str-umw3 (discovered during container verification), str-e0uy (shared heuristic).

title: Container: non-root user for bind-mounted outputs
description:
Follow-up from str-umw3. The Dockerfile has no USER directive, so processes inside run as UID 0. When the container writes into the bind-mounted /work (for .shatter/ caches and shatter-artifacts/ outputs), those files end up owned by root:root on the host — a regular host user then can't delete or edit them without sudo. Hit during str-umw3 verification; had to rm -rf from another container.

Options:
1. Document the docker run --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" ... pattern in docker/README.md so users pass their host UID/GID at runtime. Cheapest fix.
2. Bake a non-root USER into the image and make the entrypoint resilient to not knowing the host UID. Harder — the canonical Linux answer is either --user at runtime or an entrypoint that chowns outputs before exiting.

Acceptance:
- A user running the canonical docker run -v "$PWD:/work" shatter explore ... command on a Linux host does not end up with root-owned artifacts they cannot delete.
- docker/README.md documents the expected file-ownership behavior and whatever the chosen mechanism is.

Related: str-umw3 (parent containerization effort).
close_reason: Documented --user flag pattern in docker/README.md with dedicated File Ownership section; entrypoint already handles non-root UIDs

title: TS execute supports import.meta
description:
## Summary

TypeScript files that reference `import.meta` analyze successfully but fail during execute because the Shatter TS harness runs in a non-ESM context where `import.meta` is unavailable. This blocks exploration of common Vite-style code that reads `import.meta.env`.

## Protocol-Visible Behavior

- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected:
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

## Parity Impact

- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExprWithFlow` to handle the same node type
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExpr` to handle the same node type
- [ ] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) — verify concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) — verify random explorer (`explorer.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` — verify both `--concolic` and default explorer paths receive the new config or flag

## Default Behavior

- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it:

## Required Test Updates

- [ ] Unit tests added or updated
- [ ] Property tests (proptest / fast-check / rapid) added for any new public function
- [ ] E2E concolic tests verified (`cargo test --test e2e_concolic`)
- [ ] Conformance tests verified (`npx task conformance`) — required if protocol-visible
- [ ] Walkthrough updated (`demo/walkthrough.sh`) if the compact demo narrative changes
- [ ] Gauntlet updated (`demo/gauntlet.sh`) or equivalent targeted coverage added for non-demo CLI changes

## Contract Updates

- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? no
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them (Z3 FFI / subprocess JSON / cross-collection index):
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: trust boundary, type gap, silent corruption (see `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md`)

## Steps to Reproduce

Affected file example:

- `web/src/lib/posthog.ts`

```bash
shatter explore web/src/lib/posthog.ts --max-iterations 5
```

Observed failure:

```text
Execute failed: Cannot use 'import.meta' outside a module
```

`--analyze-only` succeeds; the failure is specific to execute.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Files that reference `import.meta` either execute successfully in the TS harness or fail early with a deterministic structured error before exploration time is wasted.
- The chosen handling supports common `import.meta.env` access patterns used by Vite projects.
- Regression coverage includes an `import.meta` execute case.

title: Shared walkthrough manifest
description:
## Protocol-Visible Behavior

- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? yes

## Parity Impact

Which parallel code paths are affected? Check all that apply and confirm the parallel path is updated too.

- [ ] `buildSymExpr` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExprWithFlow` to handle the same node type
- [ ] `buildSymExprWithFlow` (instrumentor.ts) — update `buildSymExpr` to handle the same node type
- [x] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) — verify concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) gets the same change
- [x] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) — verify random explorer (`explorer.rs`) gets the same change
- [x] CLI wiring in `main.rs` — verify both `--concolic` and default explorer paths receive the new config or flag

## Default Behavior

- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

## Required Test Updates

- [ ] Unit tests added or updated
- [ ] Property tests (proptest / fast-check / rapid) added for any new public function
- [ ] E2E concolic tests verified (`cargo test --test e2e_concolic`)
- [ ] Conformance tests verified (`npx task conformance`) — required if protocol-visible
- [ ] Walkthrough updated (`demo/walkthrough.sh`) — required if a CLI command is added or changed

## Contract Updates

- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? no
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them (Z3 FFI / subprocess JSON / cross-collection index): N/A
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: trust boundary, type gap, silent corruption (see `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md`)

## Notes

Refactor local and Docker walkthrough runners to consume shared step definitions so they stop drifting.

Scope:
- One source of truth for step ordering, titles, per-step assets, and persistence requirements.
- Thin local and Docker executors.
- Docker/local differences are declarative rather than hand-maintained duplication.

Acceptance criteria:
- Step inventory lives in one place.
- Adding or removing a walkthrough step updates both runners together.
- Remaining Docker-specific differences are encoded as explicit metadata, not copy-pasted script divergence.

title: Prepared target shutdown cleanup
description:
Release prepared execute state on frontend shutdown so cached harness subprocesses and temporary directories do not accumulate across sessions. Keep this issue focused on cleanup behavior for already-prepared targets rather than broader lifecycle redesign.

Protocol-Visible Behavior
- Does this change the wire format or semantics of any protocol message? no
- If yes, which commands or fields are affected: N/A
- Is this change backward-compatible? N/A

Parity Impact
- [ ] buildSymExpr (instrumentor.ts) — update buildSymExprWithFlow to handle the same node type
- [ ] buildSymExprWithFlow (instrumentor.ts) — update buildSymExpr to handle the same node type
- [ ] Random explorer (`shatter-core/src/explorer.rs`) — verify concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] Concolic orchestrator (`shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs`) — verify random explorer (`explorer.rs`) gets the same change
- [ ] CLI wiring in `main.rs` — verify both `--concolic` and default explorer paths receive the new config or flag

Default Behavior
- Is new functionality enabled by default? yes
- If no, what flag or config option enables it: N/A

Required Test Updates
- [x] Unit tests added or updated
- [ ] Property tests (proptest / fast-check / rapid) added for any new public function
- [ ] E2E concolic tests verified (`cargo test --test e2e_concolic`)
- [ ] Conformance tests verified (`npx task conformance`) - required if protocol-visible
- [ ] Walkthrough updated (`demo/walkthrough.sh`) - required if a CLI command is added or changed

Contract Updates
- Are runtime contracts (`#[requires]` / `#[ensures]`) needed? no
- If yes, identify the trust boundary that justifies them: N/A
- Confirm all three qualifying criteria are met: N/A
acceptance_criteria:
Frontend shutdown closes cached prepared harness subprocesses.
Temporary directories or build outputs owned by the prepared-target lifecycle are cleaned up according to policy.
Tests cover normal shutdown and at least one dead-process cleanup case.

title: Harness docs align wording
description:
Align the written harness plans and docs with the resolved architecture decisions.

Scope:
- correct overstated Rust `pub(crate)` claims in the newer plans
- document `bin_only` default vs `crate_bridge` opt-in for Rust
- document lifecycle-specific storage, standalone fallback, and deferred persistent subprocess work
acceptance_criteria:
- Planning/docs no longer claim full `pub(crate)` support for Rust `bin_only` mode.
- Docs clearly describe Rust mode boundaries and standalone fallback behavior.
- Docs reflect lifecycle-specific storage and deferred persistent subprocesses.

title: TS: replace structuredClone + JSON.stringify in global_state_change detection
description:
The global_state_change side effect detection in executor.ts (lines ~1038-1090) uses structuredClone to snapshot exported module variables before execution, then JSON.stringify on both before and after values to detect changes. Both operations are unnecessarily expensive.

**structuredClone cost:** Implements the HTML structured clone algorithm — cycle detection (maintains an identity Map of every visited object), handles ArrayBuffer/SharedArrayBuffer byte-level copy, Map/Set/Date/RegExp/Error/Blob/File/ImageBitmap, recursive traversal of every enumerable own property. For Shatter's use case (module-level exports which are almost always primitives, plain objects, or arrays), this is massive overkill. The cycle detection alone adds O(n) memory overhead.

**JSON.stringify comparison cost:** Serializes both before and after to strings just to compare them. O(n) in the size of the object graph, twice. Also lossy: undefined → omitted, functions → omitted, NaN → null, Infinity → null, BigInt → throws. If a global changes from undefined to being omitted, JSON.stringify won't see it. If a global is a BigInt, the comparison throws.

**Three options, in order of increasing sophistication:**

1. **Quick win — util.isDeepStrictEqual + JSON-roundtrip snapshot**: Replace structuredClone with JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) for the snapshot (already losing the same information as the comparison, so no fidelity regression), and replace JSON.stringify comparison with util.isDeepStrictEqual (faster for early-exit on first difference, handles more types correctly). Downside: still O(n) snapshot even when nothing changes.

2. **Medium — conditional snapshot**: Most function executions don't mutate globals. Check whether the function body references any exported variable names (static analysis during instrumentation). If not, skip snapshotting entirely. When snapshotting is needed, use option 1.

3. **Best — Proxy-based dirty tracking**: Wrap each exported variable in a Proxy that sets a dirty flag on set/defineProperty/deleteProperty. Only snapshot (via any method) when at least one dirty flag is set. Cost: one Proxy allocation per export at module load time, zero cost per execute when globals aren't mutated (the common case). Snapshot only the dirty variables, not all of them.

This is part of the side effect capture pipeline. The SideEffect::GlobalStateChange variant is one of the 7 canonical kinds defined in shatter-core/src/execution_record.rs.
acceptance_criteria:
structuredClone is no longer used for global state snapshotting.
JSON.stringify is no longer used for global state comparison.
global_state_change side effects are still correctly detected (before/after values included in the side effect payload).
BigInt globals don't throw.
No regression in existing property tests or E2E tests.
notes: Side effect tracking (including global_state_change) is typically disabled during normal exploration. The structuredClone + JSON.stringify cost only matters when side effects are explicitly enabled, which is the minority case. Lower priority accordingly — correctness fixes (BigInt throwing) still matter but performance is not urgent.

title: Use cargo check for harness validation before full build
description:
When validating that a harness compiles (e.g., during instrumentation validation or early error detection), use cargo check instead of cargo build. cargo check is ~3x faster because it skips codegen and linking — it only runs type checking and borrow checking.

Flow: instrument → write harness → cargo check (fast validation) → if check passes and execution needed → cargo build (full compile).

This is most valuable for the 'instrument then report errors' path where we want fast feedback on whether the instrumented code is valid, without paying the full build cost.
acceptance_criteria:
Instrumentation validation uses cargo check, not cargo build.
Validation-only path is measurably faster than full build path.
Full build still runs when actual execution is needed.

title: Precompiled harness template library
description:
Factor out the common harness boilerplate (stdin JSON reading, runtime reset, panic catching, result serialization, timing, side effect capture) into a precompiled library crate/package. Per-function harness binaries become thin wrappers that link against this library, containing only the function call and type deserialization logic.

This shrinks the per-function compilation unit from ~100 lines to ~10 lines, reducing incremental compile time for new functions.

Rust: create a shatter-harness-runtime crate with the execute loop, JSON protocol, and result serialization. Per-function binary just calls harness_runtime::run(|inputs| { my_crate::my_fn(inputs.get::<i64>(0)) }).

Go: create a harness package with the same structure. Per-function cmd/ binary calls harness.Run(func(inputs []json.RawMessage) { ... }).
acceptance_criteria:
Common harness logic is in a shared library, not duplicated per function.
Adding a new function harness compiles only the thin wrapper, not the boilerplate.
Incremental compile time for a new function is measurably reduced vs current approach.

title: Parallel harness compilation across functions
description:
When exploring multiple functions, compile all harness binaries in parallel rather than sequentially. For Rust, pass multiple --bin or --test targets to a single cargo build invocation so Cargo parallelizes across cores. For Go, run multiple go build commands concurrently.

Impact: first-function cold start stays ~15-30s, but all-functions cold start drops from N*30s to ~30s + small marginal cost per function (mostly linking).

This matters most for scan mode (many functions) and walkthrough (4+ functions).
acceptance_criteria:
Multiple harness binaries are compiled in parallel when possible.
Scan/walkthrough of N functions has sub-linear total compile time.
No race conditions on shared build artifacts (Cargo handles this; Go needs separate output dirs or sequential linking).

title: Medium-confidence heuristics for opaque type detection
description:
Add medium-confidence heuristics for detecting opaque types via static analysis. These supplement the high-confidence heuristics (str-io5d) with signals that are suggestive but not definitive.

## Heuristics

### 1. Infrastructure package prefix matching
Types imported from known infrastructure packages are almost always opaque. A curated list of package prefixes:
- **Go**: `database/sql`, `net/http`, `cloud.google.com/go`, `github.com/go-redis`, `github.com/jackc/pgx`, `github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go`, etc.
- **TypeScript**: `pg`, `redis`, `ioredis`, `mongoose`, `typeorm`, `@aws-sdk/*`, `nodemailer`, etc.

This is an expanded version of today's per-type hardcoded lists, applied at the package level. Easier to maintain and catches new types from known packages automatically.

### 2. Implements Closeable / Disposable
Types implementing cleanup interfaces often hold external resources:
- **Go**: `io.Closer`, `io.ReadCloser`, `io.WriteCloser`
- **TypeScript**: `Disposable` (TC39 explicit resource management), or classes with a `close()`/`dispose()`/`destroy()` method

Not definitive — a `StringReader` might implement `io.Closer` but be perfectly synthesizable. Use as supporting evidence, not standalone.

### 3. Native handle fields
If a type's fields (where visible) include names/types suggesting OS handles: `fd`, `handle`, `FileDescriptor`, `unsafe.Pointer`, `*C.something`. Indicates the type wraps native state.

## Confidence model
These should be reported at lower confidence than str-io5d heuristics. In the learning mode (str-gtrv), medium-confidence static signals can upgrade a tier-2 (execution-only) suggestion partway but should not by themselves produce a tier-1 suggestion. They serve as supporting evidence.

## Dependencies
- str-io5d (high-confidence heuristics — build on the same infrastructure)
- str-asnl (diagnostics format — categories used in reporting)

title: Askama for Markdown output
description:
Migrate the existing MiniJinja Markdown templates to Askama, unifying all template rendering on a single engine.

## Scope

1. Convert shatter-cli/templates/explore_fn.md.j2 and scan.md.j2 to Askama templates
2. Convert the view models in shatter-cli/src/render.rs to Askama #[derive(Template)] structs
3. Remove MiniJinja dependency from shatter-cli
4. Terminal Markdown output must be identical (snapshot tests)

## Rationale

After str-heiu (Askama for HTML), the project would have two template engines. This consolidates on Askama for compile-time checking across all templates.

## Acceptance criteria

- MiniJinja dependency removed
- Askama renders all Markdown output
- Existing terminal output unchanged
- All existing render tests pass
acceptance_criteria: MiniJinja removed; Askama renders Markdown; terminal output unchanged

title: Perf recurring cadence
description: Document and automate the weekly and monthly external profiling loop so Shatter regularly produces actionable performance insights. Include baseline retention, regression triage, and follow-up issue creation guidance.
acceptance_criteria: Weekly run procedure is documented; monthly review procedure is documented; regression triage checklist is documented; follow-up issue template or process is defined
notes: Merged locally into main at 51d2935. Not pushed yet; leaving issue open/in_progress. Pre-completion is still blocked by the local E2E environment error from z3-sys missing z3-4.16.0.

title: Rust checks use nextest
description: Reduce Rust pre-push latency by preferring cargo-nextest for the main workspace test run when available, while keeping a clear fallback to cargo test. Measure or report the selected runner so local behavior is obvious.
acceptance_criteria: Hook output makes it clear which Rust test runner was used.

title: Loop analysis: state merging at backedges
description:
Merge per-iteration symbolic states using ITE (if-then-else) chains. Instead of N negation candidates per loop, produce one merged formula where iteration count is a Z3 free variable.

Key components:
- SymExpr::Ite {condition, then_expr, else_expr} variant (pervasive — every match arm)
- Z3 ITE conversion in solver.rs using Z3's native ite()
- merge_loop_states() in loop_analysis.rs: build ITE chains from per-iteration constraints
- MergedLoopState type: loop_id, iteration_var, merged_constraint, iteration_count
- Iteration variable bounded: 0 <= __loop_L_iter < N
- Cap merge depth at 10 to limit Z3 formula size

MEDIUM EFFORT — changes 5-6 files. Depends on technique 5 (induction variable analysis) for loop_id-to-constraint mapping.
notes: See docs/plans/loop-analysis-compiler-techniques.md — Technique 6. Deferred. Depends on technique 5 (induction variables).

title: Loop analysis: induction variable + trip count analysis
description:
Recognize canonical counted loops (for (i = 0; i < n; i++)), extract induction variable metadata (init, step, bound), and encode i == k directly in Z3 instead of building k prefix backedge constraints. Collapses O(k) constraints per iteration into O(1).

Key components:
- Protocol types: InductionVar {name, init_expr, step_expr, bound_expr, bound_op}, LoopInfo {loop_id, line, induction_var}
- FunctionAnalysis.loops: Vec<LoopInfo> (new field, backward compatible)
- loop_analysis.rs (NEW): rewrite_loop_constraints() — replace backedge constraints with direct i == init + step * iteration
- TS instrumentor: analyzeForLoopInductionVar() for canonical ForStatement recognition
- Go frontend: analyzeForStmtInductionVar()
- Conservative: only match when induction variable is unmodified in body; defer float induction variables

MEDIUM EFFORT — changes 7-8 files across core + all frontends.
notes: See docs/plans/loop-analysis-compiler-techniques.md — Technique 5. Deferred. No dependencies on techniques 1-4.

title: CLI main decomposition
description: shatter-cli/src/main.rs is a very large mixed-responsibility unit (command parsing, orchestration, frontend build logic, and test blocks). This increases review cost and change coupling.
acceptance_criteria: Split main.rs into focused modules (command args, command handlers, shared helpers); keep behavior unchanged with existing tests green; move parser/command tests into dedicated test modules per command area
close_reason: CLI main.rs decomposed into focused modules

title: Mock invalidation and callee re-exploration during scan
description:
The scan orchestrator explores callees before callers (topological order) and uses callee behavior maps as mocks when exploring callers. If a caller invokes a callee with inputs outside the callee's explored behavior map, the mock returns a fabricated response — the caller's exploration proceeds on a false assumption.

## Detection

During caller exploration, when the executor dispatches a call through a mock, check whether the input falls within the callee's behavior map domain. If not, record a "mock miss" event: (callee_name, missed_inputs, caller_execution_id).

## Response options (from lightweight to heavyweight)

1. **Log and report**: surface mock misses in the scan report so the user knows which callee behaviors are assumed, not observed. No re-exploration — cheapest option, still valuable.
2. **Deferred re-exploration**: after the current layer completes, re-explore callees that had mock misses using the missed inputs as seeds. Then re-explore affected callers with updated mocks. Adds a "fixup" pass after each layer.
3. **Iterative convergence**: repeat layers until no new mock misses occur (fixed-point). Guaranteed to terminate if the behavior map is monotonically growing, but could be expensive.

## Scope

Start with option 1 (detection + reporting). Option 2 is the sweet spot for correctness vs cost. Option 3 is likely overkill for most codebases.

## Relationship to existing issues

- str-7o20 (cross-function seeding) propagates interesting inputs backward through the call graph. That's complementary — it seeds exploration proactively, while this issue detects when mocks are wrong reactively.
- str-7w5 (mocking epic) owns the mock generation pipeline that this feature validates.

title: Dynamic budget reallocation across functions during scan
description:
The scan orchestrator gives each function a fixed budget (max_iterations_per_function, timeout_per_fn). If a function reaches full coverage or plateaus early, its remaining budget is wasted. Redistribute unused budget to functions that are still discovering new paths.

## Mechanism

Track per-function exploration outcomes during parallel_scan. When a function terminates early (WorklistExhausted, CoveragePlateau, or full branch coverage), compute its unused budget as (allocated_executions - actual_executions). Feed that surplus into a shared atomic counter. Functions still exploring can request additional budget from the surplus when they exhaust their initial allocation but are still finding new paths.

## Design considerations

- **Surplus accounting**: track by execution count, not wall time — time-based reallocation is harder to reason about with parallel workers.
- **Claim policy**: a function should only claim surplus if its recent hit rate (new paths / last N executions) exceeds a threshold. Don't let a stalled function consume the entire surplus.
- **Layer boundaries**: surplus resets at each topological layer boundary. Budget from layer 0 functions should not carry over to layer 1 — the functions are structurally different.
- **Interaction with str-l22q (stall detection)**: a function that has abandoned all its frontiers should release budget even if its execution count hasn't reached the cap.

## Why this matters

In practice, simple leaf functions (validators, formatters) often hit full coverage in <10 executions while complex functions with opaque constraints need hundreds. Fixed budgets waste most of the compute on functions that don't need it.

Analogous to str-l22q (frontier-level abandonment) but operates at the function level within a scan.

title: strace-based dep discovery tool
description: Optional shatter discover-deps --strace command that uses strace to catalog all network syscalls (connect, sendto, sendmsg, socket ~10-15 syscalls) made by a function. One-shot diagnostic tool, not for runtime interception. Useful for finding deps that static analysis and runtime detection both miss.
acceptance_criteria: strace-based discovery lists network syscalls with source info. Works on Linux. Clearly documented as diagnostic-only.
close_reason: Merged to main. strace-based discover-deps CLI command.

title: Go frontend mock parity
description: Extend Go generateMockFile for per-execution mock variation, ThrowError support (panic or error return), discovered_dependencies reporting, and custom generator integration. Go already receives mocks per Execute call so no architectural change needed.
acceptance_criteria: Go frontend varies mocks per execution. ThrowError produces panic/error. Discovered deps reported. E2E test with Go function and dynamic mocks.
close_reason: Go mock parity: error-return variant, behavior constants, property tests, fuzz tests.

title: Refactoring recs for hard-to-mock deps
description: Detect hard-to-mock calls (dynamic dispatch, closures, subprocess spawning, multi-layer indirection). Produce refactoring recommendations in scan output: extract to parameter, wrap in named function, move behind interface.
acceptance_criteria: Hard-to-mock calls detected. Recommendations appear in scan report. Suggestions are actionable (extract, wrap, interface).
close_reason: Merged to main. MockabilityAssessment + refactoring recs in scan reports.

title: Go native fuzz tests for shatter-go parsing code
description: Add Go 1.18+ native fuzzing (testing.F) to shatter-go for all input parsing and deserialization code. Separate from rapid property tests (which test logical properties) — native fuzzing finds panics and crashes on malformed input. Covers protocol message parsing (handler.go), any query string / env var parsing. Seed corpus from existing test cases. Could be part of str-ho1d or standalone.

title: CLI subcommand for property/invariant export
description: Add a CLI subcommand (NOT export — that's taken; something like 'shatter properties' or 'shatter infer') that outputs discovered invariants and behavioral properties. Distinct from shatter export (which generates regression test files). Outputs to behavioral spec format (YAML) enriched with property descriptions. Could eventually support language-specific codegen (--format=proptest, --format=fast-check, --format=rapid) but the YAML spec is the primary output. Depends on invariant export in spec. Relates to str-pu5 (CLI subcommands), str-wt7 (staged pipeline).

title: Inferred invariant export as property descriptions in behavioral spec
description: Extend the behavioral spec format to include inferred invariants from invariants.rs as property descriptions. These are the logical properties Shatter discovers (idempotency, monotonicity, roundtrip, bounds) expressed declaratively in the spec YAML. Example: function: validateEmail, invariants: empty string always throws ValidationError, result is always boolean when no exception, string without @ returns false. NOT generating executable test files — just enriching the spec with discovered properties. The spec is the canonical language-neutral output. Depends on invariants.rs being connected to the spec pipeline.

title: CLI subcommand for discovered properties and invariants (name TBD)
description:
Add a CLI subcommand that outputs discovered invariants and behavioral properties. NOT 'export' (already taken for regression test files). Candidate names: 'shatter properties', 'shatter infer', or extending 'shatter spec' with a --properties flag.

Outputs to behavioral spec format (YAML) enriched with property descriptions. Primary output is language-neutral spec, not executable test code.

Could eventually support language-specific codegen (--format=proptest, --format=fast-check, --format=rapid) but the YAML spec is the primary output and that's lower priority.

Related: str-pu5 (CLI subcommands), str-wt7 (staged pipeline)
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Enrich behavioral spec with inferred invariant properties
description:
Extend the behavioral spec format to include inferred invariants from invariants.rs as property descriptions. These are logical properties Shatter discovers (idempotency, monotonicity, roundtrip, bounds) expressed declaratively in the spec YAML.

Example output:
  function: validateEmail
  invariants:
    - property: 'empty string always throws ValidationError'
    - property: 'result is always boolean when no exception'
    - property: 'string without @ returns false'

NOT generating executable test files. The spec is the canonical language-neutral output. Properties are descriptions, not executable code.

Connects invariants.rs inference to the spec pipeline so discovered properties appear in shatter spec output.
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)

title: Add --changed/--since to walkthrough demo
description: Once --changed/--since is implemented and examples are in a separate repo, add a walkthrough step exercising incremental scan. The separate repo gives examples their own commit history, making --since demos meaningful. Depends on both the --changed implementation and the examples repo split.

title: Move example programs to a separate repository
description: The examples/ directory serves as both E2E test fixtures and demo material. Moving to a separate repo enables: cleaner commit history for examples, natural --since demos, community contributions. Requires: updating E2E tests to fetch examples, walkthrough to clone/manage the repo, CI to pull examples. Cross-repo versioning must be solved.

title: PostHog integration for telemetry flush (Phase 2)
description: Server-side analytics via PostHog (recommended backend — MIT self-hostable, free cloud tier 1M events/mo, built-in dashboards/funnels/retention). Add posthog-rs or ureq dependency to shatter-cli. Implement flush_queue(): read JSONL, batch POST to PostHog /capture endpoint, truncate queue on HTTP 2xx. 2-second flush timeout, fire-and-forget after command completes. SHATTER_TELEMETRY_DEBUG=1 env var to print events to stderr instead of sending. SHATTER_TELEMETRY_URL env var for endpoint override (testing). Batch max 100 events per flush, respect PostHog rate limits. PostHog project setup (cloud free tier initially, self-host option documented). Alternatives evaluated: Segment (overpowered/proprietary), Amplitude (no Rust SDK), custom server (too much work), telemetry-kit (operational overhead). See docs/plans/telemetry-anonymous-usage-bad-args.md for full comparison.

title: Static heuristic: infer integer type refinement from usage patterns in source
description:
## Feature

Complement the behavioral float probe (str-1hnm) with a static analysis heuristic that infers whether a `number` parameter is integer-intended based on how it's used in the function body. This runs during the analyze phase (before any execution) and provides an initial type refinement signal.

## Signals (strongest to weakest)

1. **All compared-against literals are integers**: `n <= 0`, `n > 3999` — no fractional comparisons
2. **Integer-domain arithmetic**: `remaining -= value` where `value` comes from an integer-only lookup table
3. **Integer coercion calls**: `Math.floor(n)`, `Math.trunc(n)`, `n | 0`, `n >>> 0`, `parseInt(n)` suggest the function expects or normalizes to integers
4. **Function/parameter naming**: name contains `int`, `count`, `index`, `length`, `size`, `offset`, `id`
5. **JSDoc annotations**: `@param {integer}` or comments mentioning 'integer'
6. **No fractional operations**: absence of `.toFixed()`, `% 1`, `Math.round()`, etc. (weak negative signal)

## Output

During analysis, if the heuristic fires, refine the `TypeInfo` from `Float` to `Int` for that parameter. This feeds into input generation (the generator already produces integers for `Int` params) without needing the behavioral probe.

## Relationship to float probe

- Static heuristic: fast, runs during analyze, no execution needed, but can be wrong (false positives from range-check functions)
- Behavioral probe (str-1hnm): slower, requires execution, but definitive
- When both are available, the behavioral probe overrides the static heuristic
- When only analysis is available (e.g. `--analyze-only`), the static heuristic is the only signal

## Scope

TypeScript only initially. Go and Rust have proper integer types so this is primarily a JS/TS concern. Could extend to Python (`float` vs `int` ambiguity) later.

## Configuration

- `analyze.integer_heuristic.enabled`: bool (default: true)
- `analyze.integer_heuristic.min_signals`: minimum number of signals required to refine (default: 2)
notes: Complementary to behavioral float probe: str-1hnm. Config system: str-nh7l.

title: Add --set flag for dotted-path configuration overrides
description:
## Feature

The CLI has accumulated many top-level flags (~60+ `#[arg(long)]` entries). Not every tuning parameter deserves a dedicated flag — obscure settings like float probe thresholds, genetic algorithm weights, or loop bucket boundaries should be settable without polluting the flag namespace.

## Design

Add a `--set key=value` flag (repeatable) that accepts dotted-path keys mapping to the YAML config structure:

```bash
shatter explore --set explore.float_probe.threshold=0.9 --set explore.max_iterations=200 target.ts:fn
```

### Key rules
- Paths correspond to the `ShatterConfig` YAML structure: `defaults.max_iterations`, `explore.float_probe.threshold`, etc.
- Values are parsed as the target field's type (number, bool, string)
- `--set` overrides take precedence over YAML config files (but below explicit CLI flags)

### Precedence (highest to lowest)
1. Dedicated CLI flag (`--max-iterations 200`)
2. `--set` override (`--set defaults.max_iterations=200`)
3. Nearest `.shatter/config.yaml`
4. Built-in defaults

### Relationship to dedicated flags
Some settings have BOTH a dedicated flag and a `--set` path. The dedicated flag is sugar for the common case; `--set` is the uniform access method. Example:
- `--max-iterations 200` is equivalent to `--set defaults.max_iterations=200`
- `--exec-timeout 5` is equivalent to `--set defaults.exec_timeout=5`

### Implementation
- Add `--set` as a global clap arg: `Vec<String>` with `key=value` parsing
- Parse dotted paths into the `ShatterConfig` struct using serde or manual traversal
- Merge `--set` values into the resolved config after YAML loading but before applying dedicated CLI flag overrides

## Follow-up
Audit all existing CLI flags to identify which should remain top-level vs move to `--set`-only. Candidates for demotion: `--loop-buckets`, `--solver-timeout`, `--no-boundary-values`, and any future float-probe or genetic-algorithm tuning parameters.

title: Rust native file handle generator
description:
Native Rust generator that creates std::fs::File handles via tempfile crate. Returns file handle via sentinel in handle table. Recipe stores path + content descriptor.

Source: examples/generators/native-rust/file_handle.rs
Opaque types covered: File, BufReader<File>, BufWriter<File>

BLOCKED: Rust frontend execute is unimplemented (returns 'not yet implemented'). This issue cannot be completed until Rust execute is implemented.
notes: Epic: str-m6rs

title: Formal vector/value strategy decomposition
description: Refactor InputStrategy trait (strategy.rs) into vector-level and value-level tiers. Vector strategies operate on the full input vector (crossover, parameter drilling, pool seeding). Value strategies operate on individual values (char mutation, havoc, fragment injection). GA operates at both levels — vector-level for recombination, value-level for mutation. Document which existing strategies belong at which level. Depends on str-7stb (input strategy decomposition) being further along.

title: Byte-level mutation for binary buffers only
description: AFL-style bit flips, byte arithmetic, block insertion/deletion. Activates ONLY for bitstrings and byte buffers (Uint8Array, []byte, Buffer), NOT for strings. Strings get structure-aware string mutation instead. Independent.

title: Havoc mode: compound multi-mutation sequences
description: New mutation operator that applies 2-5 random mutations in sequence (inspired by AFL's havoc stage). Compound mutations find deeper paths than single edits. Applicable to all types, not just strings. For strings, composes fragment injection, char substitution, substring duplication, and crossover operations. Depends on fragment injection and str-ek5g (string crossover).

title: Substring duplication mutation operator
description: New mutation operator in mutate_string: copy a random slice of the string and insert it at another random position. Finds length-sensitive bugs and repeated-pattern paths. Independent of other mutation operators.

title: Coverage-guided frontier prioritization
description: Order frontiers by: depth (deeper = more likely to reach novel behavior), stall state (deprioritize stalled frontiers), constraint availability (boost frontiers where blocking parameter has a known symbolic constraint — solver can help). Different workers in distributed farming could be assigned different frontiers for natural parallelism.

title: Stall detection and frontier abandonment
description: Track stall_count per frontier — increment when drilling produces no new paths after each iteration. When stall_count exceeds a configurable threshold (named constant FRONTIER_STALL_THRESHOLD), abandon the frontier and move exploration budget to the next-priority frontier. Prevents infinite investment in unpromising frontiers.

title: Slow nondeterminism heuristics for dates, timestamps, and counters
description: Type-based detection for slow nondeterminism: values that are deterministic within a single run but vary across runs. Examples: today's date as formatted string, monotonic auto-increment IDs, environment-dependent values (hostname, PID). Applied at comparison time without extra execution. Uses value-pattern heuristics (ISO date, Unix timestamp) combined with cross-run structural similarity to identify and flag.

title: Interactive nondeterminism review CLI command
description: Add 'shatter nondeterminism review' command. Presents suspected-nondeterministic fields from the most recent scan, one at a time, with evidence type and sample value. User confirms (y), rejects (n), skips (s), or re-runs to check (?). Confirmed/rejected entries saved to .shatter/config.yaml. On subsequent scans, only new or escalated candidates are shown.

title: SSA phi-nodes for conditional reassignment in symbolic data flow
description:
buildSymExprWithFlow tracks the last assignment to a variable on the concrete execution path. This loses precision for conditional reassignment:

  let x = a;
  if (cond) x = b;
  use(x)  // symbolic value is just 'b' if that branch was taken, 'a' otherwise

With SSA, this becomes x₂ = φ(x₀, x₁) which maps to ite(cond, b, a). Z3 handles ite natively, so the solver could reason about both branches simultaneously.

Current behavior: correct for the concrete path taken (concolic sees the right value), but the symbolic expression doesn't capture the relationship to the branch condition. When the solver negates a downstream constraint, it doesn't know that x's value depends on cond.

Implementation:
- Track assignment versions per variable in the data flow map (x_0, x_1, ...)
- At control flow merge points (after if/else, switch), insert phi entries: x_merge = ite(branch_condition, x_then, x_else)
- Emit the ite SymExpr so Z3 can reason about both possibilities

Applies to all frontends:
- TS: let/var reassignment inside if/else/switch blocks
- Go: any variable reassignment inside if/else/switch
- Rust: less common due to immutability defaults, but let mut reassignment in branches applies. Deferred until execute lands.

Key files: shatter-ts/src/instrumentor.ts, shatter-go/instrument/

title: Support destructuring in buildSymExprWithFlow data flow tracking
description:
buildSymExprWithFlow in instrumentor.ts tracks variable assignments for symbolic data flow, but doesn't handle destructuring patterns. const {a, b} = param should register a → {kind: 'param', name: 'param', path: ['a']} and b → {kind: 'param', name: 'param', path: ['b']} in the data flow map.

Applies to all three frontends:
- TS: destructuring in const/let/var declarations and function params
- Go: no destructuring syntax, but multiple return values (a, b := fn()) are analogous — currently not tracked
- Rust: pattern matching in let bindings (let (a, b) = tuple, let Struct { field } = val) and match arms. Rust's pattern matching is pervasive and more expressive than TS/Go destructuring. However, the Rust frontend's execute is unimplemented, so this is deferred until execute lands.

Key files: shatter-ts/src/instrumentor.ts (buildSymExprWithFlow, data flow map construction), shatter-go/instrument/ (equivalent flow tracking), shatter-rust/src/ (deferred)

title: Detect closures over mutable state and mark symbolic links as unknown
description:
When a closure captures a variable that is reassigned after closure creation, the symbolic link between the captured value and the original expression becomes invalid. The instrumentor should detect this and mark the captured reference as unknown at the closure boundary.

Detection strategy: when processing a function literal (arrow function, function expression), collect all free variables referenced inside it. For each, check if the variable is reassigned anywhere in the enclosing scope after the closure's position. If so, the closure observes mutable state — mark the variable as {kind: 'unknown'} in the closure's data flow context. Variables declared with const are always safe.

Language-specific concerns:
- TS: arrow functions and function expressions. const captures are safe. let/var captures need checking.
- Go: func literals capture by reference. All non-const captured vars are potentially mutable. Go has no const keyword for local vars, so all captured vars that are assigned to after the closure's definition are suspect.
- Rust frontend: closures are explicit about capture mode (move vs borrow). Rust's ownership model makes this statically determinable, but the Rust frontend's execute is unimplemented so this is deferred.

Key files: shatter-ts/src/instrumentor.ts, shatter-go/instrument/

title: No pre-commit or pre-push hook enforces cargo build/test before commits
description: The repository has GitHub Actions CI (devcontainer.yml runs cargo test + clippy, release.yml builds binaries), but there are no local git hooks. A partially-complete feature (scope_events) was committed across multiple files without all construction sites updated, creating a broken intermediate state. While this was on a feature branch (not main), the lack of local gates means broken code can be pushed to any branch. Consider adding a pre-push hook or Makefile target that runs 'cargo clippy -p shatter-core -- -D warnings' before allowing pushes, at minimum for main/release branches.
close_reason: Subsumed by str-zwgc (test impact analysis). The pre-commit hook verifying tiered test markers is part of that design.

title: Build-time Z3 verification and codegen for string operation mappings
description:
Replace hand-coded string operation→Z3 mappings with a spec-driven build step:

1. Per-language YAML spec files (~15 lines each) mapping language method names to canonical operations:
   typescript.yaml: includes→contains, indexOf→indexOf, startsWith→prefix, etc.
   go.yaml: strings.Contains→contains, strings.Index→indexOf, strings.HasPrefix→prefix, etc.
   rust.yaml: contains→contains, find→indexOf, starts_with→prefix, etc.

2. Build-time Z3 verifier: for each mapping, assert that the language-level operation and Z3 Seq operation produce identical results for all inputs. check-sat the negation — UNSAT proves correctness. Flag any mapping that can't be verified (encoding edge cases like UTF-16 length vs byte length).

3. Code generator: emit Rust match arms for solver.rs from verified mappings. Generated code is identical to hand-written but proven correct and trivially extensible.

4. Encoding model per language: specify how 'length' works (UTF-16 code units for JS, bytes for Go len(), chars for Go range, bytes for Rust len(), chars for Rust chars().count()). Generator produces the correct Z3 length encoding per language.

Adding a new language = write a YAML spec + run build. Build either succeeds (correct mappings generated) or fails with specific verification errors.

Depends on str-pmp5 (hand-coded mappings first to validate the approach).
close_reason: Implemented YAML-driven build step generating StringOp enum, resolve_string_op(), and sort-grouped constants. Refactored solver.rs to dispatch via enum. Added 2 verification tests. All 961 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Remove redundant test order output from walkthrough
description: The walkthrough prints a long 'Test order: ...' line listing all functions alphabetically before scanning. This is redundant (the scan output already shows each function) and clutters the output. Remove or suppress this line from the scan/explore output.

title: Explore output should show function line span
description: When exploring a function, the output should include how many lines the function body spans (e.g. 'Function body: lines 10-45 (36 lines)'). This provides context for interpreting coverage numbers and helps users understand the scope of what's being analyzed.

title: Cross-language behavioral comparison via input/output spec diff
description:
## Summary

Enable comparing two implementations of the same function across languages (e.g., Go vs TypeScript) to determine behavioral equivalence.

## Motivation

When migrating code between languages, developers need confidence that the new implementation behaves identically. Shatter's spec format is already language-agnostic (serde_json::Value throughout), but spec-diff matches equivalence classes by branch path — which is language-specific (different AST positions).

## Approach: Input/output-only diff

With complete coverage, each function is a table: f(inputs) → output. Two approaches:

**A. Example replay (preferred when both functions are executable):**
- Generate spec for function A, extract all concrete example inputs
- Execute function B with those same inputs via the execute protocol message
- Compare outputs directly
- Output: 'for input (5, "hello"), function A returns 42 but function B returns 43'

**B. Spec table diff (offline, no execution needed):**
- Collect all (inputs, output) pairs from both specs
- Normalize values (sort object keys, canonicalize numbers)
- Set comparison: pairs unique to A, unique to B, same inputs with different outputs

## Why not branch-path matching?

Branch paths encode source positions, so cross-language paths never match. Relative/structural paths (strip positions, keep condition+decision) are fragile due to condition ordering differences between languages and don't reconstruct decision trees reliably.

## Prerequisites
- Both frontends must produce compatible JSON representations for equivalent values
- ComplexKind types (Date, Money, UUID) need canonical JSON format conventions
- Side effects should be excluded from cross-language comparison (only TS captures them)
- Error types need normalization (TS preserves constructor names, Go flattens to runtime_error)

## Scope
- New `shatter compare` CLI command or `--cross-language` flag on spec-diff
- Example replay execution mode
- Offline spec table diff mode
- Canonical value normalization
- Similarity summary metric ('4 of 6 behaviors match — 67% equivalent')

title: HTML report output for scan results
description: Add an HTML report format option for scan output (e.g. --format=html). Currently scan supports JSON and markdown formats. An HTML report would provide a richer, more visual presentation of scan results including behavior maps, coverage metrics, and cluster visualization.
notes: Dependency: comrak crate for MD→HTML. Prerequisite str-3k35 generates the Markdown first.

title: Add realistic multi-branch example functions
description:
Current examples are purpose-built test fixtures. Add more realistic utility functions that exercise complex branching naturally, making the demo more compelling and testing the engine against real-world patterns.

**User-proposed candidates:**
- Roman numeral converter (int→roman and roman→int) — lots of conditional mapping, validation of malformed input
- URL parser — protocol detection, authority/path/query/fragment splitting, edge cases (relative URLs, punycode, missing scheme)
- Email address validator — local-part rules, domain validation, quoted strings, plus-addressing, length limits

**Additional candidates to consider:**
- Semver parser/comparator — major.minor.patch parsing, pre-release tags, range matching (^, ~, >=)
- JSON path evaluator — dot/bracket notation, array indexing, wildcard matching
- Cron expression parser — minute/hour/day/month/weekday fields, ranges, steps, special strings (@daily, @weekly)
- Markdown inline parser — bold, italic, code, links, escaping — many interacting state transitions
- IPv4/IPv6 address classifier — format detection, validation, mapped addresses, CIDR notation
- HTTP content negotiation — Accept header parsing, q-values, media type matching, wildcard handling
- Color format converter — hex/rgb/hsl/named, with clamping, alpha channel, shorthand expansion

**Criteria for good examples:**
1. Recognizable real-world utility (not contrived)
2. Rich branching (10+ distinct paths) without being artificially complex
3. Pure functions with no external dependencies
4. Interesting edge cases that reward thorough exploration
5. Implementable in both TS and Go with comparable branch structure

**Scope:** Pick 3-4 from the combined list. Implement in both examples/typescript/src/ and examples/go/. Add known-answer comments documenting expected branches and triggering inputs per CLAUDE.md conventions.
notes: For the semver parser/comparator: implement the richer version that includes range matching (caret ^, tilde ~, >=, <, wildcard x/*). This pushes the branch count to 15+ distinct paths and exercises more interesting constraint-solving scenarios (numeric comparison, string lexical ordering, array-length comparisons for pre-release precedence). The parse-only version is too simple to be compelling.
close_reason: Added 4 realistic multi-branch example functions (roman numerals, semver, email validator, cron parser) in both TypeScript and Go

title: Graceful stub/mock fallback for unresolvable imports

Mark the function as 'partially analyzed' in results. This is the safety net after project root detection — ensures scan never crashes on missing deps.
